title: Claude Certified Architect — Foundations: Study Guide

units.title: 1.1 Design and implement agentic loops for autonomous task execution

subtopics.label: Agentic loop lifecycle
subtopics.detail: The agentic loop lifecycle: sending requests to Claude, inspecting stop_reason ("tool_use" vs "end_turn"), executing requested tools, and returning results for the next iteration

subtopics.label: Appending tool results to history
subtopics.detail: How tool results are appended to conversation history so the model can reason about the next action

subtopics.label: Model-driven vs scripted control
subtopics.detail: The distinction between model-driven decision-making (Claude reasons about which tool to call next based on context) and pre-configured decision trees or tool sequences

subtopics.label: Loop control via stop_reason
subtopics.detail: Implementing agentic loop control flow that continues when stop_reason is "tool_use" and terminates when stop_reason is "end_turn"

subtopics.label: Tool results between iterations
subtopics.detail: Adding tool results to conversation context between iterations so the model can incorporate new information into its reasoning

subtopics.label: Loop termination anti-patterns
subtopics.detail: Avoiding anti-patterns such as parsing natural language signals to determine loop termination, setting arbitrary iteration caps as the primary stopping mechanism, or checking for assistant text content as a completion indicator

units.enhancementInstructions: Frame for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam: emphasise production tradeoffs and architectural judgment; include one realistic scenario and 2–3 exam-style multiple-choice questions with explained answers.

units.title: 1.2 Orchestrate multi-agent systems with coordinator-subagent patterns

subtopics.label: Hub-and-spoke coordinator architecture
subtopics.detail: Hub-and-spoke architecture where a coordinator agent manages all inter-subagent communication, error handling, and information routing

subtopics.label: Subagent isolated context
subtopics.detail: How subagents operate with isolated context—they do not inherit the coordinator's conversation history automatically

subtopics.label: Coordinator decomposition and delegation
subtopics.detail: The role of the coordinator in task decomposition, delegation, result aggregation, and deciding which subagents to invoke based on query complexity

subtopics.label: Narrow decomposition coverage risks
subtopics.detail: Risks of overly narrow task decomposition by the coordinator, leading to incomplete coverage of broad research topics

subtopics.label: Dynamic subagent selection
subtopics.detail: Designing coordinator agents that analyze query requirements and dynamically select which subagents to invoke rather than always routing through the full pipeline

subtopics.label: Partitioning scope to reduce duplication
subtopics.detail: Partitioning research scope across subagents to minimize duplication (e.g., assigning distinct subtopics or source types to each agent)

subtopics.label: Iterative refinement loops
subtopics.detail: Implementing iterative refinement loops where the coordinator evaluates synthesis output for gaps, re-delegates to search and analysis subagents with targeted queries, and re-invokes synthesis until coverage is sufficient

subtopics.label: Routing communication through coordinator
subtopics.detail: Routing all subagent communication through the coordinator for observability, consistent error handling, and controlled information flow

units.enhancementInstructions: Frame for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam: emphasise production tradeoffs and architectural judgment; include one realistic scenario and 2–3 exam-style multiple-choice questions with explained answers.

units.title: 1.3 Configure subagent invocation, context passing, and spawning

subtopics.label: Task tool for spawning
subtopics.detail: The Task tool as the mechanism for spawning subagents, and the requirement that allowedTools must include "Task" for a coordinator to invoke subagents

subtopics.label: Explicit subagent context passing
subtopics.detail: That subagent context must be explicitly provided in the prompt—subagents do not automatically inherit parent context or share memory between invocations

subtopics.label: AgentDefinition configuration
subtopics.detail: The AgentDefinition configuration including descriptions, system prompts, and tool restrictions for each subagent type

subtopics.label: Fork-based session management
subtopics.detail: Fork-based session management for exploring divergent approaches from a shared analysis baseline

subtopics.label: Including prior findings in prompts
subtopics.detail: Including complete findings from prior agents directly in the subagent's prompt (e.g., passing web search results and document analysis outputs to the synthesis subagent)

subtopics.label: Structured formats preserve attribution
subtopics.detail: Using structured data formats to separate content from metadata (source URLs, document names, page numbers) when passing context between agents to preserve attribution

subtopics.label: Spawning parallel subagents
subtopics.detail: Spawning parallel subagents by emitting multiple Task tool calls in a single coordinator response rather than across separate turns

subtopics.label: Goal-based coordinator prompts
subtopics.detail: Designing coordinator prompts that specify research goals and quality criteria rather than step-by-step procedural instructions, to enable subagent adaptability

units.enhancementInstructions: Frame for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam: emphasise production tradeoffs and architectural judgment; include one realistic scenario and 2–3 exam-style multiple-choice questions with explained answers.

units.title: 1.4 Implement multi-step workflows with enforcement and handoff patterns

subtopics.label: Programmatic enforcement vs prompts
subtopics.detail: The difference between programmatic enforcement (hooks, prerequisite gates) and prompt-based guidance for workflow ordering

subtopics.label: Prompt failure rate limits
subtopics.detail: When deterministic compliance is required (e.g., identity verification before financial operations), prompt instructions alone have a non-zero failure rate

subtopics.label: Structured handoff protocols
subtopics.detail: Structured handoff protocols for mid-process escalation that include customer details, root cause analysis, and recommended actions

subtopics.label: Programmatic prerequisite gates
subtopics.detail: Implementing programmatic prerequisites that block downstream tool calls until prerequisite steps have completed (e.g., blocking process_refund until get_customer has returned a verified customer ID)

subtopics.label: Parallel multi-concern decomposition
subtopics.detail: Decomposing multi-concern customer requests into distinct items, then investigating each in parallel using shared context before synthesizing a unified resolution

subtopics.label: Compiling handoff summaries
subtopics.detail: Compiling structured handoff summaries (customer ID, root cause, refund amount, recommended action) when escalating to human agents who lack access to the conversation transcript

units.enhancementInstructions: Frame for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam: emphasise production tradeoffs and architectural judgment; include one realistic scenario and 2–3 exam-style multiple-choice questions with explained answers.

units.title: 1.5 Apply Agent SDK hooks for tool call interception and data normalization

subtopics.label: PostToolUse result transformation
subtopics.detail: Hook patterns (e.g., PostToolUse) that intercept tool results for transformation before the model processes them

subtopics.label: Intercepting outgoing tool calls
subtopics.detail: Hook patterns that intercept outgoing tool calls to enforce compliance rules (e.g., blocking refunds above a threshold)

subtopics.label: Deterministic vs probabilistic compliance
subtopics.detail: The distinction between using hooks for deterministic guarantees versus relying on prompt instructions for probabilistic compliance

subtopics.label: Normalizing heterogeneous data formats
subtopics.detail: Implementing PostToolUse hooks to normalize heterogeneous data formats (Unix timestamps, ISO 8601, numeric status codes) from different MCP tools before the agent processes them

subtopics.label: Blocking policy-violating actions
subtopics.detail: Implementing tool call interception hooks that block policy-violating actions (e.g., refunds exceeding $500) and redirect to alternative workflows (e.g., human escalation)

subtopics.label: Hooks for guaranteed compliance
subtopics.detail: Choosing hooks over prompt-based enforcement when business rules require guaranteed compliance

units.enhancementInstructions: Frame for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam: emphasise production tradeoffs and architectural judgment; include one realistic scenario and 2–3 exam-style multiple-choice questions with explained answers.

units.title: 1.6 Design task decomposition strategies for complex workflows

subtopics.label: Fixed vs adaptive decomposition
subtopics.detail: When to use fixed sequential pipelines (prompt chaining) versus dynamic adaptive decomposition based on intermediate findings

subtopics.label: Prompt chaining review steps
subtopics.detail: Prompt chaining patterns that break reviews into sequential steps (e.g., analyze each file individually, then run a cross-file integration pass)

subtopics.label: Adaptive investigation plans
subtopics.detail: The value of adaptive investigation plans that generate subtasks based on what is discovered at each step

subtopics.label: Selecting decomposition patterns
subtopics.detail: Selecting task decomposition patterns appropriate to the workflow: prompt chaining for predictable multi-aspect reviews, dynamic decomposition for open-ended investigation tasks

subtopics.label: Per-file plus integration passes
subtopics.detail: Splitting large code reviews into per-file local analysis passes plus a separate cross-file integration pass to avoid attention dilution

subtopics.label: Decomposing open-ended tasks
subtopics.detail: Decomposing open-ended tasks (e.g., "add comprehensive tests to a legacy codebase") by first mapping structure, identifying high-impact areas, then creating a prioritized plan that adapts as dependencies are discovered

units.enhancementInstructions: Frame for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam: emphasise production tradeoffs and architectural judgment; include one realistic scenario and 2–3 exam-style multiple-choice questions with explained answers.

units.title: 1.7 Manage session state, resumption, and forking

subtopics.label: Named session resumption
subtopics.detail: Named session resumption using --resume <session-name> to continue a specific prior conversation

subtopics.label: fork_session branching
subtopics.detail: fork_session for creating independent branches from a shared analysis baseline to explore divergent approaches

subtopics.label: Informing agent of file changes
subtopics.detail: The importance of informing the agent about changes to previously analyzed files when resuming sessions after code modifications

subtopics.label: Fresh summary over stale resume
subtopics.detail: Why starting a new session with a structured summary is more reliable than resuming with stale tool results

subtopics.label: Resuming named investigation sessions
subtopics.detail: Using --resume with session names to continue named investigation sessions across work sessions

subtopics.label: Parallel exploration branches
subtopics.detail: Using fork_session to create parallel exploration branches (e.g., comparing two testing strategies or refactoring approaches from a shared codebase analysis)

subtopics.label: Resume vs fresh start
subtopics.detail: Choosing between session resumption (when prior context is mostly valid) and starting fresh with injected summaries (when prior tool results are stale)

subtopics.label: Targeted resumed re-analysis
subtopics.detail: Informing a resumed session about specific file changes for targeted re-analysis rather than requiring full re-exploration

units.title: 2.1 Design effective tool interfaces with clear descriptions and boundaries

subtopics.label: Descriptions drive tool selection
subtopics.detail: Tool descriptions as the primary mechanism LLMs use for tool selection; minimal descriptions lead to unreliable selection among similar tools

subtopics.label: Rich description content
subtopics.detail: The importance of including input formats, example queries, edge cases, and boundary explanations in tool descriptions

subtopics.label: Overlapping descriptions cause misrouting
subtopics.detail: How ambiguous or overlapping tool descriptions cause misrouting (e.g., analyze_content vs analyze_document with near-identical descriptions)

subtopics.label: System prompt keyword sensitivity
subtopics.detail: The impact of system prompt wording on tool selection: keyword-sensitive instructions can create unintended tool associations

subtopics.label: Differentiating tool descriptions
subtopics.detail: Writing tool descriptions that clearly differentiate each tool's purpose, expected inputs, outputs, and when to use it versus similar alternatives

subtopics.label: Renaming to remove overlap
subtopics.detail: Renaming tools and updating descriptions to eliminate functional overlap (e.g., renaming analyze_content to extract_web_results with a web-specific description)

subtopics.label: Splitting generic tools
subtopics.detail: Splitting generic tools into purpose-specific tools with defined input/output contracts (e.g., splitting a generic analyze_document into extract_data_points, summarize_content, and verify_claim_against_source)

subtopics.label: Auditing keyword-sensitive prompts
subtopics.detail: Reviewing system prompts for keyword-sensitive instructions that might override well-written tool descriptions

units.enhancementInstructions: Frame for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam: emphasise production tradeoffs and architectural judgment; include one realistic scenario and 2–3 exam-style multiple-choice questions with explained answers.

units.title: 2.2 Implement structured error responses for MCP tools

subtopics.label: MCP isError flag
subtopics.detail: The MCP isError flag pattern for communicating tool failures back to the agent

subtopics.label: Error category taxonomy
subtopics.detail: The distinction between transient errors (timeouts, service unavailability), validation errors (invalid input), business errors (policy violations), and permission errors

subtopics.label: Uniform errors block recovery
subtopics.detail: Why uniform error responses (generic "Operation failed") prevent the agent from making appropriate recovery decisions

subtopics.label: Retryable vs non-retryable errors
subtopics.detail: The difference between retryable and non-retryable errors, and how returning structured metadata prevents wasted retry attempts

subtopics.label: Structured error metadata
subtopics.detail: Returning structured error metadata including errorCategory (transient/validation/permission), isRetryable boolean, and human-readable descriptions

subtopics.label: Business-rule violation flags
subtopics.detail: Including retriable: false flags and customer-friendly explanations for business rule violations so the agent can communicate appropriately

subtopics.label: Local recovery and propagation
subtopics.detail: Implementing local error recovery within subagents for transient failures, propagating to the coordinator only errors that cannot be resolved locally along with partial results and what was attempted

subtopics.label: Access failures vs empty results
subtopics.detail: Distinguishing between access failures (needing retry decisions) and valid empty results (representing successful queries with no matches)

units.enhancementInstructions: Frame for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam: emphasise production tradeoffs and architectural judgment; include one realistic scenario and 2–3 exam-style multiple-choice questions with explained answers.

units.title: 2.3 Distribute tools appropriately across agents and configure tool choice

subtopics.label: Too many tools degrades selection
subtopics.detail: The principle that giving an agent access to too many tools (e.g., 18 instead of 4-5) degrades tool selection reliability by increasing decision complexity

subtopics.label: Out-of-specialization tool misuse
subtopics.detail: Why agents with tools outside their specialization tend to misuse them (e.g., a synthesis agent attempting web searches)

subtopics.label: Scoped tool access
subtopics.detail: Scoped tool access: giving agents only the tools needed for their role, with limited cross-role tools for specific high-frequency needs

subtopics.label: tool_choice configuration options
subtopics.detail: tool_choice configuration options: "auto", "any", and forced tool selection ({"type": "tool", "name": "..."})

subtopics.label: Restricting subagent tool sets
subtopics.detail: Restricting each subagent's tool set to those relevant to its role, preventing cross-specialization misuse

subtopics.label: Constrained tool alternatives
subtopics.detail: Replacing generic tools with constrained alternatives (e.g., replacing fetch_url with load_document that validates document URLs)

subtopics.label: Scoped cross-role tools
subtopics.detail: Providing scoped cross-role tools for high-frequency needs (e.g., a verify_fact tool for the synthesis agent) while routing complex cases through the coordinator

subtopics.label: Forced first tool selection
subtopics.detail: Using tool_choice forced selection to ensure a specific tool is called first (e.g., forcing extract_metadata before enrichment tools), then processing subsequent steps in follow-up turns

subtopics.label: tool_choice any setting
subtopics.detail: Setting tool_choice: "any" to guarantee the model calls a tool rather than returning conversational text

units.title: 2.4 Integrate MCP servers into Claude Code and agent workflows

subtopics.label: Project vs user MCP scoping
subtopics.detail: MCP server scoping: project-level (.mcp.json) for shared team tooling vs user-level (~/.claude.json) for personal/experimental servers

subtopics.label: Env variable expansion
subtopics.detail: Environment variable expansion in .mcp.json (e.g., ${GITHUB_TOKEN}) for credential management without committing secrets

subtopics.label: Simultaneous tool discovery
subtopics.detail: That tools from all configured MCP servers are discovered at connection time and available simultaneously to the agent

subtopics.label: MCP resources as catalogs
subtopics.detail: MCP resources as a mechanism for exposing content catalogs (e.g., issue summaries, documentation hierarchies, database schemas) to reduce exploratory tool calls

subtopics.label: Configuring shared MCP servers
subtopics.detail: Configuring shared MCP servers in project-scoped .mcp.json with environment variable expansion for authentication tokens

subtopics.label: Configuring personal MCP servers
subtopics.detail: Configuring personal/experimental MCP servers in user-scoped ~/.claude.json

subtopics.label: Enhancing MCP tool descriptions
subtopics.detail: Enhancing MCP tool descriptions to explain capabilities and outputs in detail, preventing the agent from preferring built-in tools (like Grep) over more capable MCP tools

subtopics.label: Community servers over custom
subtopics.detail: Choosing existing community MCP servers over custom implementations for standard integrations (e.g., Jira), reserving custom servers for team-specific workflows

subtopics.label: Exposing catalogs as resources
subtopics.detail: Exposing content catalogs as MCP resources to give agents visibility into available data without requiring exploratory tool calls

units.title: 2.5 Select and apply built-in tools (Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob) effectively

subtopics.label: Grep for content search
subtopics.detail: Grep for content search (searching file contents for patterns like function names, error messages, or import statements)

subtopics.label: Glob for path matching
subtopics.detail: Glob for file path pattern matching (finding files by name or extension patterns)

subtopics.label: Read, Write, and Edit
subtopics.detail: Read/Write for full file operations; Edit for targeted modifications using unique text matching

subtopics.label: Read plus Write fallback
subtopics.detail: When Edit fails due to non-unique text matches, using Read + Write as a fallback for reliable file modifications

subtopics.label: Selecting Grep for code search
subtopics.detail: Selecting Grep for searching code content across a codebase (e.g., finding all callers of a function, locating error messages)

subtopics.label: Selecting Glob for patterns
subtopics.detail: Selecting Glob for finding files matching naming patterns (e.g., **/*.test.tsx)

subtopics.label: Read then Write workaround
subtopics.detail: Using Read to load full file contents followed by Write when Edit cannot find unique anchor text

subtopics.label: Incremental codebase understanding
subtopics.detail: Building codebase understanding incrementally: starting with Grep to find entry points, then using Read to follow imports and trace flows, rather than reading all files upfront

subtopics.label: Tracing usage across wrappers
subtopics.detail: Tracing function usage across wrapper modules by first identifying all exported names, then searching for each name across the codebase

units.title: 3.1 Configure CLAUDE.md files with appropriate hierarchy, scoping, and modular organization

subtopics.label: CLAUDE.md configuration hierarchy
subtopics.detail: The CLAUDE.md configuration hierarchy: user-level (~/.claude/CLAUDE.md), project-level (.claude/CLAUDE.md or root CLAUDE.md), and directory-level (subdirectory CLAUDE.md files)

subtopics.label: User-level settings not shared
subtopics.detail: That user-level settings apply only to that user—instructions in ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md are not shared with teammates via version control

subtopics.label: @import for modularity
subtopics.detail: The @import syntax for referencing external files to keep CLAUDE.md modular (e.g., importing specific standards files relevant to each package)

subtopics.label: .claude/rules directory
subtopics.detail: .claude/rules/ directory for organizing topic-specific rule files as an alternative to a monolithic CLAUDE.md

subtopics.label: Diagnosing hierarchy issues
subtopics.detail: Diagnosing configuration hierarchy issues (e.g., a new team member not receiving instructions because they're in user-level rather than project-level configuration)

subtopics.label: Selective @import per package
subtopics.detail: Using @import to selectively include relevant standards files in each package's CLAUDE.md based on maintainer domain knowledge

subtopics.label: Splitting into topic files
subtopics.detail: Splitting large CLAUDE.md files into focused topic-specific files in .claude/rules/ (e.g., testing.md, api-conventions.md, deployment.md)

subtopics.label: /memory command diagnostics
subtopics.detail: Using the /memory command to verify which memory files are loaded and diagnose inconsistent behavior across sessions

units.title: 3.2 Create and configure custom slash commands and skills

subtopics.label: Project vs user commands
subtopics.detail: Project-scoped commands in .claude/commands/ (shared via version control) vs user-scoped commands in ~/.claude/commands/ (personal)

subtopics.label: Skills and SKILL.md frontmatter
subtopics.detail: Skills in .claude/skills/ with SKILL.md files that support frontmatter configuration including context: fork, allowed-tools, and argument-hint

subtopics.label: context fork isolation
subtopics.detail: The context: fork frontmatter option for running skills in an isolated sub-agent context, preventing skill outputs from polluting the main conversation

subtopics.label: Personal skill variants
subtopics.detail: Personal skill customization: creating personal variants in ~/.claude/skills/ with different names to avoid affecting teammates

subtopics.label: Creating project slash commands
subtopics.detail: Creating project-scoped slash commands in .claude/commands/ for team-wide availability via version control

subtopics.label: Isolating verbose skills
subtopics.detail: Using context: fork to isolate skills that produce verbose output (e.g., codebase analysis) or exploratory context (e.g., brainstorming alternatives) from the main session

subtopics.label: Restricting skill allowed-tools
subtopics.detail: Configuring allowed-tools in skill frontmatter to restrict tool access during skill execution (e.g., limiting to file write operations to prevent destructive actions)

subtopics.label: argument-hint for parameters
subtopics.detail: Using argument-hint frontmatter to prompt developers for required parameters when they invoke the skill without arguments

subtopics.label: Skills vs CLAUDE.md
subtopics.detail: Choosing between skills (on-demand invocation for task-specific workflows) and CLAUDE.md (always-loaded universal standards)

units.title: 3.3 Apply path-specific rules for conditional convention loading

subtopics.label: Path glob frontmatter rules
subtopics.detail: .claude/rules/ files with YAML frontmatter paths fields containing glob patterns for conditional rule activation

subtopics.label: Path-scoped rule loading
subtopics.detail: How path-scoped rules load only when editing matching files, reducing irrelevant context and token usage

subtopics.label: Glob rules over directory files
subtopics.detail: The advantage of glob-pattern rules over directory-level CLAUDE.md files for conventions that span multiple directories (e.g., test files spread throughout a codebase)

subtopics.label: Creating path-scoped rules
subtopics.detail: Creating .claude/rules/ files with YAML frontmatter path scoping (e.g., paths: ["terraform/**/*"]) so rules load only when editing matching files

subtopics.label: Glob patterns by file type
subtopics.detail: Using glob patterns in path-specific rules to apply conventions to files by type regardless of directory location (e.g., **/*.test.tsx for all test files)

subtopics.label: Path rules over subdirectories
subtopics.detail: Choosing path-specific rules over subdirectory CLAUDE.md files when conventions must apply to files spread across the codebase

units.title: 3.4 Determine when to use plan mode vs direct execution

subtopics.label: Plan mode use cases
subtopics.detail: Plan mode is designed for complex tasks involving large-scale changes, multiple valid approaches, architectural decisions, and multi-file modifications

subtopics.label: Direct execution use cases
subtopics.detail: Direct execution is appropriate for simple, well-scoped changes (e.g., adding a single validation check to one function)

subtopics.label: Safe exploration before changes
subtopics.detail: Plan mode enables safe codebase exploration and design before committing to changes, preventing costly rework

subtopics.label: Explore subagent for discovery
subtopics.detail: The Explore subagent for isolating verbose discovery output and returning summaries to preserve main conversation context

subtopics.label: Plan mode for architecture
subtopics.detail: Selecting plan mode for tasks with architectural implications (e.g., microservice restructuring, library migrations affecting 45+ files, choosing between integration approaches with different infrastructure requirements)

subtopics.label: Direct execution for clear scope
subtopics.detail: Selecting direct execution for well-understood changes with clear scope (e.g., a single-file bug fix with a clear stack trace, adding a date validation conditional)

subtopics.label: Explore subagent context preservation
subtopics.detail: Using the Explore subagent for verbose discovery phases to prevent context window exhaustion during multi-phase tasks

subtopics.label: Combining plan and execution
subtopics.detail: Combining plan mode for investigation with direct execution for implementation (e.g., planning a library migration, then executing the planned approach)

units.title: 3.5 Apply iterative refinement techniques for progressive improvement

subtopics.label: Concrete input/output examples
subtopics.detail: Concrete input/output examples as the most effective way to communicate expected transformations when prose descriptions are interpreted inconsistently

subtopics.label: Test-driven iteration
subtopics.detail: Test-driven iteration: writing test suites first, then iterating by sharing test failures to guide progressive improvement

subtopics.label: The interview pattern
subtopics.detail: The interview pattern: having Claude ask questions to surface considerations the developer may not have anticipated before implementing

subtopics.label: Single message vs sequential fixes
subtopics.detail: When to provide all issues in a single message (interacting problems) versus fixing them sequentially (independent problems)

subtopics.label: Examples clarify transformations
subtopics.detail: Providing 2-3 concrete input/output examples to clarify transformation requirements when natural language descriptions produce inconsistent results

subtopics.label: Tests before implementation
subtopics.detail: Writing test suites covering expected behavior, edge cases, and performance requirements before implementation, then iterating by sharing test failures

subtopics.label: Interview pattern for design
subtopics.detail: Using the interview pattern to surface design considerations (e.g., cache invalidation strategies, failure modes) before implementing solutions in unfamiliar domains

subtopics.label: Test cases for edge handling
subtopics.detail: Providing specific test cases with example input and expected output to fix edge case handling (e.g., null values in migration scripts)

subtopics.label: Bundling interacting fixes
subtopics.detail: Addressing multiple interacting issues in a single detailed message when fixes interact, versus sequential iteration for independent issues

units.title: 3.6 Integrate Claude Code into CI/CD pipelines

subtopics.detail: The -p (or --print) flag for running Claude Code in non-interactive mode in automated pipelines

subtopics.detail: output-format json and --json-schema CLI flags for enforcing structured output in CI contexts

subtopics.label: CLAUDE.md as CI context

subtopics.detail: Session context isolation: why the same Claude session that generated code is less effective at reviewing its own changes compared to an independent review instance

subtopics.detail: Running Claude Code in CI with the -p flag to prevent interactive input hangs

subtopics.label: Machine-parseable findings
subtopics.detail: Using --output-format json with --json-schema to produce machine-parseable structured findings for automated posting as inline PR comments

subtopics.label: Incremental review deduplication
subtopics.detail: Including prior review findings in context when re-running reviews after new commits, instructing Claude to report only new or still-unaddressed issues to avoid duplicate comments

subtopics.detail: Providing existing test files in context so test generation avoids suggesting duplicate scenarios already covered by the test suite

subtopics.detail: Documenting testing standards, valuable test criteria, and available fixtures in CLAUDE.md to improve test generation quality and reduce low-value test output

units.title: 4.1 Design prompts with explicit criteria to improve precision and reduce false positives

subtopics.label: Explicit criteria over vagueness
subtopics.detail: The importance of explicit criteria over vague instructions (e.g., "flag comments only when claimed behavior contradicts actual code behavior" vs "check that comments are accurate")

subtopics.label: General instructions fail precision
subtopics.detail: How general instructions like "be conservative" or "only report high-confidence findings" fail to improve precision compared to specific categorical criteria

subtopics.label: False positives erode trust
subtopics.detail: The impact of false positive rates on developer trust: high false positive categories undermine confidence in accurate categories

subtopics.label: Specific report criteria
subtopics.detail: Writing specific review criteria that define which issues to report (bugs, security) versus skip (minor style, local patterns) rather than relying on confidence-based filtering

subtopics.label: Disabling false-positive categories
subtopics.detail: Temporarily disabling high false-positive categories to restore developer trust while improving prompts for those categories

subtopics.label: Severity criteria with examples
subtopics.detail: Defining explicit severity criteria with concrete code examples for each severity level to achieve consistent classification

units.title: 4.3 Enforce structured output using tool use and JSON schemas

subtopics.label: Tool use for schema compliance
subtopics.detail: Tool use (tool_use) with JSON schemas as the most reliable approach for guaranteed schema-compliant structured output, eliminating JSON syntax errors

subtopics.label: tool_choice mode distinctions
subtopics.detail: The distinction between tool_choice: "auto" (model may return text instead of calling a tool), "any" (model must call a tool but can choose which), and forced tool selection (model must call a specific named tool)

subtopics.label: Schemas miss semantic errors
subtopics.detail: That strict JSON schemas via tool use eliminate syntax errors but do not prevent semantic errors (e.g., line items that don't sum to total, values in wrong fields)

subtopics.label: Schema design considerations
subtopics.detail: Schema design considerations: required vs optional fields, enum fields with "other" + detail string patterns for extensible categories

subtopics.label: Defining extraction tools
subtopics.detail: Defining extraction tools with JSON schemas as input parameters and extracting structured data from the tool_use response

subtopics.label: tool_choice any for output
subtopics.detail: Setting tool_choice: "any" to guarantee structured output when multiple extraction schemas exist and the document type is unknown

subtopics.label: Forcing a specific tool
subtopics.detail: Forcing a specific tool with tool_choice: {"type": "tool", "name": "extract_metadata"} to ensure a particular extraction runs before enrichment steps

subtopics.label: Optional fields prevent fabrication
subtopics.detail: Designing schema fields as optional (nullable) when source documents may not contain the information, preventing the model from fabricating values to satisfy required fields

subtopics.label: Enum values for ambiguity
subtopics.detail: Adding enum values like "unclear" for ambiguous cases and "other" + detail fields for extensible categorization

subtopics.label: Normalization rules in prompts
subtopics.detail: Including format normalization rules in prompts alongside strict output schemas to handle inconsistent source formatting

ts-1-1.title: 1.1 Design and implement agentic loops for autonomous task execution

lesson.topic: 1.1 Design and implement agentic loops for autonomous task execution
lesson.synopsis: An agentic loop is the core execution primitive that enables Claude to autonomously plan, act, and reason across multiple turns until a task is complete. This lesson dissects every phase of the loop lifecycle — from the initial API request through tool execution and result injection — and contrasts model-driven decision-making with scripted control flows. Special attention is given to production-grade loop termination logic, common anti-patterns that cause runaway or prematurely-halted agents, and the architectural tradeoffs that distinguish robust systems from fragile ones.
learning_objectives: Explain the full agentic loop lifecycle, including how stop_reason drives iteration versus termination decisions.
Implement correct conversation history management by appending tool results in the format Claude expects between iterations.
Distinguish model-driven control from scripted decision trees and articulate when each is appropriate in production systems.
Identify and eliminate loop termination anti-patterns such as natural-language signal parsing, arbitrary iteration caps, and text-content completion checks.
Evaluate architectural tradeoffs in agentic loop design for reliability, cost, and safety in production deployments.

sections.heading: The Agentic Loop Lifecycle
sections.body_markdown:
An agentic loop is a structured request-execute-observe cycle that repeats until Claude signals it has finished. Understanding each phase precisely is the foundation for building reliable autonomous agents.

The lifecycle has four repeating phases:

1. **Send** — Your orchestrator sends a `messages` array plus a `tools` definition to the Claude API.
2. **Inspect** — You examine the response's `stop_reason` field.
3. **Execute** — If `stop_reason == "tool_use"`, you run every tool Claude requested and collect results.
4. **Return** — You append both Claude's assistant turn and the tool results to the conversation history, then go back to step 1.

When `stop_reason == "end_turn"`, Claude has decided the task is complete and the loop exits.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A(["Orchestrator sends request"]):::process --> B["Claude API call"]:::process
    B --> C{"stop_reason?"}:::decision
    C -->|"tool_use"| D["Extract tool_use blocks"]:::process
    D --> E["Execute each tool"]:::process
    E --> F["Append assistant + tool_result to history"]:::process
    F --> B
    C -->|"end_turn"| G(["Task complete — return final answer"]):::success
    C -->|"max_tokens"| H(["Handle truncation / resume"]):::warn
```

### The `stop_reason` field is the authoritative signal

Claude's response object always contains a `stop_reason`. The values you must handle in an agentic loop are:

| `stop_reason` | Meaning | Loop action |
|:-------------|:--------|:------------|
| `tool_use` | Claude wants to call one or more tools | Execute tools, append results, continue |
| `end_turn` | Claude has finished reasoning | Exit loop, surface final response |
| `max_tokens` | Response was cut off by token limit | Decide: resume, summarise, or abort |
| `stop_sequence` | A custom stop sequence was hit | Application-specific handling |

### Minimal lifecycle in Python

```python
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def run_agentic_loop(initial_messages: list, tools: list) -> str:
    messages = list(initial_messages)  # mutable copy

    while True:
        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=4096,
            tools=tools,
            messages=messages,
        )

        # Phase 2: inspect
        if response.stop_reason == "end_turn":
            # Extract the final text block
            return next(
                block.text for block in response.content
                if block.type == "text"
            )

        if response.stop_reason == "tool_use":
            # Phase 3 & 4 handled in the next section
            messages = execute_and_append(messages, response)
            continue

        # Unexpected stop_reason — fail loudly
        raise RuntimeError(f"Unhandled stop_reason: {response.stop_reason}")
```

> The loop is not a polling mechanism — it is a deterministic state machine driven by Claude's own output. Every iteration is a new API call with a richer history.

### Edge case: multiple tool calls in one turn

Claude may request several tools in a single response (parallel tool use). Your executor must handle **all** `tool_use` blocks before returning to the model — returning only a subset leaves the conversation in an inconsistent state and will cause Claude to re-request the missing results or hallucinate them.

sections.heading: Appending Tool Results to Conversation History
sections.body_markdown:
Correct history management is the most error-prone part of agentic loop implementation. Claude reasons about its next action by reading the full conversation history, so malformed or incomplete history directly degrades output quality.

### The required message structure

After Claude returns a `tool_use` stop, you must append **two** items to the `messages` list:

1. The **assistant message** exactly as returned (containing the `tool_use` content blocks).
2. A **user message** containing one `tool_result` block per tool that was called.

```python
def execute_and_append(messages: list, response) -> list:
    # 1. Append the assistant turn verbatim
    messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": response.content})

    # 2. Collect results for every tool_use block
    tool_results = []
    for block in response.content:
        if block.type == "tool_use":
            result = dispatch_tool(block.name, block.input)  # your executor
            tool_results.append({
                "type": "tool_result",
                "tool_use_id": block.id,   # MUST match the block's id
                "content": str(result),
            })

    # 3. Append all results as a single user turn
    messages.append({"role": "user", "content": tool_results})
    return messages
```

### Why `tool_use_id` matters

Each `tool_use` block carries a unique `id` (e.g., `toolu_01XjY…`). The corresponding `tool_result` must reference this exact id. Claude uses it to correlate results with requests — a mismatch causes the model to treat the result as orphaned and may trigger a re-request or a reasoning error.

### Representing tool errors

When a tool fails, do **not** omit the `tool_result` block. Instead, set `is_error: true` and include a descriptive message. This allows Claude to reason about the failure and decide whether to retry, use a fallback, or report the problem.

```python
tool_results.append({
    "type": "tool_result",
    "tool_use_id": block.id,
    "content": "FileNotFoundError: /data/report.csv does not exist",
    "is_error": True,
})
```

### History growth and context window management

Each iteration adds tokens. For long-running tasks, the conversation history can approach Claude's context window limit ($n_{\text{ctx}}$). Production systems must monitor token usage:

$$\text{tokens\_used} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} (\text{tokens}(\text{turn}_i))\ \leq\ n_{\text{ctx}} - \text{max\_tokens\_reserved}$$

Strategies when approaching the limit:
- **Summarise** earlier turns into a compressed system prompt segment.
- **Truncate** the oldest tool results (keep tool calls for coherence).
- **Checkpoint** the task state externally and restart with a condensed history.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["New tool result"]:::process --> B{"tokens_used > threshold?"}:::decision
    B -->|"no"| C["Append verbatim"]:::success
    B -->|"yes"| D["Summarise old turns"]:::process
    D --> E["Replace history segment"]:::process
    E --> C
```

### Common mistakes

| Mistake | Consequence |
|:--------|:------------|
| Appending only the tool result, not the assistant turn | API rejects the message sequence (role ordering violation) |
| Using a fresh `id` instead of the original `tool_use_id` | Claude cannot correlate result; reasoning degrades |
| Omitting a result for one of multiple tool calls | Conversation state is inconsistent; Claude may loop or hallucinate |
| Serialising complex objects without truncation | Token bloat; context window exhaustion |

sections.heading: Model-Driven vs Scripted Control
sections.body_markdown:
The central architectural question in agentic system design is: **who decides what happens next — the model or your code?** The answer determines flexibility, debuggability, and failure modes.

### Model-driven control

In a model-driven loop, Claude inspects the current state (conversation history, tool results) and autonomously selects the next tool to call, the arguments to pass, and when to stop. Your orchestrator is a thin executor — it runs whatever Claude requests without imposing a predetermined sequence.

```python
# Model-driven: orchestrator is a pure executor
while response.stop_reason == "tool_use":
    messages = execute_and_append(messages, response)
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        tools=ALL_AVAILABLE_TOOLS,  # full tool palette exposed
        messages=messages,
        max_tokens=4096,
    )
```

**Strengths:** Handles novel situations, adapts to unexpected tool results, requires no explicit decision tree maintenance.

**Risks:** Non-deterministic paths make testing harder; a poorly-prompted model may choose inefficient or unsafe tool sequences.

### Scripted (pre-configured) control

In a scripted loop, your orchestrator enforces a fixed sequence of tool calls. Claude may still generate text, but the *control flow* is determined by your code, not by Claude's reasoning.

```python
# Scripted: orchestrator drives the sequence
for step in ["fetch_data", "validate_schema", "write_report"]:
    result = dispatch_tool(step, build_args(step, context))
    context[step] = result
final_answer = summarise_with_claude(context)
```

**Strengths:** Fully deterministic, easy to test, predictable cost.

**Risks:** Brittle when real-world conditions deviate from the expected path; requires manual updates when the task changes.

### Comparison

| Dimension | Model-driven | Scripted |
|:----------|:------------|:---------|
| Flexibility | High — adapts to novel inputs | Low — fixed sequence |
| Determinism | Low — path varies by context | High — same path every run |
| Debuggability | Harder — must inspect conversation | Easier — code is the spec |
| Maintenance | Low — prompt updates suffice | High — code changes needed |
| Safety surface | Larger — model can call any tool | Smaller — only listed tools |
| Best for | Open-ended research, multi-step reasoning | ETL pipelines, compliance workflows |

### Hybrid architectures

Production systems often combine both: a scripted outer loop enforces high-level phases (gather → analyse → report), while each phase uses a model-driven inner loop for flexible sub-task execution. This gives you determinism at the macro level and adaptability at the micro level.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A(["Task start"]):::concept --> B["Phase 1: Gather"]:::process
    B --> C["Inner agentic loop"]:::process
    C --> D["Phase 2: Analyse"]:::process
    D --> E["Inner agentic loop"]:::process
    E --> F["Phase 3: Report"]:::process
    F --> G(["Done"]):::success
    C -->|"error / timeout"| H(["Escalate"]):::warn
    E -->|"error / timeout"| H
```

### Architectural judgment: when to choose which

Choose **model-driven** when: the task space is open-ended, the set of required tools is not known in advance, or the value of the system comes from Claude's reasoning ability.

Choose **scripted** when: regulatory compliance requires an auditable, reproducible trace; the tool sequence is stable and well-understood; or cost predictability is a hard constraint.

Choose **hybrid** when: you need macro-level auditability but micro-level adaptability — the most common production pattern.

sections.heading: Loop Control via stop_reason
sections.body_markdown:
The `stop_reason` field is the **only authoritative signal** for loop control. Building your loop around it — and nothing else — is the single most important correctness principle in agentic loop design.

### The canonical control structure

```python
def run_loop(messages, tools, model="claude-opus-4-5", max_tokens=4096):
    while True:
        response = client.messages.create(
            model=model,
            max_tokens=max_tokens,
            tools=tools,
            messages=messages,
        )

        match response.stop_reason:
            case "end_turn":
                return extract_final_text(response)  # clean exit

            case "tool_use":
                messages = execute_and_append(messages, response)
                # loop continues

            case "max_tokens":
                # The response was truncated — handle explicitly
                raise TokenBudgetExceeded(
                    f"Used {response.usage.output_tokens} output tokens; "
                    "increase max_tokens or summarise history."
                )

            case _:
                raise RuntimeError(
                    f"Unexpected stop_reason: {response.stop_reason!r}"
                )
```

### Why `end_turn` is the correct exit signal

Claude emits `end_turn` when it has completed its reasoning and has no further tool calls to make. This is a **semantic** signal — it reflects the model's assessment of task completion, not a syntactic pattern in the output text. Relying on it means your loop exits at exactly the right moment regardless of what Claude writes.

### Handling `max_tokens` gracefully

A `max_tokens` stop is not a completion — it is a truncation. The response may be mid-sentence or mid-reasoning. Three production strategies:

| Strategy | When to use | Tradeoff |
|:---------|:------------|:---------|
| Increase `max_tokens` | Output is legitimately long | Higher cost per call |
| Summarise and continue | Long-running tasks | Adds latency; may lose detail |
| Abort and report | Safety-critical tasks | Loses work; safe |

### Streaming and stop_reason

When using the streaming API, `stop_reason` arrives in the `message_delta` event at the end of the stream. Your loop must buffer the full stream before making a control-flow decision — do not attempt to infer completion from partial content.

```python
with client.messages.stream(
    model="claude-opus-4-5",
    max_tokens=4096,
    tools=tools,
    messages=messages,
) as stream:
    final_message = stream.get_final_message()  # blocks until stream ends

# Now safe to inspect stop_reason
if final_message.stop_reason == "tool_use":
    ...
```

### The animated loop

```svg
<svg viewBox="0 0 320 160" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Agentic loop lifecycle animation">
  <!-- Background -->
  <rect width="320" height="160" fill="#0f172a" rx="10"/>

  <!-- Nodes -->
  <rect x="10" y="65" width="70" height="30" rx="6" fill="#6366f1"/>
  <text x="45" y="84" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#fff" font-family="monospace">Send Request</text>

  <rect x="125" y="65" width="70" height="30" rx="6" fill="#334155"/>
  <text x="160" y="84" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#e2e8f0" font-family="monospace">Claude API</text>

  <rect x="240" y="65" width="70" height="30" rx="6" fill="#f59e0b"/>
  <text x="275" y="84" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#1e293b" font-family="monospace">stop_reason?</text>

  <!-- end_turn label -->
  <text x="275" y="130" text-anchor="middle" font-size="8" fill="#22c55e" font-family="monospace">end_turn → done</text>
  <line x1="275" y1="95" x2="275" y2="122" stroke="#22c55e" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="3 2"/>

  <!-- tool_use arc back -->
  <path d="M 240 80 Q 160 20 80 80" fill="none" stroke="#6366f1" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="4 3"/>
  <text x="160" y="18" text-anchor="middle" font-size="8" fill="#6366f1" font-family="monospace">tool_use → execute & append</text>

  <!-- Arrows -->
  <line x1="80" y1="80" x2="123" y2="80" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <polygon points="123,76 131,80 123,84" fill="#94a3b8"/>
  <line x1="195" y1="80" x2="238" y2="80" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <polygon points="238,76 246,80 238,84" fill="#94a3b8"/>

  <!-- Animated token travelling the loop -->
  <circle r="6" fill="#22c55e" opacity="0.9">
    <animateMotion dur="4s" repeatCount="indefinite"
      path="M 45 80 L 125 80 L 205 80 L 240 80 Q 160 20 80 80"/>
  </circle>
</svg>
```

sections.heading: Tool Results Between Iterations
sections.body_markdown:
Each tool result injected into the conversation history is a new piece of evidence that Claude incorporates into its next reasoning step. Understanding how this information flows — and how to structure it for maximum utility — is critical for building agents that reason correctly.

### Information flow across iterations

Consider a three-iteration task: fetch a file, parse its contents, write a summary.

```
Iteration 1:
  Claude → tool_use: read_file(path="/data/q3.csv")
  Executor → tool_result: "date,revenue\n2024-07-01,142000\n..."

Iteration 2 (Claude sees the CSV data):
  Claude → tool_use: calculate_total(column="revenue")
  Executor → tool_result: "total_revenue: 1284000"

Iteration 3 (Claude sees both results):
  Claude → end_turn: "Q3 total revenue was USD 1,284,000."
```

At iteration 3, Claude's context contains the full chain of evidence. Its final answer is grounded in real data, not hallucinated.

### Structuring tool results for reasoning quality

The content of a `tool_result` is plain text (or a list of content blocks). How you format it affects Claude's ability to reason:

| Format | Good for | Avoid when |
|:-------|:---------|:-----------|
| JSON string | Structured data Claude will parse | Very large payloads (token cost) |
| Plain prose | Error messages, status updates | Numeric data (ambiguous) |
| Truncated + summary | Large files, API responses | When exact values are needed |
| Markdown table | Tabular data Claude will reason over | Deeply nested structures |

### Passing large results

When a tool returns a large payload (e.g., a 50,000-token API response), injecting it verbatim will exhaust the context window. The recommended pattern is **tool-side summarisation**: your executor truncates or summarises the result before appending it, and stores the full payload externally.

```python
MAX_RESULT_TOKENS = 2000

def safe_tool_result(raw: str, tool_use_id: str) -> dict:
    tokens = count_tokens(raw)  # your token counter
    if tokens > MAX_RESULT_TOKENS:
        truncated = truncate_to_tokens(raw, MAX_RESULT_TOKENS)
        content = f"[Truncated to {MAX_RESULT_TOKENS} tokens]\n{truncated}"
    else:
        content = raw
    return {
        "type": "tool_result",
        "tool_use_id": tool_use_id,
        "content": content,
    }
```

### Stateful context across iterations

Some tasks require state that is too large to fit in tool results (e.g., a growing dataset). The pattern is to store state externally and pass only a **reference or summary** in the tool result:

```python
# Tool: store_dataframe
def store_dataframe(df, tool_use_id):
    key = f"df_{uuid4().hex[:8]}"
    external_store[key] = df          # Redis, S3, etc.
    return {
        "type": "tool_result",
        "tool_use_id": tool_use_id,
        "content": f"DataFrame stored as '{key}'. Shape: {df.shape}. "
                   f"Columns: {list(df.columns)}. "
                   f"Use key '{key}' in subsequent tool calls.",
    }
```

Claude will carry the key forward in its reasoning and pass it to the next tool that needs the data.

### Iteration budget and cost

Each iteration incurs an API call. If $c$ is the cost per token and $t_i$ is the token count at iteration $i$, the total cost of an $n$-iteration loop is:

$$C_{\text{total}} = c \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i$$

Because history grows with each iteration, $t_i$ is strictly increasing. For a loop with $n$ iterations and average history growth $\Delta t$ per iteration:

$$C_{\text{total}} \approx c \cdot n \cdot \left(t_1 + \frac{(n-1)\,\Delta t}{2}\right)$$

This quadratic growth in cost is a key reason to keep tool results concise and to summarise history aggressively in long-running agents.

sections.heading: Loop Termination Anti-Patterns
sections.body_markdown:
Incorrect loop termination logic is the most common source of production incidents in agentic systems — causing either runaway loops that exhaust budgets or premature exits that return incomplete results. This section catalogues the canonical anti-patterns and their correct replacements.

### Anti-pattern 1: Parsing natural language signals

**What it looks like:**
```python
# WRONG — do not do this
for block in response.content:
    if block.type == "text" and "task complete" in block.text.lower():
        break  # fragile!
```

**Why it fails:** Claude's phrasing varies. "I have finished", "The task is done", "Here is your answer" — all mean the same thing but none match a fixed string. Worse, Claude may say "task complete" mid-reasoning before calling another tool, causing a false exit.

**Correct replacement:** Use `stop_reason == "end_turn"`.

---

### Anti-pattern 2: Arbitrary iteration caps as the primary mechanism

**What it looks like:**
```python
# WRONG — iteration cap as the only exit
for _ in range(10):
    response = call_claude(messages)
    messages = execute_and_append(messages, response)
# silently returns whatever the last response was
```

**Why it fails:** The cap is arbitrary. A task that legitimately needs 11 iterations is silently truncated. A task that finishes in 3 iterations wastes 7 calls. There is no signal to the caller that the loop was cut short.

**Correct replacement:** Use `stop_reason` as the primary exit; use an iteration cap as a **safety guard** that raises an explicit exception:

```python
MAX_ITERATIONS = 25  # safety guard, not primary exit

for iteration in range(MAX_ITERATIONS):
    response = call_claude(messages)
    if response.stop_reason == "end_turn":
        return extract_final_text(response)  # primary exit
    if response.stop_reason == "tool_use":
        messages = execute_and_append(messages, response)
    else:
        raise RuntimeError(f"Unexpected stop_reason: {response.stop_reason}")

raise LoopGuardExceeded(
    f"Loop did not terminate after {MAX_ITERATIONS} iterations. "
    "Possible infinite loop — inspect conversation history."
)
```

---

### Anti-pattern 3: Checking for assistant text content as a completion indicator

**What it looks like:**
```python
# WRONG — checking for text blocks as a completion signal
if any(block.type == "text" for block in response.content):
    return response  # assumes text means done
```

**Why it fails:** Claude frequently emits a `text` block alongside a `tool_use` block in the same response (e.g., "I'll look that up for you." followed by a tool call). The presence of text is not a completion signal.

**Correct replacement:** Always check `stop_reason` first; only then extract text blocks.

---

### Anti-pattern 4: Ignoring `max_tokens` as a stop reason

**What it looks like:**
```python
# WRONG — treating max_tokens like end_turn
if response.stop_reason in ("end_turn", "max_tokens"):
    return extract_final_text(response)
```

**Why it fails:** A `max_tokens` response is truncated. The "final text" may be mid-sentence. Returning it as a completed answer silently corrupts the output.

---

### Summary of anti-patterns

| Anti-pattern | Root cause | Failure mode |
|:-------------|:-----------|:-------------|
| NL signal parsing | Treating text as a protocol | False exits, missed completions |
| Iteration cap as primary exit | Distrust of model signals | Silent truncation, wasted calls |
| Text-block presence check | Misunderstanding response structure | Premature exit during tool use |
| Equating `max_tokens` with `end_turn` | Ignoring stop_reason semantics | Corrupted partial output returned |

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Response received"]:::concept --> B{"stop_reason?"}:::decision
    B -->|"end_turn"| C["Extract text → return"]:::success
    B -->|"tool_use"| D["Execute tools → append → loop"]:::process
    B -->|"max_tokens"| E["Handle truncation explicitly"]:::warn
    B -->|"other"| F["Raise exception"]:::warn
    G["❌ Check text blocks for 'done'"]:::warn --> H["False exit risk"]:::warn
    I["❌ NL parsing"]:::warn --> H
    J["❌ Iteration cap only"]:::warn --> K["Silent truncation"]:::warn
```

sections.heading: Realistic Scenario and Exam-Style Questions
sections.body_markdown:
## Realistic Scenario: Automated Financial Report Generator

Your team is building an autonomous agent that generates quarterly financial reports. The agent has access to four tools:

- `query_database(sql)` — runs a SQL query and returns JSON rows
- `calculate_metrics(data, metrics)` — computes financial KPIs
- `fetch_benchmark(ticker, period)` — retrieves market benchmark data
- `write_report(sections)` — writes the final report to a document store

A typical run takes 6–12 iterations. The agent must handle database timeouts (tool errors), partial data (some quarters missing), and must always produce a report even if some data is unavailable.

### Architecture decisions made:

1. **Model-driven inner loop** — Claude decides which queries to run and in what order based on what data it has received so far.
2. **Scripted outer phase** — The orchestrator enforces three phases: `gather → analyse → write`. Each phase runs its own inner agentic loop.
3. **Tool errors are surfaced** — Database timeouts return `is_error: True` with a descriptive message so Claude can decide to retry or proceed with partial data.
4. **Iteration guard** — Each inner loop has a 20-iteration safety cap that raises `LoopGuardExceeded`, which the outer orchestrator catches and escalates.
5. **History summarisation** — After the `gather` phase, the orchestrator summarises the raw query results into a compact JSON structure before starting the `analyse` phase, preventing context window exhaustion.

This design gives auditability at the phase level (scripted), adaptability within each phase (model-driven), and explicit failure handling at every layer.

---

## Exam-Style Questions

### Question 1

An agentic loop implementation exits the loop when it detects the string `"Final answer:"` in Claude's response text. Which of the following best describes the primary risk of this approach?

**A.** It will cause the loop to run indefinitely because Claude never emits that exact string.

**B.** It may exit prematurely if Claude uses that phrase mid-reasoning before issuing a tool call, or may fail to exit if Claude phrases its completion differently.

**C.** It is functionally equivalent to checking `stop_reason == "end_turn"` but less efficient.

**D.** It will always work correctly because Claude is instructed to emit `"Final answer:"` in the system prompt.

**Correct answer: B**

*Explanation:* Natural language parsing for loop termination is an anti-pattern because Claude's phrasing is non-deterministic. Claude may say "Final answer:" as part of an intermediate reasoning step before calling another tool (false exit), or it may complete the task using different phrasing (missed exit). Option A is wrong because Claude does sometimes emit that phrase. Option C is wrong because the two approaches are not equivalent — `stop_reason` is a reliable protocol signal; text parsing is not. Option D is wrong because even with a system prompt instruction, Claude may deviate in phrasing.

---

### Question 2

Claude returns a response with `stop_reason == "tool_use"` containing two `tool_use` blocks: one for `query_database` and one for `fetch_benchmark`. Your executor successfully runs `query_database` but `fetch_benchmark` times out. What is the correct action?

**A.** Append only the successful `tool_result` for `query_database` and continue the loop.

**B.** Abort the loop and return an error to the caller.

**C.** Append both `tool_result` blocks — one with the database result and one with `is_error: True` for the timeout — then continue the loop.

**D.** Retry `fetch_benchmark` up to three times before appending any results.

**Correct answer: C**

*Explanation:* You must return a `tool_result` for **every** `tool_use` block in the response, even if the tool failed. Omitting a result (Option A) leaves the conversation in an inconsistent state — Claude expects a result for each `tool_use_id` it issued. Aborting (Option B) discards potentially recoverable work. Retrying before appending (Option D) may be a valid sub-strategy, but the result of the retry (success or final failure) must still be appended as a `tool_result`. The correct pattern is to surface the error via `is_error: True` so Claude can reason about it and decide the next step.

---

### Question 3

A production agentic loop has been running for 18 iterations on a complex research task. The conversation history is now consuming 180,000 tokens. The model being used has a 200,000-token context window and `max_tokens` is set to 4,096. Which of the following is the most architecturally sound response?

**A.** Increase `max_tokens` to 8,192 to give Claude more room to respond.

**B.** Summarise the oldest tool results in the conversation history into a compact representation and replace them in the `messages` array before the next API call.

**C.** Set a hard stop at 20 iterations regardless of `stop_reason` to prevent context overflow.

**D.** Switch to a model with a larger context window.

**Correct answer: B**

*Explanation:* The problem is input token growth, not output token budget. Increasing `max_tokens` (Option A) addresses output length, not input history size. A hard iteration cap (Option C) is an anti-pattern that silently truncates work and does not address the root cause. Switching models (Option D) may help temporarily but does not solve the fundamental growth problem — the history will eventually exhaust any context window. Summarising old turns (Option B) is the correct architectural response: it reduces input token count while preserving the semantic content Claude needs to continue reasoning, and it scales to arbitrarily long tasks.

key_takeaways: The agentic loop lifecycle has four phases — send, inspect, execute, return — and stop_reason is the only authoritative signal for loop control; build your entire control flow around it.
Every tool_use block in a response must receive a corresponding tool_result in the next user turn, including failed tool calls (use is_error: True); omitting any result corrupts conversation state.

Model-driven loops give Claude full autonomy over tool selection and sequencing; scripted loops enforce a predetermined sequence; hybrid architectures combine macro-level scripting with micro-level model-driven flexibility for most production use cases.

The three canonical loop termination anti-patterns — natural language signal parsing, iteration caps as the primary exit, and text-block presence checks — all share the same root cause: treating unreliable heuristics as protocol signals.

Tool result size must be actively managed: verbatim injection of large payloads causes quadratic token cost growth across iterations; summarise, truncate, or externalise large results before appending them to history.
An iteration cap is a valid safety guard but must raise an explicit exception when triggered — never silently return a partial result as if the task completed normally.
further_reading: Multi-agent orchestration patterns: how agentic loops compose into networks of specialised sub-agents with shared tool registries
Context window management strategies: sliding window summarisation, retrieval-augmented history, and checkpoint-restart patterns for long-horizon tasks
Tool design for agentic systems: idempotency, error taxonomy, and result schema design to maximise Claude's reasoning quality
Safety and human-in-the-loop patterns: interrupt conditions, approval gates, and audit logging for production agentic deployments
Streaming API integration with agentic loops: handling partial tool_use blocks, backpressure, and stop_reason detection in streaming mode

ts-1-2.title: 1.2 Orchestrate multi-agent systems with coordinator-subagent patterns

lesson.topic: 1.2 Orchestrate multi-agent systems with coordinator-subagent patterns
lesson.synopsis: This lesson examines the coordinator-subagent pattern as the primary architectural primitive for production multi-agent systems built on Claude. It covers how a hub-and-spoke coordinator decomposes tasks, delegates to isolated subagents, aggregates results, and enforces observability — while surfacing the real tradeoffs around decomposition granularity, dynamic routing, scope partitioning, and iterative refinement that separate robust systems from brittle ones. The lesson is framed for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam, emphasising architectural judgment and production-grade design decisions.
learning_objectives: Explain the hub-and-spoke coordinator architecture and justify why all inter-subagent communication is routed through the coordinator rather than peer-to-peer.
Identify the risks of overly narrow task decomposition and design decomposition strategies that ensure complete coverage of broad research or analysis tasks.
Design dynamic subagent selection logic that matches query complexity to the minimal sufficient set of subagents, avoiding unnecessary pipeline stages.
Apply scope-partitioning principles to assign distinct subtopics or source types to subagents, minimising redundant work and conflicting outputs.
Implement iterative refinement loops in which the coordinator evaluates synthesis quality, detects coverage gaps, and re-delegates targeted queries until a sufficiency threshold is met.

sections.heading: 1. Hub-and-Spoke Coordinator Architecture
sections.body_markdown:
The coordinator agent is the single point of control in a multi-agent system: every subagent call originates from it, every result returns to it, and no subagent ever communicates directly with another. This hub-and-spoke topology is not merely a stylistic choice — it is an architectural constraint that makes the system observable, debuggable, and safe to operate in production.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  U([User / Caller]):::concept --> C[Coordinator Agent]:::process
  C --> S1[Search Subagent]:::process
  C --> S2[Analysis Subagent]:::process
  C --> S3[Synthesis Subagent]:::process
  S1 -->|results| C
  S2 -->|results| C
  S3 -->|draft| C
  C --> U
```

**Why hub-and-spoke?**

In a peer-to-peer topology, subagent A could call subagent B directly. This creates hidden information flows that are impossible to log centrally, error-handling becomes fragmented (who retries a failed B call — A or the coordinator?), and the system's behaviour becomes emergent rather than designed. Hub-and-spoke eliminates these failure modes:

| Property | Hub-and-Spoke | Peer-to-Peer |
|:---------|:-------------:|:------------:|
| Centralised logging | ✅ | ❌ |
| Uniform error handling | ✅ | ❌ |
| Controlled information flow | ✅ | ❌ |
| Latency (sequential) | Higher | Lower |
| Complexity of coordinator | Higher | Distributed |

The latency cost is real: every subagent result must pass through the coordinator before the next subagent can be invoked (unless the coordinator explicitly fans out in parallel). Architects must decide which subagent calls are independent and can be parallelised versus which are sequentially dependent.

**Production implication:** The coordinator is the blast radius boundary. If a subagent returns malformed output, the coordinator catches it before it contaminates downstream subagents. This is the same principle as a circuit breaker in microservices — the coordinator is the circuit breaker for the agent mesh.

sections.heading: 2. Subagent Isolated Context
sections.body_markdown:
Subagents do not inherit the coordinator's conversation history. Each subagent invocation starts with a fresh context window containing only what the coordinator explicitly passes to it. This is one of the most consequential architectural facts in multi-agent design, and it is frequently misunderstood by engineers coming from monolithic LLM pipelines.

**What isolation means in practice:**

When the coordinator calls the Analysis Subagent, it must include in that call:
- The specific data to analyse (not a reference to it).
- Any constraints, personas, or output format requirements.
- Relevant prior results if the analysis depends on them.

The subagent has no memory of the user's original question, the coordinator's internal reasoning, or the output of the Search Subagent — unless the coordinator explicitly injects that information.

```python
# Coordinator constructing an isolated subagent call
def call_analysis_subagent(search_results: list[str], user_query: str) -> str:
    prompt = f"""
    You are an analysis agent. Your task is to identify key themes.

    Original user question: {user_query}

    Search results to analyse:
    {chr(10).join(search_results)}

    Return a structured list of themes with supporting evidence.
    """
    return analysis_subagent.invoke(prompt)
```

**Edge case — context window overflow:** If the coordinator accumulates results from many subagents and then passes all of them to a synthesis subagent, the combined payload may exceed the synthesis subagent's context window. Architects must design chunking or summarisation steps between delegation rounds. A common pattern is a *compression subagent* whose sole job is to distil intermediate results before they are passed forward.

**Adjacent topic — stateful agents:** Some frameworks allow subagents to maintain persistent memory across invocations (e.g., via external vector stores). Even then, the coordinator must explicitly trigger memory retrieval; it does not happen automatically. The isolation principle holds at the context-window level regardless of external memory.

**Exam tip:** A question may describe a scenario where a subagent "seems to know" information from an earlier step without the coordinator passing it. The correct diagnosis is almost always that the coordinator is inadvertently including prior conversation turns in the subagent's system prompt or that the subagent is reading from a shared memory store — not that context is inherited automatically.

sections.heading: 3. Coordinator Decomposition and Delegation
sections.body_markdown:
The coordinator's primary cognitive job is task decomposition: taking a complex user request and breaking it into subtasks that individual subagents can execute reliably within their isolated contexts. Decomposition quality determines system quality — a poorly decomposed task produces subagent outputs that are either redundant, conflicting, or collectively incomplete.

**Decomposition decision tree:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  Q[Incoming Query]:::concept --> C1{Single-step task?}:::decision
  C1 -->|yes| D1[Invoke single subagent directly]:::success
  C1 -->|no| C2{Sequential dependencies?}:::decision
  C2 -->|yes| D2[Sequential delegation chain]:::process
  C2 -->|no| D3[Parallel fan-out to independent subagents]:::process
  D2 --> AGG[Coordinator aggregates results]:::process
  D3 --> AGG
  AGG --> C3{Coverage sufficient?}:::decision
  C3 -->|yes| OUT[Return final answer]:::success
  C3 -->|no| REF[Refinement loop — re-delegate]:::warn
  REF --> AGG
```

**Delegation contract:** Each delegation must specify:
1. **Scope** — exactly what the subagent should address.
2. **Input** — all data the subagent needs (no implicit references).
3. **Output format** — structured schema the coordinator can parse reliably.
4. **Constraints** — length limits, source restrictions, confidence thresholds.

**Result aggregation** is the coordinator's second major job. Raw subagent outputs are rarely ready for the user. The coordinator must:
- Merge overlapping findings.
- Resolve contradictions (e.g., two search subagents returning conflicting facts).
- Enforce a consistent voice and format.
- Decide whether to invoke a dedicated synthesis subagent or perform aggregation inline.

**Worked example — competitive intelligence query:**

User asks: *"Summarise the competitive landscape for vector database products as of this year."*

The coordinator decomposes this into:

| Subagent | Assigned scope | Output format |
|:---------|:--------------|:--------------|
| Search-A | Product features and benchmarks | JSON list of {product, features, benchmark_score} |
| Search-B | Pricing and licensing models | JSON list of {product, pricing_tier, license} |
| Search-C | Recent funding and acquisitions | JSON list of {company, event, date, amount} |
| Analysis | Cross-cutting themes from Search-A/B/C outputs | Markdown bullet list |
| Synthesis | Final narrative report | Markdown prose, 600 words |

The coordinator invokes Search-A, Search-B, and Search-C in parallel (no dependencies), waits for all three, passes their combined outputs to Analysis, then passes the analysis to Synthesis.

sections.heading: 4. Narrow Decomposition Coverage Risks
sections.body_markdown:
Overly narrow decomposition is one of the most common production failure modes in coordinator-subagent systems. It occurs when the coordinator's task breakdown is so granular or so specific that the union of all subagent scopes fails to cover the full breadth of the original query.

**How it happens:**

A coordinator prompted to decompose *"Analyse the impact of interest rate changes on technology stocks"* might generate subtasks like:
- Fetch recent Fed rate decisions.
- Fetch NASDAQ index performance.
- Correlate the two datasets.

This decomposition misses: sector rotation dynamics, bond-equity yield spread analysis, international rate differentials, company-level debt sensitivity, and analyst sentiment shifts. The synthesis subagent will produce a confident-sounding but incomplete answer because it can only synthesise what the search subagents returned.

**The coverage gap formula:** If the coordinator decomposes a query of breadth $B$ into $n$ subtasks each covering scope $s_i$, the uncovered breadth is:

$$\text{Gap} = B - \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} s_i$$

A gap $> 0$ means the final answer will be incomplete regardless of how well each subagent performs.

**Mitigation strategies:**

| Strategy | Mechanism | Tradeoff |
|:---------|:----------|:---------|
| Breadth-first decomposition prompt | Instruct coordinator to enumerate all major dimensions before assigning subtasks | Increases coordinator token usage |
| Coverage checklist | Coordinator maintains a checklist of required coverage areas and verifies each is assigned | Adds latency; checklist must be domain-specific |
| Iterative gap detection | Coordinator evaluates synthesis output and identifies missing dimensions (see Section 6) | Multiple LLM calls; higher cost |
| Human-in-the-loop review | Coordinator surfaces its decomposition plan for human approval before executing | Breaks full automation |
| Broad initial search + refinement | First subagent does a wide sweep; coordinator uses its output to generate targeted follow-up subtasks | Two-phase latency |

**Edge case — false completeness:** A synthesis subagent may produce a fluent, well-structured answer that *appears* complete but silently omits uncovered dimensions. The coordinator must not rely on the synthesis subagent to self-report gaps — it must evaluate coverage independently, either by comparing against a pre-defined coverage schema or by running a dedicated gap-detection step.

sections.heading: 5. Dynamic Subagent Selection and Scope Partitioning
sections.body_markdown:
Not every query requires the full pipeline. A coordinator that always routes through every subagent wastes tokens, increases latency, and introduces unnecessary failure points. Dynamic subagent selection means the coordinator analyses the incoming query and invokes only the subagents whose capabilities are actually needed.

**Selection logic pattern:**

```python
def select_subagents(query: str, coordinator_llm) -> list[str]:
    """
    Coordinator analyses query and returns the minimal sufficient
    set of subagent identifiers to invoke.
    """
    selection_prompt = f"""
    Available subagents:
    - search_web: retrieves current information from the web
```
