```
- tests/test_token_handler.py: Updated to reflect the refactor.

Please treat your prior analysis of token_handler.py as potentially stale
and re-read the relevant files before drawing conclusions.
```

This notification does three things:
1. **Invalidates specific prior tool results** without discarding the entire session
2. **Directs the agent's attention** to the files that need re-reading
3. **Preserves the valid baseline** — all analysis of unchanged files remains authoritative

### Targeted Re-Analysis vs. Full Re-Exploration

The power of targeted notification is that it is *surgical*. A large codebase investigation may have read 50 files. If only 3 changed, the agent needs to re-read only those 3 — not restart the entire investigation. This is the core value of **targeted resumed re-analysis**: preserving the 47-file baseline while refreshing the 3 changed files.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Resume session]:::process --> B{Files changed since last session?}:::decision
    B -->|No changes| C[Proceed with full prior context]:::success
    B -->|Some files changed| D[Notify agent of changed files]:::process
    D --> E[Agent re-reads changed files]:::process
    E --> F[Targeted re-analysis on changed scope]:::success
    B -->|Major restructuring| G{Is prior context still useful?}:::decision
    G -->|Partially| D
    G -->|Mostly invalidated| H[Fresh start with injected summary]:::warn
```

### Tooling Integration

In production pipelines, change notification can be automated by diffing the git state at session-start time against the current HEAD:

```bash
# Generate change manifest since session was created
git diff --name-only <session-start-commit> HEAD | \
  xargs -I{} echo "- {}: $(git log --oneline -1 -- {})" \
  > change_manifest.txt

# Prepend to resumed session
claude --resume auth-service-audit < <(cat change_manifest.txt session_prompt.txt)
```

This automation eliminates the human error of forgetting to notify — a common failure in fast-moving development environments.

sections.heading: 5. Fresh Summary Over Stale Resume
sections.body_markdown:
There is a class of situations where resuming a session — even with change notifications — is the wrong choice. When prior tool results are so stale that the cost of correcting them exceeds the cost of re-deriving them, starting fresh with a structured summary is more reliable.

### When Stale Beats Resume

The staleness threshold is not purely about time elapsed. It is about the **fraction of prior tool results that remain valid**. A useful heuristic:

$$\text{Resume if: } \frac{\text{valid prior results}}{\text{total prior results}} > 0.6$$

Below that threshold, the cognitive overhead of tracking which results are stale — both for the agent and for the human reviewing its output — outweighs the benefit of preserved context.

Additional triggers for fresh start:

| Trigger | Reason to Start Fresh |
|:--------|:----------------------|
| Major architectural refactor | File structure, module boundaries, and call graphs have changed |
| Dependency version upgrade | Library APIs, error codes, and behaviour may differ |
| Schema migration | Database queries, ORM models, and data shapes are invalidated |
| Team handoff after weeks | Implicit assumptions in the session may not match new team's context |
| Context window near limit | Resuming would leave insufficient room for new tool calls |

### The Structured Summary Pattern

A fresh start does not mean discarding all prior work. The correct pattern is to distil the prior session's findings into a structured summary and inject it as the opening system or user message of the new session:

```
## Investigation Summary: auth-service-audit (prior session)

### Confirmed Findings
- Token refresh logic had a race condition (now fixed in refresh_service.py)
- JWT signing key rotation is not implemented
- Session invalidation on logout is incomplete for mobile clients

### Ruled Out
- SQL injection in login endpoint (parameterised queries confirmed)
- Timing attack on password comparison (constant-time comparison in use)

### Open Questions
- Does the new refresh_service.py correctly handle concurrent refresh requests?
- Is the mobile session invalidation gap a known accepted risk?

### Files Analysed (prior session)
- auth/token_handler.py, auth/session_manager.py, auth/login_handler.py
- tests/test_auth.py

Please begin a fresh analysis of the auth service, treating the above as
established context but re-reading all files to verify current state.
```

This pattern gives the new session the *conclusions* of the prior session without the *stale tool results* that produced them. The agent can verify, extend, or contradict the summary based on fresh reads — which is exactly the right epistemic posture.

### Why Stale Tool Results Are Worse Than No Results

A tool result from a prior session is not marked as stale in the context window — it looks identical to a fresh result. The agent has no native mechanism to distinguish "I read this file 3 days ago" from "I read this file 30 seconds ago." This is the fundamental asymmetry: **a human knows their notes are old; the agent does not**.

Injecting a structured summary sidesteps this entirely. The summary is explicitly framed as prior findings, not as current tool output, so the agent treats it with appropriate epistemic humility — as a starting hypothesis to verify rather than a ground truth to build on.

sections.heading: 6. Resume vs. Fresh Start: Architectural Decision Framework
sections.body_markdown:
Choosing between session resumption and a fresh start with injected summary is one of the highest-leverage architectural decisions in a long-running agentic workflow. The wrong choice either wastes the value of prior work or silently corrupts the new work.

### The Decision Matrix

| Dimension | Favour Resume | Favour Fresh Start |
|:----------|:-------------|:-------------------|
| **Time since last session** | Hours to 1–2 days | Days to weeks |
| **Code change volume** | Few targeted files | Major refactor or restructure |
| **Context window utilisation** | < 60% full | > 80% full |
| **Prior tool result validity** | > 60% still accurate | < 60% still accurate |
| **Investigation continuity** | Same hypothesis thread | New question or new team member |
| **Cost sensitivity** | Re-derivation is expensive | Re-derivation is cheap |

### Realistic Scenario: The Three-Week Security Audit

**Context**: A platform team runs a security audit of their payments service. The audit starts on a Monday and is named `payments-security-q4`. The team makes significant progress: 40 files analysed, 6 vulnerabilities identified, 12 files cleared.

**Week 1 (Monday–Friday)**: Active investigation. Daily resumptions with small change notifications. Resume is clearly correct — context is fresh, changes are targeted, the investigative thread is tight.

**Week 2 (Monday)**: A sprint boundary. The payments service received a dependency upgrade (Stripe SDK v4 → v5) and a schema migration over the weekend. Approximately 15 of the 40 analysed files were touched.

*Decision point*: $\frac{25}{40} = 62.5\%$ of prior results are still valid — just above the resume threshold. The architect chooses to resume with a detailed change notification covering the 15 affected files. Targeted re-analysis is scoped to those files.

**Week 3 (Monday)**: A major architectural refactor landed — the payments service was split into two microservices. The prior session's entire mental model of the service boundary is now wrong.

*Decision point*: $\frac{\text{valid results}}{\text{total results}} \approx 20\%$ — well below threshold. The architect distils the prior session into a structured summary (confirmed vulnerabilities, ruled-out attack surfaces, open questions) and starts a fresh session for each new microservice.

### The Hybrid Pattern

For large investigations, a hybrid approach often dominates: resume for continuity within a sprint, then distil and restart at sprint boundaries. This gives you the best of both worlds — preserved momentum within a focused work period, and clean context at natural breakpoints.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start investigation]:::concept --> B[Named session: payments-security-q4]:::process
    B --> C{End of sprint / major change?}:::decision
    C -->|No| D[Resume with change notification]:::process
    D --> E[Targeted re-analysis]:::success
    E --> C
    C -->|Yes| F[Distil findings to structured summary]:::process
    F --> G{Staleness > threshold?}:::decision
    G -->|No| H[Resume + inject summary as context refresh]:::process
    G -->|Yes| I[Fresh session + injected summary]:::process
    H --> C
    I --> C
```

### Edge Case: The Interrupted Investigation

An investigation interrupted mid-hypothesis — the agent was in the middle of tracing a call chain when the session ended — is a special case. Resuming is almost always correct here, because the agent's partial reasoning is still valid and the alternative (re-deriving the call chain from scratch) is expensive. The key is to resume quickly, before the codebase changes.

sections.heading: 7. Exam Preparation: Production Tradeoffs and Architectural Judgment
sections.body_markdown:
The Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam tests not just knowledge of session management mechanics but the judgment to apply them correctly under realistic production constraints. The following questions are representative of the reasoning expected.

---

### Exam-Style Question 1

**A team has been running a named session `api-gateway-audit` for two days, accumulating analysis of 60 files. Overnight, a developer merged a PR that refactored 8 of those files to extract a new authentication middleware. The team resumes the session the next morning without any notification to the agent. Which of the following best describes the primary risk?**

**A)** The session will fail to load because the file paths have changed.

**B)** The agent will reason from stale tool results about the 8 refactored files, producing confident but potentially incorrect findings.

**C)** The context window will overflow because the new files add too many tokens.

**D)** The agent will automatically detect the file changes and re-read the affected files.

**✅ Correct Answer: B**

*Explanation*: The agent has no mechanism to detect that files have changed since it last read them. Tool results from prior turns are stored in the context window without timestamps or staleness markers. The agent will treat its prior analysis of the 8 refactored files as current, potentially citing line numbers, function signatures, or logic that no longer exists. Option A is wrong — the session loads regardless of file changes. Option C is possible but not the *primary* risk described. Option D is incorrect — the agent has no automatic file-change detection.

---

### Exam-Style Question 2

**An architect wants to compare two database migration strategies (online migration vs. blue-green deployment) starting from a shared analysis of the current database schema and access patterns. Which session management approach best preserves the shared baseline while preventing cross-contamination between the two strategy analyses?**

**A)** Run both strategy analyses sequentially in the same resumed session.

**B)** Start two completely independent fresh sessions, each re-deriving the schema analysis from scratch.

**C)** Fork the session after the shared schema analysis is complete, creating two independent branches.

**D)** Resume the session for the first strategy, then use `--reset-context` before the second strategy.

**✅ Correct Answer: C**

*Explanation*: `fork_session` at the point of completed shared analysis gives each strategy branch the full validated baseline without requiring either branch to re-derive it. Option A risks contamination — conclusions about the online migration will influence reasoning about blue-green. Option B is correct in isolation but wastes the cost of re-deriving the schema analysis twice. Option D is incorrect — `--reset-context` would discard the shared baseline, defeating the purpose.

---

### Exam-Style Question 3

**A security investigation session named `infra-sec-audit` was last active 3 weeks ago. Since then, the infrastructure has undergone a major Kubernetes version upgrade, a Terraform module restructure, and a secrets management migration. The prior session analysed 45 files. Approximately 10 of those files remain unchanged. What is the most architecturally sound approach?**

**A)** Resume `infra-sec-audit` and notify the agent about all changed files.

**B)** Resume `infra-sec-audit` without notification, since the agent's general knowledge of the infrastructure is still useful.

**C)** Distil the prior session's confirmed findings and open questions into a structured summary, then start a fresh session with that summary injected as context.

**D)** Fork `infra-sec-audit` into two branches: one for the Kubernetes changes and one for the Terraform changes.

**✅ Correct Answer: C**

*Explanation*: With only 10 of 45 files unchanged, approximately $\frac{10}{45} \approx 22\%$ of prior tool results remain valid — far below the resume threshold. The prior session's stale tool results would actively mislead the agent about the current infrastructure state. A structured summary preserves the *conclusions* (confirmed vulnerabilities, cleared areas, open questions) without carrying the stale tool results that produced them. Option A is tempting but impractical — notifying about 35 changed files is nearly equivalent to a fresh start, and the agent would still hold stale results for those files until it re-reads them. Option B is the most dangerous choice. Option D is a misapplication of `fork_session` — forking is for divergent exploration from a valid baseline, not for managing staleness.

---

### Key Architectural Principles Summary

| Principle | Production Implication |
|:----------|:----------------------|
| **Stale tool results are invisible** | The agent cannot self-detect staleness; the architect must manage it explicitly |
| **Fork at the last valid shared moment** | Forking after divergent work has begun contaminates the "clean" branch |
| **Summaries carry conclusions, not evidence** | Fresh-start summaries give the agent hypotheses to verify, not facts to accept |
| **Named sessions enable team coordination** | Consistent naming conventions prevent session collisions and enable handoffs |
| **Targeted notification is surgical** | Notifying about specific changed files preserves the valid baseline |
| **Cost model favours forking over re-derivation** | When baseline derivation is expensive, forking dominates two independent sessions |

key_takeaways: Named session resumption (`--resume <session-name>`) preserves investigative momentum across work sessions, but every resumption after code changes requires an explicit change notification to prevent the agent from reasoning on stale tool results.

`fork_session` should be invoked at the last moment of shared validity — after a complete baseline analysis but before any divergent exploration begins — to give each branch clean, uncontaminated context.

The resume-vs-fresh-start decision is driven by the fraction of prior tool results that remain valid: above ~60% validity, resume with targeted notifications; below ~60%, distil findings into a structured summary and start fresh.

Stale tool results are architecturally more dangerous than no results, because the agent treats them as current evidence and cannot self-detect their staleness — the architect must manage this explicitly.

Targeted resumed re-analysis is surgical: notifying the agent about specific changed files allows it to re-read only those files while preserving the validated baseline for unchanged files, minimising both cost and disruption.

The hybrid pattern — resume within a sprint, distil and restart at sprint boundaries — captures the benefits of both approaches and is the recommended default for long-running production investigations.
further_reading: Context window management and token budget optimisation in long-running agentic sessions
Agentic tool-call result caching and invalidation strategies
Multi-agent coordination patterns: when to use parallel agents vs. forked sessions vs. sequential handoffs
Structured prompt injection patterns for knowledge transfer between sessions
Git-integrated session state management: automating change manifests from commit history

ts-2-1.title: 2.1 Design effective tool interfaces with clear descriptions and boundaries

lesson.topic: 2.1 Design effective tool interfaces with clear descriptions and boundaries
lesson.synopsis: Tool descriptions are the primary signal Claude uses to decide which tool to invoke — poorly written or overlapping descriptions are a leading cause of misrouting in production agentic systems. This lesson examines how to craft rich, differentiated tool descriptions, eliminate functional overlap through renaming and splitting, and audit system prompts for keyword-sensitive instructions that can silently override even well-written descriptions. The goal is to give architects the judgment needed to design tool interfaces that behave reliably at scale.
learning_objectives: Explain how Claude uses tool descriptions as the primary mechanism for tool selection and why minimal descriptions produce unreliable behavior among similar tools.
Write rich tool descriptions that include input formats, example queries, edge cases, and explicit boundary explanations.
Identify overlapping tool descriptions and apply renaming and splitting strategies to eliminate functional ambiguity.
Diagnose system prompt keyword sensitivity and audit prompts for instructions that create unintended tool associations.
Apply architectural judgment to tradeoffs between generic multi-purpose tools and purpose-specific tools with defined input/output contracts.

sections.heading: 1. Descriptions Drive Tool Selection
sections.body_markdown:
Tool descriptions are the single most important signal Claude uses when deciding which tool to call. When a user message arrives, Claude does not execute code to probe tool behavior — it reads the description strings you provide and reasons about which tool best matches the intent. This makes the description field functionally equivalent to a routing rule in a traditional API gateway, except the routing is probabilistic and language-driven rather than deterministic and path-driven.

### Why minimal descriptions fail

Consider a system with two tools registered with bare-minimum descriptions:

```python
tools = [
    {"name": "analyze_content",   "description": "Analyzes content."},
    {"name": "analyze_document",  "description": "Analyzes a document."}
]
```

From Claude's perspective, these descriptions are semantically indistinguishable. A user query like *"Can you look at this PDF report?"* could legitimately route to either tool. In practice, Claude will make a choice — but that choice will be sensitive to superficial cues: the order the tools appear in the list, keywords in the system prompt, or even the phrasing of the user's message. None of these are the routing criteria you intended.

### The selection mechanism in detail

Claude's tool selection process can be modeled as a soft-matching operation over the semantic space of the user's intent and the tool descriptions:

$$\hat{t} = \arg\max_{t \in T} \; \text{sim}(\text{intent}(q),\; \text{desc}(t))$$

where $q$ is the user query, $T$ is the set of available tools, and $\text{sim}$ is a learned similarity function. When two descriptions are near-identical, the similarity scores are nearly equal and the selection becomes unstable — small perturbations in phrasing flip the outcome.

### Production consequence

In a production system processing thousands of requests per day, even a 5% misrouting rate compounds quickly. If `analyze_document` is the expensive tool (calling a vector database and an OCR pipeline) and `analyze_content` is the cheap one (a simple regex pass), random misrouting between them creates both cost overruns and incorrect results — and the errors are silent because the tool call succeeds; it just returns the wrong kind of answer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[User query arrives]:::concept
    D{Claude reads tool descriptions}:::decision
    M[Descriptions are minimal / identical]:::warn
    R[Descriptions are rich / differentiated]:::concept
    MIS[Misrouting — wrong tool called]:::warn
    OK[Correct tool selected]:::success

    Q --> D
    D --> M
    D --> R
    M --> MIS
    R --> OK
```

**Key architectural principle:** Treat every tool description as a first-class routing contract. The description is not documentation for human readers — it is executable routing logic for the model.

sections.heading: 2. Rich Description Content
sections.body_markdown:
A rich tool description answers four questions that Claude needs to route correctly: *What does this tool do? What inputs does it expect? What does it return? When should I use it instead of a similar tool?*

### The anatomy of a rich description

A well-structured description contains:

| Element | Purpose | Example |
|:--------|:--------|:--------|
| **Core function** | One-sentence statement of what the tool does | "Extracts structured data fields from a PDF invoice" |
| **Input format** | Accepted types, encodings, size limits | "Accepts a base64-encoded PDF up to 10 MB" |
| **Output contract** | Shape and type of the return value | "Returns a JSON object with keys: vendor, amount, date, line_items" |
| **Example queries** | Concrete user utterances that should trigger this tool | "e.g., 'Pull the total from this invoice', 'What vendor sent this bill?'" |
| **Edge cases** | What the tool handles that might surprise the model | "Also handles scanned PDFs via embedded OCR; does not process password-protected files" |
| **Boundary statement** | Explicit statement of what the tool does NOT do | "Does not summarize or interpret the document — use summarize_content for that" |

### Before and after

**Before (minimal):**
```python
{
  "name": "extract_invoice_data",
  "description": "Extracts data from invoices."
}
```

**After (rich):**
```python
{
  "name": "extract_invoice_data",
  "description": (
      "Extracts structured fields from a PDF invoice and returns them as JSON. "
      "Input: a base64-encoded PDF string (max 10 MB). "
      "Output: JSON with keys vendor_name, invoice_number, issue_date, due_date, "
      "line_items (array), subtotal, tax, total_amount. "
      "Use this tool when the user asks to pull, read, or retrieve specific values "
      "from an invoice file — e.g., 'What is the total on this invoice?' or "
      "'List the line items from this bill.' "
      "Handles scanned PDFs via OCR. Does NOT summarize or explain the invoice; "
      "use summarize_content for narrative summaries. "
      "Does NOT process password-protected PDFs."
  )
}
```

### Edge cases worth including

- **Format variants:** If a tool accepts both JSON and CSV, say so explicitly. Otherwise Claude may assume it only handles one format and route elsewhere.
- **Negative examples:** Stating what the tool does *not* do is often more valuable than stating what it does, because it prevents false-positive matches.
- **Size and rate limits:** Including "max 10 MB" prevents Claude from routing a 50 MB file to the tool and then handling a downstream error — it can proactively tell the user the file is too large.
- **Ambiguous verbs:** Words like "analyze," "process," and "handle" are semantically broad. Replace them with precise verbs: "extracts," "classifies," "validates," "summarizes," "compares."

### Connection to adjacent topics

Rich descriptions also serve as implicit few-shot examples for the model's parameter-filling step. When the description says *"Input: a base64-encoded PDF string,"* Claude is more likely to correctly encode the file before passing it as an argument, reducing argument-format errors downstream.

sections.heading: 3. Overlapping Descriptions Cause Misrouting
sections.body_markdown:
Overlapping tool descriptions are the most common source of silent routing failures in multi-tool systems. When two tools share vocabulary, purpose language, or input types in their descriptions, Claude cannot reliably distinguish them — and the failure mode is insidious because the system appears to work (a tool is called, a result is returned) while actually producing wrong outputs.

### A canonical misrouting scenario

Imagine a content intelligence platform with these two tools:

```python
tools = [
    {
        "name": "analyze_content",
        "description": "Analyzes content and returns insights. Use for web pages and articles."
    },
    {
        "name": "analyze_document",
        "description": "Analyzes a document and returns insights. Use for files and documents."
    }
]
```

A user sends: *"Analyze this article I uploaded as a PDF."*

The query contains both "article" (pointing to `analyze_content`) and "PDF" (pointing to `analyze_document`). Claude must break the tie using some signal — and that signal is arbitrary from the system designer's perspective. In testing, you might observe 60% routing to `analyze_document` and 40% to `analyze_content`, with the split varying by Claude version, system prompt wording, and conversation history.

### Why overlap is structurally dangerous

The problem is not just incorrect routing — it is *inconsistent* routing. A system that routes correctly 80% of the time is harder to debug than one that always fails, because:

1. Errors are intermittent and hard to reproduce.
2. The failure condition is not an exception — it is a semantically wrong but syntactically valid tool call.
3. Downstream systems receive plausible-looking but incorrect data.

### Measuring overlap

A practical heuristic: if you can swap the names of two tools in their descriptions without the descriptions becoming obviously wrong, the descriptions overlap too much. More formally, compute the cosine similarity between the embedding vectors of the two descriptions:

$$\text{overlap} = \frac{\vec{d_1} \cdot \vec{d_2}}{\|\vec{d_1}\| \cdot \|\vec{d_2}\|}$$

Descriptions with cosine similarity above approximately $0.92$ (using a standard sentence embedding model) are empirically prone to misrouting. This is not a hard threshold — treat it as a signal to review, not a rule to enforce blindly.

### The overlap audit checklist

- [ ] Do two or more descriptions use the same action verbs ("analyzes," "processes")?
- [ ] Do two or more descriptions list the same input types without distinguishing them?
- [ ] Could a reasonable user query plausibly trigger either tool?
- [ ] Are the tool names themselves semantically similar (e.g., `analyze_content` vs `analyze_document`)?

If any box is checked, the descriptions need differentiation, renaming, or splitting — covered in the next two sections.

sections.heading: 4. System Prompt Keyword Sensitivity
sections.body_markdown:
System prompts can silently override well-written tool descriptions by introducing keyword associations that Claude picks up during its reasoning. This is one of the least-understood failure modes in production agentic systems, and it requires deliberate auditing to catch.

### How keyword sensitivity works

Claude reads the system prompt before processing any user message or tool list. If the system prompt contains instructions that associate certain keywords with certain behaviors, those associations can influence tool selection even when the tool descriptions are unambiguous.

**Example — unintended association:**

```
System prompt:
"You are a document intelligence assistant. When the user mentions a 'document',
always use the most thorough analysis available."
```

This instruction seems harmless, but it creates a keyword trigger: the word "document" → "most thorough analysis." If the system has both `extract_data_points` (fast, targeted) and `analyze_document` (slow, comprehensive), the system prompt instruction will bias Claude toward `analyze_document` whenever the user says "document" — even when the user only needs a quick field extraction.

### Keyword sensitivity patterns to watch for

| Pattern | Example system prompt phrase | Unintended effect |
|:--------|:-----------------------------|:------------------|
| **Verb anchoring** | "Always *analyze* user-provided content" | Biases toward any tool with "analyze" in its name or description |
| **Noun anchoring** | "Treat every *file* as a document" | Conflates file-handling tools with document-handling tools |
| **Superlative instructions** | "Use the most comprehensive tool available" | Consistently routes to the most expensive tool regardless of need |
| **Negation traps** | "Never use simple extraction" | Suppresses lightweight tools even when they are correct |
| **Role framing** | "You are a deep research analyst" | Biases toward research/analysis tools over retrieval tools |

### A subtle real-world case

A financial services team deployed a system with this system prompt fragment:

```
"You are a compliance assistant. Always verify claims against source documents."
```

They had two tools: `verify_claim_against_source` (correct for compliance checks) and `summarize_content` (for general summaries). The word "verify" in the system prompt created a strong association with `verify_claim_against_source`. When users asked for a simple summary of a regulation, Claude would call `verify_claim_against_source` instead of `summarize_content` — because the system prompt had primed it to "verify" everything.

### The interaction model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[System prompt keywords]:::concept
    TD[Tool descriptions]:::concept
    CR[Claude reasoning]:::process
    TS[Tool selection]:::decision
    INT[Intended tool]:::success
    UNINT[Unintended tool]:::warn

    SP --> CR
    TD --> CR
    CR --> TS
    TS -->|aligned| INT
    TS -->|keyword override| UNINT
```

The key insight is that system prompt instructions and tool descriptions are **not isolated** — they are both inputs to the same reasoning process, and they interact. A system prompt that is perfectly reasonable in isolation can corrupt an otherwise well-designed tool interface.

sections.heading: 5. Differentiating and Renaming Tools
sections.body_markdown:
Once you have identified overlapping descriptions, the first remediation strategy is differentiation through rewriting and renaming. The goal is to make each tool's description occupy a distinct region of semantic space so that no plausible user query maps ambiguously to two tools.

### Differentiating descriptions

Effective differentiation requires making four things explicit for each tool:

1. **Unique purpose:** What this tool does that no other tool does.
2. **Unique input:** What input format or source this tool expects.
3. **Unique output:** What the return value looks like.
4. **Contrast statement:** Explicit mention of when to use *this* tool versus the most similar alternative.

**Before (overlapping):**

```python
{"name": "analyze_content",  "description": "Analyzes content and returns insights."},
{"name": "analyze_document", "description": "Analyzes a document and returns insights."}
```

**After (differentiated):**

```python
{
  "name": "extract_web_results",
  "description": (
      "Fetches and parses a live web page URL, then extracts the main textual content, "
      "metadata (title, author, publish date), and any structured data (tables, lists). "
      "Input: a valid HTTP/HTTPS URL string. "
      "Output: JSON with keys url, title, author, publish_date, body_text, tables. "
      "Use when the user provides a URL or asks about a web page, article, or online source. "
      "Do NOT use for uploaded files or local documents — use extract_data_points or "
      "summarize_content for those."
  )
},
{
  "name": "analyze_document",
  "description": (
      "Performs deep semantic analysis of an uploaded document (PDF, DOCX, or TXT), "
      "identifying themes, argument structure, sentiment, and key entities. "
      "Input: a base64-encoded file string plus a MIME type. "
      "Output: JSON with keys themes (array), argument_map, sentiment_score, entities. "
      "Use when the user uploads a file and asks for interpretation, not just extraction. "
      "Do NOT use for web URLs — use extract_web_results for those."
  )
}
```

### Renaming to remove overlap

Renaming is often the highest-leverage intervention because the tool name itself is part of the signal Claude uses. Names like `analyze_content` and `analyze_document` share the verb "analyze" and differ only in a noun that is nearly synonymous in common usage. Renaming `analyze_content` to `extract_web_results` immediately:

- Removes the shared verb.
- Introduces a domain-specific noun ("web") that anchors the tool to a specific input type.
- Makes the contrast with `analyze_document` obvious without requiring the user to read the full description.

| Old name | Problem | New name | Improvement |
|:---------|:--------|:---------|:------------|
| `analyze_content` | Shares verb with `analyze_document`; "content" is too broad | `extract_web_results` | Domain-specific; verb "extract" is distinct from "analyze" |
| `process_file` | "Process" is semantically empty; "file" overlaps with "document" | `convert_file_format` | Precise verb; implies a specific transformation |
| `get_data` | Maximally generic; matches almost any query | `query_customer_database` | Scoped to a specific data source and operation |
| `handle_request` | No semantic content at all | `route_support_ticket` | Describes the specific action and domain |

### Architectural judgment: when renaming is not enough

Renaming resolves overlap when two tools are genuinely distinct but poorly named. If two tools are genuinely doing overlapping work — if their *implementations* share significant logic — renaming is cosmetic. In that case, the right intervention is splitting (covered in the next section) or merging, depending on whether the overlap is accidental or intentional.

sections.heading: 6. Splitting Generic Tools
sections.body_markdown:
Generic tools — tools designed to "do everything" with a document, a dataset, or a request — are a common architectural antipattern in agentic systems. They create routing ambiguity, make descriptions impossible to write precisely, and produce outputs that are too broad to be reliably consumed by downstream steps.

### The generic tool antipattern

A team building a document intelligence system might start with a single tool:

```python
{
  "name": "analyze_document",
  "description": "Analyzes a document. Can extract data, summarize, or verify claims."
}
```

This tool does three distinct things. Its description is forced to be vague because it must cover all three. Claude cannot know which of the three operations to perform without additional context — and the tool's implementation must somehow infer the intent from the arguments, adding complexity and failure modes.

### Splitting into purpose-specific tools

The correct intervention is to split `analyze_document` into three tools, each with a defined input/output contract:

```python
tools = [
    {
        "name": "extract_data_points",
        "description": (
            "Extracts specific structured fields from a document (PDF, DOCX, or TXT). "
            "Input: base64-encoded file + a list of field names to extract (e.g., ['date', 'total', 'vendor']). "
            "Output: JSON object mapping each requested field name to its extracted value or null if not found. "
            "Use when the user asks to pull, read, or retrieve specific values from a document. "
            "Does NOT summarize or interpret — use summarize_content for that."
        )
    },
    {
        "name": "summarize_content",
        "description": (
            "Generates a concise narrative summary of a document's main points. "
            "Input: base64-encoded file (PDF, DOCX, TXT) and an optional target_length "
            "('short' = 1 paragraph, 'medium' = 3 paragraphs, 'long' = full executive summary). "
            "Output: a plain-text string containing the summary. "
            "Use when the user asks to summarize, explain, or give an overview of a document. "
            "Does NOT extract structured fields — use extract_data_points for that."
        )
    },
    {
        "name": "verify_claim_against_source",
        "description": (
            "Checks whether a specific factual claim is supported, contradicted, or unaddressed "
            "by the content of a provided document. "
            "Input: base64-encoded file + a claim string (e.g., 'The contract expires on 2025-12-31'). "
            "Output: JSON with keys verdict ('supported'|'contradicted'|'not_found'), "
            "confidence (0.0–1.0), and supporting_excerpt (string or null). "
            "Use when the user asks to check, confirm, or validate a specific statement against a document. "
            "Does NOT summarize or extract fields."
        )
    }
]
```

### The input/output contract as an architectural primitive

Each split tool now has a **defined input/output contract** — a precise specification of what goes in and what comes out. This is the key architectural benefit of splitting:

- **Downstream composability:** A pipeline step that calls `extract_data_points` knows it will receive a JSON object with named fields. It does not need to parse a free-form analysis result.
- **Error isolation:** If `verify_claim_against_source` fails, the failure is scoped to claim verification. A generic `analyze_document` failure gives no information about which sub-operation failed.
- **Independent scaling:** `extract_data_points` might be called 100× more often than `verify_claim_against_source`. With separate tools, you can scale them independently.

### Tradeoffs of splitting

| Dimension | Generic tool | Split tools |
|:----------|:-------------|:------------|
| **Description clarity** | Forced to be vague | Each description is precise |
| **Routing reliability** | Low — Claude must infer sub-intent | High — sub-intent is the tool selection |
| **Tool list size** | Small | Grows with granularity |
| **Maintenance surface** | One tool to update | Multiple tools to keep consistent |
| **Composability** | Poor — output shape varies | High — each output is typed |
| **Latency** | One round-trip | May require multiple round-trips |

The production tradeoff is real: splitting increases the number of tools Claude must reason over, which can slow selection and increase the chance of a different kind of misrouting (choosing the wrong specific tool). The mitigation is rich descriptions with explicit contrast statements — exactly what this lesson teaches.

```svg
<svg viewBox="0 0 320 160" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Generic tool splitting into three purpose-specific tools">
  <!-- Generic tool box -->
  <rect x="10" y="60" width="90" height="40" rx="6" fill="#f59e0b" opacity="0.9"/>
  <text x="55" y="76" text-anchor="middle" font-size="8" fill="#1f2937" font-family="monospace">analyze_document</text>
  <text x="55" y="89" text-anchor="middle" font-size="7" fill="#1f2937" font-family="sans-serif">(generic)</text>

  <!-- Arrow from generic to split -->
  <line x1="100" y1="80" x2="130" y2="80" stroke="#334155" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#arr)"/>
  <text x="115" y="73" text-anchor="middle" font-size="7" fill="#334155">split</text>

  <!-- Three specific tools -->
  <rect x="135" y="10" width="110" height="36" rx="6" fill="#6366f1" opacity="0.85"/>
  <text x="190" y="25" text-anchor="middle" font-size="7.5" fill="#fff" font-family="monospace">extract_data_points</text>
  <text x="190" y="38" text-anchor="middle" font-size="6.5" fill="#e0e7ff" font-family="sans-serif">fields → JSON object</text>

  <rect x="135" y="62" width="110" height="36" rx="6" fill="#6366f1" opacity="0.85"/>
  <text x="190" y="77" text-anchor="middle" font-size="7.5" fill="#fff" font-family="monospace">summarize_content</text>
  <text x="190" y="90" text-anchor="middle" font-size="6.5" fill="#e0e7ff" font-family="sans-serif">doc → plain-text summary</text>

  <rect x="135" y="114" width="110" height="36" rx="6" fill="#6366f1" opacity="0.85"/>
  <text x="190" y="129" text-anchor="middle" font-size="7.5" fill="#fff" font-family="monospace">verify_claim_against</text>
  <text x="190" y="142" text-anchor="middle" font-size="6.5" fill="#e0e7ff" font-family="sans-serif">claim + doc → verdict JSON</text>

  <!-- Connector lines from arrow to boxes -->
  <line x1="130" y1="80" x2="135" y2="28" stroke="#334155" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="3 2"/>
  <line x1="130" y1="80" x2="135" y2="80" stroke="#334155" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="3 2"/>
  <line x1="130" y1="80" x2="135" y2="132" stroke="#334155" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="3 2"/>

  <!-- Animate the split lines growing -->
  <defs>
    <marker id="arr" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6" refX="3" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z" fill="#334155"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
</svg>
```

sections.heading: 7. Auditing Keyword-Sensitive Prompts
sections.body_markdown:
Auditing system prompts for keyword sensitivity is a production discipline, not a one-time setup task. As system prompts evolve — through feature additions, compliance requirements, or persona tuning — they accumulate instructions that can silently corrupt tool routing. A structured audit process catches these regressions before they reach users.

### The audit process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Collect current system prompt]:::process
    B[Extract all imperative instructions]:::process
    C{Does any instruction contain\na tool-name keyword?}:::decision
    D[Flag for review]:::warn
    E{Does any instruction use\nbroad action verbs?}:::decision
    F[Flag for review]:::warn
    G[Run routing test suite]:::process
    H{Any unexpected\ntool selections?}:::decision
    I[Trace to system prompt phrase]:::process
    J[Rewrite or scope the instruction]:::process
    K[Audit complete]:::success

    A --> B --> C
    C -->|yes| D --> G
    C -->|no| E
    E -->|yes| F --> G
    E -->|no| G
    G --> H
    H -->|yes| I --> J --> G
    H -->|no| K
```

### Step 1: Extract imperative instructions

Parse the system prompt for sentences containing imperative verbs: "always," "never," "use," "prefer," "avoid," "ensure." These are the instructions most likely to create keyword associations.

**Example extraction:**

```
System prompt fragment:
"Always analyze user-provided content thoroughly. Never skip document verification.
Use the most comprehensive tool when documents are involved."

Extracted imperatives:
- "Always analyze ... thoroughly" → keyword: analyze
- "Never skip document verification" → keyword: document, verification
- "Use the most comprehensive tool when documents are involved" → keyword: document
```

### Step 2: Cross-reference with tool names and descriptions

For each extracted keyword, check whether it appears in any tool name or description. If it does, the instruction may create an unintended association.

| System prompt keyword | Appears in tool | Risk |
|:----------------------|:----------------|:-----|
| `analyze` | `analyze_document` (name) | High — verb match on tool name |
| `document` | `analyze_document` (name), `extract_data_points` (description) | Medium — noun match |
| `verification` | `verify_claim_against_source` (name) | High — root word match |
| `comprehensive` | `analyze_document` (description: "deep semantic analysis") | Medium — semantic match |

### Step 3: Build a routing test suite

A routing test suite is a set of (query, expected_tool) pairs that you run against the live system after any system prompt change. Minimum coverage:

- One query per tool that should clearly route to that tool.
- One query per pair of similar tools that tests the boundary between them.
- One query that contains keywords from the system prompt to verify they do not override tool descriptions.

```python
routing_tests = [
    {"query": "Pull the vendor name from this invoice PDF",
     "expected_tool": "extract_data_points"},
    {"query": "Give me a summary of this contract",
     "expected_tool": "summarize_content"},
    {"query": "Does this document say the warranty is 2 years?",
     "expected_tool": "verify_claim_against_source"},
    # Boundary test — 'analyze' keyword from system prompt
    {"query": "Analyze this document and tell me the key themes",
     "expected_tool": "summarize_content"},  # NOT analyze_document if themes = summary
]
```

### Step 4: Rewrite keyword-sensitive instructions

When an instruction creates an unintended association, rewrite it to be scoped to the intended behavior without using tool-name vocabulary.

**Before:**
```
"Always analyze documents thoroughly."
```

**After:**
```
"When the user uploads a file, ensure the response addresses their specific question 
completely — do not truncate or skip sections of the document."
```

The rewritten instruction preserves the intent (thoroughness) without anchoring to the word "analyze" or "document" in a way that biases tool selection.

### Governance recommendation

For production systems, treat system prompt changes as requiring the same review process as code changes: a pull request, a routing test run, and sign-off from the architect responsible for the tool interface. The system prompt is part of the tool interface — it is not a configuration file that can be edited casually.

sections.heading: 8. Realistic Scenario and Exam Questions
sections.body_markdown:
## Realistic Scenario: The Compliance Intelligence Platform

A financial services firm deploys an agentic compliance assistant with the following initial tool configuration:

```python
tools = [
    {"name": "analyze_content",
     "description": "Analyzes content and returns insights."},
    {"name": "analyze_document",
     "description": "Analyzes a document and returns insights."},
    {"name": "get_regulation",
     "description": "Gets regulatory information."}
]
```

The system prompt reads:

```
"You are a compliance intelligence assistant. Always analyze user content carefully.
When regulations are mentioned, use the most thorough analysis available.
Never provide incomplete answers about documents."
```

**Observed failures in production:**

1. Queries about web-based regulatory guidance route randomly between `analyze_content` and `analyze_document`.
2. Simple requests to retrieve a regulation's text trigger `analyze_document` instead of `get_regulation`, because the system prompt phrase "most thorough analysis" biases toward the tool with "analyze" in its name.
3. A user asking "What does GDPR say about data retention?" triggers `analyze_document` even though no document was uploaded — the system prompt's "Never provide incomplete answers about documents" creates a spurious association.

**Remediation applied:**

1. Renamed `analyze_content` → `extract_web_results` with a URL-specific description.
2. Rewrote `analyze_document` with explicit input format (base64 file), output contract (themes, entities, sentiment), and a contrast statement against `extract_web_results`.
3. Renamed `get_regulation` → `query_regulation_database` with a description specifying it accepts a regulation name or code and returns the official text.
4. Rewrote the system prompt to remove "analyze," "document," and "thorough analysis" from imperative instructions.
5. Built a 15-query routing test suite and ran it after each change.

**Result:** Misrouting rate dropped from ~35% to under 2% in A/B testing.

---

## Exam-Style Questions

### Question 1

A system has two tools: `search_knowledge_base` (description: "Searches the internal knowledge base for relevant documents") and `retrieve_document` (description: "Retrieves a document from the knowledge base"). Users report that queries like "Find the onboarding guide" sometimes call `search_knowledge_base` and sometimes call `retrieve_document`. What is the most likely root cause?

**A.** The tools have different latency profiles, causing Claude to prefer the faster one inconsistently.

**B.** The tool descriptions overlap in purpose (both retrieve content from the knowledge base) and do not differentiate when to use each tool versus the other.

**C.** The tool names are too long, causing Claude to truncate them during reasoning.

**D.** The user query does not contain enough tokens for Claude to make a reliable selection.

**Correct answer: B**

> **Explanation:** Both descriptions reference the same data source (knowledge base) and similar actions (search/retrieve documents). Claude cannot reliably distinguish between them because neither description explains when to use it *instead of* the other. Option A is incorrect — Claude does not observe tool latency during selection. Option C is incorrect — name length is not a routing factor. Option D is incorrect — the query length is sufficient; the problem is description quality, not query length.

---

### Question 2

An architect is reviewing a system prompt that contains the instruction: *"Always use the most comprehensive analysis tool when the user mentions a file."* The system has three tools: `extract_data_points`, `summarize_content`, and `verify_claim_against_source`. What is the primary risk of this instruction?

**A.** It will cause Claude to ignore tool descriptions entirely and always call the first tool in the list.

**B.** It creates a keyword association between the word "file" and whichever tool Claude interprets as "most comprehensive," potentially overriding correct tool selection based on user intent.

**C.** It will cause Claude to request clarification from the user before every tool call.

**D.** It has no effect because tool descriptions always take precedence over system prompt instructions.

**Correct answer: B**

> **Explanation:** The instruction introduces a superlative bias ("most comprehensive") tied to a keyword trigger ("file"). Claude will attempt to satisfy this instruction, which may cause it to route to `verify_claim_against_source` or `summarize_content` based on which it interprets as more comprehensive — regardless of what the user actually needs. Option A is incorrect — Claude does not ignore descriptions entirely. Option C is incorrect — the instruction does not trigger clarification behavior. Option D is incorrect — system prompt instructions and tool descriptions interact; neither unconditionally overrides the other.

---

### Question 3

A team is deciding whether to split a generic `process_request` tool into three purpose-specific tools. Which of the following is the strongest architectural argument *against* splitting in this specific case: the three sub-operations are called in a fixed sequence (A → B → C) and always together, with no user-facing scenario that requires only one of them?

**A.** Splitting increases the number of tools Claude must reason over, which always degrades routing accuracy.

**B.** If the three operations are always called together in a fixed sequence, splitting them into separate tools adds round-trip overhead and description complexity without improving routing reliability, since the user never needs to select among them individually.

**C.** Splitting tools is only valid when the tools have different input formats.

**D.** Claude cannot handle more than two tools in a single system, so splitting would exceed the limit.

**Correct answer: B**

> **Explanation:** The architectural judgment here is that splitting is valuable when it improves routing reliability or composability. If the three operations are always called together in a fixed sequence, the user never needs Claude to choose among them — the orchestration logic should handle the sequence internally, and a single well-described tool (or a pipeline abstraction) is cleaner. Option A is an overstatement — splitting can improve routing accuracy when descriptions are well-written. Option C is incorrect — input format is one reason to split, not the only one. Option D is incorrect — Claude supports many tools simultaneously.

key_takeaways: Tool descriptions are executable routing logic, not documentation — treat them as first-class architectural artifacts that determine which tool Claude calls.
Rich descriptions must include input formats, output contracts, example queries, edge cases, and explicit contrast statements against similar tools; vague descriptions produce unreliable routing.
Overlapping descriptions cause silent misrouting — two tools with near-identical descriptions will be selected inconsistently, producing intermittent errors that are hard to reproduce and debug.
System prompt instructions interact with tool descriptions and can override them through keyword associations; audit every imperative instruction for tool-name vocabulary and superlative biases.
Renaming tools to use domain-specific, precise verbs (e.g., `extract_web_results` instead of `analyze_content`) is often the highest-leverage single intervention for eliminating routing ambiguity.

Splitting generic tools into purpose-specific tools with defined input/output contracts improves routing reliability, downstream composability, and error isolation — but adds maintenance surface and should only be done when sub-operations are independently selectable.
Maintain a routing test suite and treat system prompt changes with the same review rigor as code changes — the system prompt is part of the tool interface.
further_reading: Tool call parameter validation and argument-format contracts — how to enforce input schemas at the API layer to complement description-level routing controls.
Multi-step agentic orchestration patterns — how tool selection interacts with planning, memory, and error recovery in long-horizon tasks.
Prompt injection and adversarial tool misuse — how malicious user inputs can exploit keyword-sensitive system prompts to trigger unintended tool calls.
Evaluation frameworks for agentic systems — building automated routing test suites and measuring misrouting rates in CI/CD pipelines.
Claude's tool use documentation and best practices — the official Anthropic guidance on structuring tool definitions for production deployments.

ts-2-2.title: 2.2 Implement structured error responses for MCP tools

lesson.topic: 2.2 Implement structured error responses for MCP tools
lesson.synopsis: Structured error responses are the backbone of resilient, self-healing multi-agent systems built on the Model Context Protocol (MCP). This lesson dissects every layer of the error-response contract — from the `isError` flag and error category taxonomy through retryability metadata, business-rule violation flags, and the critical distinction between access failures and valid empty results. Architects will leave with the judgment to design error surfaces that empower agents to recover autonomously, communicate failures clearly, and avoid wasted work.
learning_objectives: Explain the role of the MCP `isError` flag and why it must be paired with structured metadata rather than used alone.
Classify errors into the four canonical categories (transient, validation, business, permission) and map each to the correct agent recovery strategy.
Design structured error response schemas that include `errorCategory`, `isRetryable`, and human-readable descriptions to enable autonomous agent recovery.
Distinguish between access failures and valid empty results, and articulate why conflating them causes incorrect agent behaviour.
Apply local-recovery and propagation patterns within subagent architectures to minimise coordinator-level error handling overhead.

sections.heading: 1. The MCP `isError` Flag — Signal, Not Solution
sections.body_markdown:
The `isError` flag is the MCP protocol's primary mechanism for communicating tool-level failures back to the calling agent. When a tool sets `isError: true` in its response, it signals that the operation did not complete successfully — but the flag alone tells the agent almost nothing about *what* went wrong or *what to do next*.

In the MCP tool-response envelope, a minimal error looks like this:

```json
{
  "isError": true,
  "content": [
    { "type": "text", "text": "Operation failed." }
  ]
}
```

This is technically valid MCP, but architecturally it is a dead end. The agent receives a binary signal — failure — with no actionable information. Compare it with a structured response:

```json
{
  "isError": true,
  "content": [
    {
      "type": "text",
      "text": "Inventory service timed out after 3000 ms."
    }
  ],
  "_meta": {
    "errorCategory": "transient",
    "isRetryable": true,
    "retryAfterMs": 2000,
    "errorCode": "INVENTORY_TIMEOUT",
    "attemptedOperation": "GET /inventory/items?sku=ABC-99"
  }
}
```

The `_meta` field is the MCP-sanctioned extension namespace for tool-specific metadata. Placing structured error data here keeps the response envelope valid while giving the agent everything it needs to make an autonomous recovery decision.

**Key architectural principle:** Treat `isError` as a *routing signal* — it tells the agent to switch from the happy-path handler to the error handler. The error handler's quality depends entirely on the richness of the accompanying metadata. A flag without metadata is like a fire alarm with no evacuation map.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  A[Tool executes]:::process --> B{Succeeded?}:::decision
  B -->|yes| C[isError: false + result payload]:::success
  B -->|no| D[isError: true]:::warn
  D --> E[Attach structured _meta]:::process
  E --> F[Agent error handler]:::process
  F --> G{Recovery strategy}:::decision
  G -->|retry| H[Retry with backoff]:::process
  G -->|escalate| I[Propagate to coordinator]:::warn
  G -->|abort| J[Return user-friendly message]:::success
```

**Edge case — partial success:** Some tools complete part of their work before failing (e.g., a batch write that persists 7 of 10 records before a network drop). In this case, set `isError: true` *and* include partial results in `content`. The `_meta` block should carry `partialSuccess: true` and a `completedCount` field. Agents that ignore this nuance will either retry the entire batch (causing duplicates) or discard the partial result (causing data loss).

sections.heading: 2. Error Category Taxonomy — Four Canonical Classes
sections.body_markdown:
Every error a tool can produce falls into one of four categories. Getting the classification right is the single most important decision in error-response design, because the category determines the agent's recovery strategy.

| Category | Root Cause | Agent Recovery | `isRetryable` Default |
|:---------|:-----------|:---------------|----------------------:|
| **Transient** | Timeout, service unavailability, network blip | Retry with exponential backoff | `true` |
| **Validation** | Invalid input format, missing required field, type mismatch | Fix the request; do not retry as-is | `false` |
| **Business** | Policy violation, quota exceeded, workflow state conflict | Inform user; may require human action | `false` |
| **Permission** | Insufficient scope, expired token, IP allowlist block | Re-authenticate or escalate; do not retry blindly | `false` (usually) |

### 2.1 Transient Errors

Transient errors are *environmental* — the tool's logic is correct, but the infrastructure is temporarily unavailable. Classic examples: upstream HTTP 503, database connection pool exhausted, DNS resolution failure. The defining characteristic is that the *same request*, sent later, is likely to succeed.

```json
{ "errorCategory": "transient", "isRetryable": true, "retryAfterMs": 1500 }
```

### 2.2 Validation Errors

Validation errors are *caller errors* — the agent sent a malformed or semantically invalid request. Retrying without modification is pointless. The agent must either correct the input or surface the error to the user.

```json
{
  "errorCategory": "validation",
  "isRetryable": false,
  "fieldErrors": [
    { "field": "shipDate", "issue": "Must be ISO-8601; received '12/31/2024'" }
  ]
}
```

### 2.3 Business Errors

Business errors occur when the request is technically valid but violates a domain rule — a customer tries to place an order that exceeds their credit limit, or a refund is requested outside the 30-day window. These are *expected* failure modes that the business has explicitly modelled. They are almost never retryable, and they require a customer-friendly explanation.

### 2.4 Permission Errors

Permission errors arise from access-control failures: the calling agent's token lacks the required scope, the resource is owned by a different tenant, or the IP is not on the allowlist. Retrying with the same credentials will always fail. The agent must trigger a re-authentication flow or escalate to a human operator.

**Adjacent topic — HTTP status code mapping:** When your MCP tool wraps an HTTP API, map upstream status codes to these categories consistently:

| HTTP Status | Category |
|:------------|:---------|
| 408, 429, 502, 503, 504 | Transient |
| 400, 422 | Validation |
| 402, 409, 451 | Business |
| 401, 403 | Permission |

This mapping should live in a shared library so every tool in your system classifies errors identically. Inconsistent classification is one of the most common sources of agent misbehaviour in production.

sections.heading: 3. Why Uniform Error Responses Block Recovery
sections.body_markdown:
A uniform error response — one that returns the same generic message regardless of failure type — is an anti-pattern that systematically prevents agents from making correct recovery decisions.

Consider a coordinator agent orchestrating three subagents: `InventoryAgent`, `PricingAgent`, and `ShippingAgent`. Each tool returns:

```json
{ "isError": true, "content": [{ "type": "text", "text": "Operation failed." }] }
```

The coordinator now faces an impossible decision tree:

- Should it retry? (Unknown — could be transient or permanent.)
- Should it fix the request? (Unknown — could be a validation error.)
- Should it inform the user? (Unknown — could be a permission issue requiring re-auth.)
- Should it proceed with partial results? (Unknown — no partial data was returned.)

In practice, agents confronted with uniform errors default to one of two failure modes:

1. **Aggressive retry loops** — the agent retries indefinitely, hammering a service that is down for a non-transient reason (e.g., a permission error), wasting tokens and compute.
2. **Premature abort** — the agent gives up immediately even when a simple retry would have succeeded, degrading user experience unnecessarily.

The information-theoretic argument is straightforward. Let $R$ be the set of possible recovery actions and $E$ be the error event. The agent's ability to select the optimal recovery action $r^* \in R$ is bounded by the mutual information between the error signal and the error cause:

$$I(R; E) = H(E) - H(E \mid R)$$

A uniform error response drives $H(E \mid R) \to H(E)$, collapsing $I(R; E) \to 0$ — the agent learns nothing from the error signal.

**Production tradeoff:** Some teams argue that exposing detailed error metadata leaks internal implementation details (stack traces, internal service names, database schemas). This is a valid security concern, but the solution is *structured sanitisation*, not opacity. Strip internal identifiers; keep the category, retryability, and a safe human-readable description. The agent needs the *class* of failure, not the implementation detail.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[Uniform error response]:::warn --> B[Agent cannot classify failure]:::warn
  B --> C{Guess recovery strategy}:::decision
  C -->|retry blindly| D[Hammers unavailable service]:::warn
  C -->|abort immediately| E[Discards recoverable failure]:::warn
  C -->|escalate always| F[Floods coordinator with noise]:::warn
  G[Structured error response]:::concept --> H[Agent classifies failure]:::process
  H --> I{Apply correct strategy}:::decision
  I -->|transient| J[Retry with backoff]:::success
  I -->|validation| K[Fix request]:::success
  I -->|business| L[Inform user]:::success
  I -->|permission| M[Re-authenticate]:::success
```

sections.heading: 4. Retryable vs Non-Retryable Errors — Metadata That Prevents Wasted Work
sections.body_markdown:
The `isRetryable` boolean is the single most operationally impactful field in a structured error response. It directly controls whether the agent enters a retry loop — and getting it wrong in either direction has measurable costs.

**False positive (marking non-retryable as retryable):** The agent retries a permission error or a business-rule violation. Each retry consumes tokens, latency, and potentially rate-limit quota. In a multi-agent system with $n$ subagents each retrying $k$ times, the wasted call volume is $O(n \cdot k)$ before the coordinator finally gives up.

**False negative (marking retryable as non-retryable):** The agent aborts on a transient failure that would have resolved in 1–2 seconds. The user sees a failure that was never real.

### Retry Metadata Schema

Beyond the boolean, a production-grade error response should carry retry *guidance*:

```json
{
  "isError": true,
  "_meta": {
    "errorCategory": "transient",
    "isRetryable": true,
    "retryAfterMs": 2000,
    "maxRetries": 3,
    "retryStrategy": "exponential-backoff",
    "errorCode": "UPSTREAM_TIMEOUT",
    "description": "The pricing service did not respond within the 3-second SLA window."
  }
}
```

| Field | Type | Purpose |
|:------|:-----|:--------|
| `isRetryable` | boolean | Primary gate: should the agent retry at all? |
| `retryAfterMs` | integer | Minimum wait before first retry (respects upstream rate limits) |
| `maxRetries` | integer | Tool's recommendation for retry ceiling |
| `retryStrategy` | string | `"immediate"`, `"fixed-delay"`, `"exponential-backoff"` |
| `errorCode` | string | Machine-readable code for programmatic branching |
| `description` | string | Human-readable explanation safe to surface in logs |

### Exponential Backoff Formula

When `retryStrategy` is `"exponential-backoff"`, the recommended wait before attempt $i$ is:

$$t_i = \min\left(t_{\text{base}} \cdot 2^{i-1} + \text{jitter},\; t_{\text{max}}\right)$$

where $t_{\text{base}}$ is the `retryAfterMs` value, jitter is a uniform random value in $[0, t_{\text{base}}]$, and $t_{\text{max}}$ is a system-wide cap (commonly 30 000 ms). The jitter term prevents the *thundering herd* problem when many agents retry simultaneously after a shared upstream outage.

### Edge Case — Conditional Retryability

Some errors are retryable only under specific conditions. A rate-limit error (HTTP 429) is retryable *after* the `Retry-After` window, but not retryable if the agent has already exhausted its daily quota. Model this with a `retryCondition` field:

```json
{
  "isRetryable": true,
  "retryCondition": "after_window",
  "retryAfterMs": 60000,
  "quotaResetEpochMs": 1735689600000
}
```

The agent can inspect `quotaResetEpochMs` against the current time to decide whether retrying within the session is feasible or whether it should defer to the next day's quota window.

sections.heading: 5. Business-Rule Violation Flags and Customer-Friendly Explanations
sections.body_markdown:
Business errors deserve special treatment because they sit at the intersection of technical systems and human communication. When a tool rejects a request due to a policy violation, the agent must do two things simultaneously: understand that retrying is futile, and communicate the reason to the user in language that is accurate, empathetic, and actionable.

### The Business Error Schema

```json
{
  "isError": true,
  "_meta": {
    "errorCategory": "business",
    "isRetryable": false,
    "errorCode": "REFUND_WINDOW_EXPIRED",
    "description": "Refund requests must be submitted within 30 days of purchase. This order was placed 47 days ago.",
    "customerMessage": "We're sorry — refunds are available within 30 days of purchase. Your order from 47 days ago is outside this window. Please contact our support team if you have exceptional circumstances.",
    "policyReference": "RETURNS_POLICY_V3",
    "humanEscalationAvailable": true
  }
}
```

Notice the separation of concerns:

- `description` — safe for internal logs and agent reasoning; may include policy codes.
- `customerMessage` — pre-approved, empathetic language the agent can surface verbatim or paraphrase.
- `humanEscalationAvailable` — tells the agent whether to offer a human handoff.

### Why `isRetryable: false` Is Non-Negotiable for Business Errors

A business rule violation is a *deterministic* outcome: the same request will always fail against the same policy. Marking it retryable would cause the agent to loop until it hits a retry ceiling, then surface a confusing timeout error instead of the actual policy explanation. The user loses both time and clarity.

**Production tradeoff — policy versioning:** Business rules change. A refund window that is 30 days today might be 60 days after a policy update. The `policyReference` field allows the agent (or a monitoring system) to detect when a cached policy assumption is stale. If the tool returns `REFUND_WINDOW_EXPIRED` but the agent's policy cache says the window is 60 days, it knows to invalidate the cache and re-fetch policy metadata before surfacing the error.

### Quota Exhaustion — A Hybrid Case

Quota exhaustion (`QUOTA_EXCEEDED`) straddles business and transient categories. It is a business constraint (the customer has used their allocation), but it may become retryable at quota reset. Model it as `errorCategory: "business"` with a conditional retry annotation:

```json
{
  "errorCategory": "business",
  "isRetryable": true,
  "retryCondition": "after_quota_reset",
  "quotaResetEpochMs": 1735689600000,
  "customerMessage": "You've reached your monthly API limit. Your quota resets on January 1st."
}
```

This is one of the few cases where a business error carries `isRetryable: true` — but only with an explicit temporal condition attached.

sections.heading: 6. Local Recovery and Propagation in Subagent Architectures
sections.body_markdown:
In a multi-agent system, the question of *where* to handle an error is as important as *how* to handle it. The guiding principle is **handle errors at the lowest level that has enough context to resolve them**; propagate only what cannot be resolved locally.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[Coordinator Agent]:::concept --> B[InventorySubagent]:::process
  A --> C[PricingSubagent]:::process
  A --> D[ShippingSubagent]:::process
  B --> E{Tool error?}:::decision
  E -->|transient| F[Local retry with backoff]:::process
  F --> G{Resolved?}:::decision
  G -->|yes| H[Return result to coordinator]:::success
  G -->|no after max retries| I[Propagate structured error + partial result]:::warn
  E -->|validation/permission/business| I
  I --> A
```

### What Subagents Should Handle Locally

- **Transient failures** within their tool calls — up to `maxRetries` with backoff.
- **Fallback data sources** — if the primary inventory API times out, try the read replica before escalating.
- **Stale cache invalidation** — if a permission error suggests an expired token, refresh and retry once.

### What Subagents Should Propagate

- Any error that persists after local retry exhaustion.
- Validation and business errors (the coordinator may have context to fix the request).
- Permission errors that require credential refresh at a higher level.
- Partial results alongside the error, so the coordinator can decide whether to proceed with incomplete data.

### The Propagation Envelope

When a subagent propagates an error to the coordinator, it should include a *propagation context* block:

```json
{
  "isError": true,
  "_meta": {
    "errorCategory": "transient",
    "isRetryable": false,
    "propagatedFrom": "InventorySubagent",
    "localRetriesAttempted": 3,
    "lastAttemptTimestampMs": 1735600000000,
    "partialResult": {
      "itemsRetrieved": 142,
      "itemsRequested": 200,
      "missingSkus": ["ABC-99", "DEF-01"]
    },
    "description": "Inventory service remained unavailable after 3 retries over 6 seconds."
  }
}
```

Note that `isRetryable` is now `false` at the propagation level — the subagent has already exhausted local retries. The coordinator should not retry the subagent call immediately; it should either wait for a longer backoff window or proceed with the partial result.

**Architectural judgment — partial result acceptance:** The coordinator must decide whether a partial result is *good enough* to proceed. This is a business decision, not a technical one. A pricing coordinator that has prices for 142 of 200 SKUs might still be able to generate a useful quote for the available items. Encode this threshold as a configuration parameter (`minimumCoverageRatio: 0.8`) rather than hardcoding it in the agent's logic.

**Edge case — cascading failures:** If multiple subagents fail simultaneously (e.g., during a regional outage), the coordinator must avoid the *retry storm* anti-pattern — all subagents retrying in lockstep. Stagger retry schedules using the subagent's index as a jitter seed:

$$t_{\text{jitter},i} = t_{\text{base}} + i \cdot \Delta t$$

where $i$ is the subagent index and $\Delta t$ is a fixed stagger interval (e.g., 500 ms).

sections.heading: 7. Access Failures vs Valid Empty Results — A Critical Distinction
sections.body_markdown:
One of the most consequential and frequently mishandled distinctions in MCP tool design is the difference between an **access failure** and a **valid empty result**. Conflating them causes agents to make incorrect decisions with high confidence.

### The Semantic Difference

| Scenario | Meaning | Correct Response |
|:---------|:--------|:----------------|
| Database query returns zero rows | The query succeeded; no data matches the criteria | `isError: false`, empty result set |
| Database connection refused | The query could not execute | `isError: true`, `errorCategory: "transient"` |
| Search index returns no hits | Valid result — nothing matches | `isError: false`, empty hits array |
| Search service returns 503 | Access failure — result unknown | `isError: true`, `errorCategory: "transient"` |
| Permission denied on resource | Access failure — result unknown | `isError: true`, `errorCategory: "permission"` |

### Why the Distinction Matters

Consider an agent checking whether a customer has any outstanding orders:

- **Empty result (correct):** `{ "orders": [] }` — the agent correctly concludes the customer has no outstanding orders and proceeds to offer new products.
- **Access failure (mishandled as empty):** The order service is down, the tool returns `{ "orders": [] }` anyway — the agent incorrectly concludes the customer has no orders and may cancel a pending shipment or offer a discount that should not apply.

The second scenario is a *silent data corruption* bug. It is far more dangerous than an explicit error because the agent acts with false confidence.

### Implementation Pattern

```python
def get_customer_orders(customer_id: str) -> dict:
    try:
        response = order_service.query(customer_id=customer_id)
        # Valid empty result — query succeeded, no orders found
        return {
            "isError": False,
            "content": [{"type": "text", "text": "Query successful."}],
            "_meta": {
                "resultCount": len(response.orders),
                "queryExecuted": True
            },
            "orders": response.orders  # May be []
        }
    except TimeoutError as e:
        # Access failure — we do NOT know if orders exist
        return {
            "isError": True,
            "content": [{"type": "text", "text": "Order service timed out."}],
            "_meta": {
                "errorCategory": "transient",
                "isRetryable": True,
                "retryAfterMs": 2000,
                "queryExecuted": False  # Explicitly signal: result is unknown
            }
        }
    except PermissionError as e:
        # Access failure — we do NOT know if orders exist
        return {
            "isError": True,
            "content": [{"type": "text", "text": "Insufficient permissions to query orders."}],
            "_meta": {
                "errorCategory": "permission",
                "isRetryable": False,
                "queryExecuted": False
            }
        }
```

The `queryExecuted: false` flag is the key signal. It tells the agent: *do not treat the absence of results as evidence that no results exist*.

### Animated: The Access Failure vs Empty Result Decision Flow

```svg
<svg viewBox="0 0 320 160" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Access failure vs empty result decision flow">
  <!-- Background -->
  <rect width="320" height="160" fill="#0f172a" rx="8"/>
  <!-- Labels -->
  <text x="160" y="18" text-anchor="middle" fill="#94a3b8" font-size="9" font-family="monospace">Tool returns response</text>
  <!-- isError decision diamond -->
  <polygon points="160,28 195,50 160,72 125,50" fill="#334155" stroke="#6366f1" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="160" y="47" text-anchor="middle" fill="#e2e8f0" font-size="8" font-family="monospace">isError?</text>
  <text x="160" y="57" text-anchor="middle" fill="#e2e8f0" font-size="8" font-family="monospace">flag</text>
  <!-- false branch -->
  <line x1="125" y1="50" x2="60" y2="50" stroke="#22c55e" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="90" y="44" text-anchor="middle" fill="#22c55e" font-size="8" font-family="monospace">false</text>
  <rect x="10" y="62" width="100" height="28" rx="4" fill="#14532d" stroke="#22c55e" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="60" y="74" text-anchor="middle" fill="#86efac" font-size="8" font-family="monospace">Valid result</text>
  <text x="60" y="84" text-anchor="middle" fill="#86efac" font-size="8" font-family="monospace">(may be empty [])</text>
  <!-- true branch -->
  <line x1="195" y1="50" x2="260" y2="50" stroke="#f59e0b" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="230" y="44" text-anchor="middle" fill="#f59e0b" font-size="8" font-family="monospace">true</text>
  <rect x="210" y="38" width="100" height="24" rx="4" fill="#451a03" stroke="#f59e0b" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="260" y="48" text-anchor="middle" fill="#fcd34d" font-size="8" font-family="monospace">Access failure</text>
  <text x="260" y="58" text-anchor="middle" fill="#fcd34d" font-size="8" font-family="monospace">result unknown</text>
  <!-- Animated pulse on the true branch box -->
  <rect x="210" y="38" width="100" height="24" rx="4" fill="none" stroke="#f59e0b" stroke-width="2">
    <animate attributeName="stroke-opacity" values="1;0.2;1" dur="2s" repeatCount="indefinite"/>
    <animate attributeName="stroke-width" values="2;4;2" dur="2s" repeatCount="indefinite"/>
  </rect>
  <!-- queryExecuted field -->
  <line x1="260" y1="62" x2="260" y2="90" stroke="#f59e0b" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="3 2"/>
  <rect x="210" y="90" width="100" height="28" rx="4" fill="#1e1b4b" stroke="#6366f1" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="260" y="102" text-anchor="middle" fill="#a5b4fc" font-size="8" font-family="monospace">queryExecuted:</text>
  <text x="260" y="112" text-anchor="middle" fill="#a5b4fc" font-size="8" font-family="monospace">false → retry</text>
  <!-- empty result note -->
  <line x1="60" y1="90" x2="60" y2="110" stroke="#22c55e" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="3 2"/>
  <rect x="10" y="110" width="100" height="28" rx="4" fill="#14532d" stroke="#22c55e" stroke-width="1"/>
```
