A. Stop — you have found the definition and one re-export, which is sufficient.
B. Read `src/adapters/notifier/index.ts` in full to understand the adapter, then stop.
C. Add `notify` to your search set and run `Grep` for both `sendNotification` and `notify` across the codebase.
D. Use `Glob("**/*.ts")` to list all files, then `Read` each one to find `notify`.

**Correct answer: C**

*Explanation:* The wrapper-tracing pattern requires building a **complete alias set** and then searching for all aliases. Stopping at the re-export (options A and B) misses all call sites that use the `notify` alias. Option D is correct in logic but catastrophically inefficient — Glob + Read-all is the anti-pattern that incremental Grep exploration is designed to replace.

---

```svg
<svg viewBox="0 0 320 160" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Animated incremental exploration: Grep finds entry point, Read follows imports">
  <!-- Background -->
  <rect width="320" height="160" fill="#0f172a" rx="10"/>

  <!-- Labels -->
  <text x="30" y="30" font-family="monospace" font-size="11" fill="#94a3b8">Grep</text>
  <text x="130" y="30" font-family="monospace" font-size="11" fill="#94a3b8">Read</text>
  <text x="230" y="30" font-family="monospace" font-size="11" fill="#94a3b8">Grep</text>

  <!-- Nodes -->
  <rect x="10" y="40" width="70" height="36" rx="6" fill="#6366f1"/>
  <text x="45" y="63" font-family="monospace" font-size="10" fill="#fff" text-anchor="middle">find callers</text>

  <rect x="110" y="40" width="70" height="36" rx="6" fill="#22c55e"/>
  <text x="145" y="63" font-family="monospace" font-size="10" fill="#fff" text-anchor="middle">read range</text>

  <rect x="210" y="40" width="70" height="36" rx="6" fill="#6366f1"/>
  <text x="245" y="63" font-family="monospace" font-size="10" fill="#fff" text-anchor="middle">find imports</text>

  <!-- Arrows -->
  <line x1="80" y1="58" x2="110" y2="58" stroke="#f59e0b" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#arr)"/>
  <line x1="180" y1="58" x2="210" y2="58" stroke="#f59e0b" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#arr)"/>

  <defs>
    <marker id="arr" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6" refX="3" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z" fill="#f59e0b"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>

  <!-- Animated token pulse -->
  <circle r="6" fill="#f59e0b" opacity="0.9">
    <animateMotion dur="3s" repeatCount="indefinite"
      path="M 45 58 L 145 58 L 245 58"/>
    <animate attributeName="opacity" values="0;1;1;0" dur="3s" repeatCount="indefinite"/>
  </circle>

  <!-- Bottom label -->
  <text x="160" y="115" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="10" fill="#64748b" text-anchor="middle">Token flow: only relevant lines loaded at each step</text>
  <text x="160" y="130" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="10" fill="#64748b" text-anchor="middle">vs. Read-all: entire codebase in context upfront</text>

  <!-- Cost bar comparison -->
  <rect x="40" y="140" width="240" height="8" rx="3" fill="#1e293b"/>
  <rect x="40" y="140" width="14" height="8" rx="3" fill="#22c55e">
    <animate attributeName="width" values="0;14" dur="1.5s" fill="freeze"/>
  </rect>
  <text x="58" y="148" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="8" fill="#22c55e">incremental</text>
  <rect x="40" y="150" width="240" height="8" rx="3" fill="#1e293b"/>
  <rect x="40" y="150" width="200" height="8" rx="3" fill="#ef4444">
    <animate attributeName="width" values="0;200" dur="1.5s" fill="freeze"/>
  </rect>
  <text x="244" y="158" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="8" fill="#ef4444">read-all</text>
</svg>
```

key_takeaways: Grep searches file **contents** for patterns; Glob matches file **paths** by naming convention — never swap them.
Edit is token-efficient but requires `old_string` to match exactly once; when uniqueness fails, Read the full file and Write it back as a reliable fallback.
Incremental exploration (Grep → Read range → Grep imports → Read range) consumes a small fraction of the tokens that reading all files upfront would require.
Tracing function usage across wrapper modules requires building a complete alias set from re-exports before running the final Grep sweep.
Always Grep for uniqueness before calling Edit on common patterns like `return null;` — include surrounding context lines in `old_string` to engineer uniqueness.
The architectural judgment rule: use discovery tools (Grep, Glob) to constrain scope before invoking inspection or mutation tools (Read, Write, Edit).
further_reading: Bash tool usage patterns: when to shell out vs. use built-in tools for file operations
Context window management strategies for large codebase tasks
Structured output and tool-chaining patterns for multi-step code analysis workflows
AST-based code search tools (tree-sitter, ast-grep) as complements to regex-based Grep for semantic queries

ts-3-1.title: 3.1 Configure CLAUDE.md files with appropriate hierarchy, scoping, and modular organization

lesson.topic: 3.1 Configure CLAUDE.md files with appropriate hierarchy, scoping, and modular organization
lesson.synopsis: CLAUDE.md files form a layered memory system that shapes Claude's behavior across sessions, teams, and monorepo packages. This lesson dissects the full configuration hierarchy—from user-level personal preferences to project-level shared standards to directory-level package rules—and shows how to keep that system modular, auditable, and correctly scoped. Architects will leave with concrete patterns for splitting monolithic files, using @import selectively, and diagnosing hierarchy failures before they silently corrupt team workflows.
learning_objectives: Explain the three-tier CLAUDE.md hierarchy (user, project, directory) and the scoping rules that govern each tier.
Distinguish which configuration belongs in user-level versus project-level files and articulate the version-control implications of that choice.
Apply @import syntax and the .claude/rules/ directory pattern to decompose a monolithic CLAUDE.md into maintainable, topic-specific modules.
Diagnose hierarchy and scoping failures using the /memory command and identify corrective actions for common misconfiguration scenarios.
Evaluate production tradeoffs between monolithic and modular CLAUDE.md architectures for large, multi-package repositories.

sections.heading: 1. The Three-Tier Configuration Hierarchy
sections.body_markdown:
Claude resolves its persistent instructions by merging three tiers of CLAUDE.md files in a well-defined precedence order. Understanding that order is the prerequisite for every other topic in this lesson.

| Tier | Location | Scope | Version-controlled? |
|:-----|:---------|:------|:--------------------:|
| User-level | `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` | That user on any project | No |
| Project-level | `<repo-root>/.claude/CLAUDE.md` or `<repo-root>/CLAUDE.md` | All contributors to the repo | Yes |
| Directory-level | `<any-subdir>/CLAUDE.md` | Claude sessions rooted at that subdirectory | Yes |

**Resolution order:** When Claude starts a session in a given working directory, it loads the user-level file first, then walks up from the current directory to the repo root collecting every CLAUDE.md it encounters, and finally merges them. Instructions from more-specific (deeper) files take precedence over more-general ones when they conflict.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  U["~/.claude/CLAUDE.md\n(user-level)"]:::concept
  P[".claude/CLAUDE.md\n(project-level)"]:::process
  D["packages/api/CLAUDE.md\n(directory-level)"]:::process
  M["Merged instruction set\ndelivered to Claude"]:::success

  U --> M
  P --> M
  D --> M
```

**Key architectural insight:** The hierarchy is *additive by default*. Lower tiers do not replace upper tiers; they append to them. A directory-level file that says "always use tabs" does not erase a project-level file that says "always use spaces"—both instructions reach Claude, and the conflict must be resolved explicitly (e.g., by overriding in the more-specific file with a clear directive like "Override project default: use tabs in this package").

**Edge case — dual project-level locations:** Claude recognizes both `<root>/CLAUDE.md` and `<root>/.claude/CLAUDE.md`. If both exist, both are loaded. This is a common source of duplicate or contradictory instructions. The canonical recommendation is to pick one location per project and document it in the contributing guide.

**Connection to adjacent topics:** The hierarchy mirrors how many build systems (Gradle, Bazel) resolve configuration: a global defaults file, a project-level override, and a module-level override. Architects familiar with those systems will find the mental model transferable.

sections.heading: 2. User-Level Settings: Personal, Not Shared
sections.body_markdown:
The user-level file at `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` is the right place for preferences that are personal to a developer and should never be imposed on teammates. Because this file lives outside any repository, it is never committed to version control and never propagates through `git clone`.

**What belongs here:**
- Personal coding style preferences (preferred variable naming conventions, comment verbosity)
- Editor-specific instructions ("always emit file paths relative to the workspace root so my IDE can jump to them")
- Persona or tone preferences ("respond concisely; I prefer bullet points over prose")
- API keys or environment-specific paths that differ per machine
- Experimental instructions the developer is testing before proposing to the team

**What does NOT belong here:**
- Team coding standards
- Security policies (e.g., "never log PII")
- Architecture decisions that all contributors must follow
- CI/CD conventions

**The silent-failure trap:** The most dangerous misconfiguration pattern is placing team-wide instructions in `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`. The original author's Claude sessions work perfectly. A new team member clones the repo, opens Claude, and receives none of those instructions—silently. There is no error message. The new member's Claude may generate code that violates the team's standards, and the root cause is invisible without deliberate diagnosis.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  A["Senior dev writes\nAPI conventions\nin ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md"]:::warn
  B["New hire clones repo"]:::process
  C{"~/.claude/CLAUDE.md\nexists on new machine?"}:::decision
  D["Instructions missing\nsilently"]:::warn
  E["Claude generates\nnon-compliant code"]:::warn
  F["Move instructions to\n.claude/CLAUDE.md\nin repo"]:::success

  A --> B --> C
  C -->|No| D --> E
  C -->|Yes| F
```

**Production tradeoff:** Some teams deliberately keep sensitive instructions (internal system names, proprietary algorithm hints) out of version control by placing them in user-level files. This is a valid security tradeoff, but it must be documented explicitly—e.g., a `CONTRIBUTING.md` section titled "Claude configuration" that lists which instructions each developer must add to their personal file.

**Exam-relevant nuance:** User-level instructions are loaded *before* project-level instructions. If a user-level file says "always respond in French" and the project-level file says "always respond in English," the project-level instruction wins because it is more specific and loaded later in the merge sequence.

sections.heading: 3. @import for Modularity
sections.body_markdown:
@import is the mechanism that prevents CLAUDE.md files from becoming unmanageable walls of text. It lets any CLAUDE.md reference an external Markdown file, whose contents are inlined at load time.

**Syntax:**
```markdown
@import ./standards/python-style.md
@import .claude/rules/testing.md
@import ../shared/security-policy.md
```

Paths are resolved relative to the file containing the @import directive. Absolute paths are not supported; this keeps the configuration portable across machines.

**Why this matters architecturally:** A monorepo with 12 packages might have a single authoritative `python-style.md` maintained by the platform team. Without @import, every package's CLAUDE.md would either duplicate that content (drift risk) or omit it (compliance risk). With @import, each package's CLAUDE.md contains a single line that always reflects the current canonical version.

**Worked example — shared security policy:**

```
repo-root/
├── .claude/
│   ├── CLAUDE.md          ← project-level entry point
│   └── rules/
│       ├── security.md    ← maintained by security team
│       ├── testing.md
│       └── api-conventions.md
├── packages/
│   ├── api/
│   │   └── CLAUDE.md      ← imports security + api-conventions
│   └── worker/
│       └── CLAUDE.md      ← imports security + testing
```

`packages/api/CLAUDE.md`:
```markdown
# API Package — Claude Instructions

@import ../../.claude/rules/security.md
@import ../../.claude/rules/api-conventions.md

## Package-specific notes
- This package owns the `/v2/` route namespace.
- Prefer streaming responses for payloads > 1 MB.
```

**Edge cases:**
- **Circular imports** are not detected at authoring time; they cause a load error at session start. Use `/memory` (covered in Section 7) to detect them.
- **Missing files** referenced by @import produce a silent no-op in some Claude versions and a warning in others. Always verify with `/memory` after adding a new import.
- **Deeply nested imports** (A imports B imports C) are supported but make the effective instruction set hard to reason about. Limit nesting to two levels.

**Connection to adjacent topics:** @import is conceptually identical to `#include` in C or `@use` in Sass. The same discipline applies: avoid importing the same file from multiple places in the same chain, and keep imported files focused on a single concern.

sections.heading: 4. The .claude/rules/ Directory Pattern
sections.body_markdown:
The `.claude/rules/` directory is the recommended home for topic-specific rule files that are imported by one or more CLAUDE.md files. It provides a discoverable, version-controlled library of reusable instruction modules.

**Canonical structure:**
```
.claude/
├── CLAUDE.md              ← project entry point; imports from rules/
└── rules/
    ├── testing.md
    ├── api-conventions.md
    ├── deployment.md
    ├── security.md
    └── data-models.md
```

**Why a dedicated directory instead of inline content?**

| Approach | Maintainability | Discoverability | Ownership |
|:---------|:---------------:|:---------------:|:---------:|
| Monolithic CLAUDE.md | Low — one large file, merge conflicts | Low — no structure | Unclear |
| Inline per-package CLAUDE.md | Medium — duplication across packages | Medium | Per-package team |
| `.claude/rules/` + @import | High — single source of truth | High — named files | Per-topic team |

**Splitting a monolithic file — step-by-step:**

1. Audit the existing CLAUDE.md and tag each paragraph with a topic label (testing, security, style, deployment, etc.).
2. Create `.claude/rules/<topic>.md` for each label.
3. Move the tagged paragraphs into the corresponding file.
4. Replace the moved content in the original CLAUDE.md with `@import .claude/rules/<topic>.md`.
5. Run `/memory` to verify all files load correctly.
6. Assign a CODEOWNERS entry for each rules file to the team that owns that domain.

**Example — before and after:**

*Before (monolithic):*
```markdown
# Project Claude Instructions

## Testing
Always write unit tests before implementation (TDD).
Use pytest for Python, Jest for TypeScript.
Minimum coverage: 80%.

## API Conventions
All endpoints must return JSON:API-compliant envelopes.
Version all routes under /vN/.

## Deployment
Never deploy on Fridays.
All deploys require a JIRA ticket in the commit message.
```

*After (modular):*
```markdown
# Project Claude Instructions

@import .claude/rules/testing.md
@import .claude/rules/api-conventions.md
@import .claude/rules/deployment.md
```

**Production tradeoff:** The `.claude/rules/` pattern adds a layer of indirection. A developer reading a package's CLAUDE.md must follow imports to understand the full instruction set. Mitigate this with a `README.md` inside `.claude/rules/` that lists each file and its owning team.

**Edge case — rules files that import other rules files:** This is valid but creates a dependency graph that must be manually tracked. A `deployment.md` that imports `security.md` means every package importing `deployment.md` also gets `security.md`—which may or may not be intended. Document transitive imports explicitly.

sections.heading: 5. Selective @import per Package
sections.body_markdown:
Not every package in a monorepo needs every rule. Selective @import is the pattern where each package's CLAUDE.md imports only the rules relevant to its domain, based on the maintainer's judgment.

**The principle:** A data-pipeline package has no need for API routing conventions. A frontend package has no need for database migration rules. Importing irrelevant rules wastes context window space and can confuse Claude with contradictory or inapplicable instructions.

**Worked scenario — e-commerce monorepo:**

```
packages/
├── storefront/       ← React SPA
├── api-gateway/      ← Node.js REST API
├── order-worker/     ← Python background worker
└── data-pipeline/    ← Python ETL
```

`packages/storefront/CLAUDE.md`:
```markdown
@import ../../.claude/rules/security.md
@import ../../.claude/rules/frontend-style.md
@import ../../.claude/rules/testing.md

## Storefront-specific
- Use React Query for all server state.
- Tailwind utility classes only; no custom CSS files.
```

`packages/data-pipeline/CLAUDE.md`:
```markdown
@import ../../.claude/rules/security.md
@import ../../.claude/rules/data-models.md
@import ../../.claude/rules/testing.md

## Data pipeline-specific
- All transforms must be idempotent.
- Log row counts at each stage for observability.
```

Note that `api-conventions.md` is imported by `api-gateway` but not by `storefront` or `data-pipeline`. `frontend-style.md` is imported only by `storefront`.

**Architectural judgment — when to share vs. isolate:**

| Rule type | Share universally | Share selectively | Keep isolated |
|:----------|:-----------------:|:-----------------:|:-------------:|
| Security policies | ✓ | | |
| Testing standards | ✓ | | |
| Language style guides | | ✓ (by language) | |
| Domain conventions | | ✓ (by domain) | |
| Package-specific quirks | | | ✓ |

**Edge case — a rule that is almost universal:** If 11 of 12 packages need a rule, it is tempting to put it in the project-level CLAUDE.md and let the one exception override it. This works, but the override must be explicit and clearly commented. Implicit overrides (the exception simply doesn't import the rule) are safer because they don't require Claude to reconcile conflicting instructions.

**Context window economics:** Each imported file consumes tokens from Claude's context window. In a session with a large codebase already loaded, an unnecessarily large CLAUDE.md can crowd out actual code context. Selective import is therefore not just an organizational preference—it has a measurable effect on response quality for complex tasks.

sections.heading: 6. Diagnosing Hierarchy Issues
sections.body_markdown:
Configuration hierarchy failures are silent by default. Claude does not emit a warning when an expected CLAUDE.md is missing or when an @import target does not exist. Architects must build active diagnostic habits.

**The /memory command:**
Running `/memory` inside a Claude session prints the list of all memory files currently loaded, their resolved paths, and (in recent versions) the effective merged instruction set. This is the primary diagnostic tool.

```
/memory

> Loaded memory files:
>   1. /home/alice/.claude/CLAUDE.md  (user-level)
>   2. /repo/.claude/CLAUDE.md        (project-level)
>      └── @import .claude/rules/security.md  ✓
>      └── @import .claude/rules/testing.md   ✓
>   3. /repo/packages/api/CLAUDE.md   (directory-level)
>      └── @import ../../.claude/rules/api-conventions.md  ✓
```

**Common failure patterns and their diagnoses:**

| Symptom | Likely cause | Diagnostic step | Fix |
|:--------|:-------------|:----------------|:----|
| New team member's Claude ignores team standards | Standards in `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`, not in repo | Ask member to run `/memory`; compare with senior dev's output | Move instructions to `.claude/CLAUDE.md` |
| Package-level rules not applied | Working directory set to repo root, not package dir | Check `/memory` for directory-level file | Start Claude session from package directory |
| @import file not loaded | Relative path wrong or file missing | `/memory` shows import with ✗ or absent | Fix path; verify file exists |
| Duplicate instructions causing contradictions | Both `CLAUDE.md` and `.claude/CLAUDE.md` exist at root | `/memory` lists both | Remove one; consolidate |
| Rules applied to wrong package | Transitive import from a shared rules file | Trace import chain in `/memory` output | Restructure imports to be explicit |

**Realistic scenario — the onboarding failure:**

A senior architect at a fintech company has spent weeks tuning Claude's behavior for their payments API. All the critical instructions—PCI-DSS compliance reminders, never-log-card-numbers, always-use-parameterized-queries—live in her `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`. She onboards a junior developer. On day three, the junior's Claude generates a logging statement that includes raw card data. A code review catches it, but the team is shaken.

Diagnosis: The junior runs `/memory` and sees only the user-level file (his own, nearly empty) and the project-level file (which contains only style preferences). The security instructions are invisible to him.

Fix: The architect moves all compliance-critical instructions to `.claude/rules/security.md` and adds `@import .claude/rules/security.md` to the project-level `.claude/CLAUDE.md`. She documents in `CONTRIBUTING.md` that personal preferences (tone, verbosity) belong in `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`, while compliance rules belong in the repo. She adds a CI check that verifies `.claude/rules/security.md` has not been deleted.

**Proactive hygiene:** Add `/memory` verification to your team's onboarding checklist. A new developer should run `/memory` on day one and compare the output against a documented baseline. Any discrepancy is a configuration bug, not a user error.

sections.heading: 7. /memory Command Diagnostics in Depth
sections.body_markdown:
The `/memory` command is the single most important operational tool for CLAUDE.md architecture. This section covers its output format, what to look for, and how to use it to maintain consistency across sessions.

**What /memory reveals:**
- The ordered list of loaded memory files with their absolute paths
- Which @import directives resolved successfully (✓) and which failed (✗ or absent)
- The effective merged instruction set (in some Claude versions, accessible via `/memory --verbose` or equivalent)
- Whether user-level, project-level, and directory-level tiers are all represented

**Interpreting the output — annotated example:**

```
/memory

Loaded memory files (in merge order):

[1] USER   /home/bob/.claude/CLAUDE.md
    - "Prefer concise responses"
    - "Use British English spelling"

[2] PROJECT /repo/.claude/CLAUDE.md
    @import .claude/rules/security.md        ✓  (42 lines)
    @import .claude/rules/testing.md         ✓  (31 lines)
    @import .claude/rules/api-conventions.md ✗  FILE NOT FOUND

[3] DIR    /repo/packages/api/CLAUDE.md
    - "This package owns /v2/ routes"
    - "Prefer streaming for payloads > 1 MB"
```

The ✗ on `api-conventions.md` is immediately actionable: the file was renamed or moved, and the import path was not updated.

**Diagnosing inconsistent behavior across sessions:**

Inconsistent behavior—Claude following a rule in one session but not another—almost always traces to one of three causes:

1. **Working directory differs:** A session started from the repo root does not load `packages/api/CLAUDE.md`. A session started from `packages/api/` does. The `/memory` output will differ between the two.

2. **User-level file differs across machines:** A developer working on two laptops with different `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` files will see different behavior. `/memory` output from each machine will show different `[USER]` entries.

3. **File was modified between sessions:** An @import target was edited, adding or removing instructions. The session that ran before the edit and the session that ran after will behave differently. Git blame on the rules file will confirm this.

**Animated visualization — the merge sequence:**

```svg
<svg viewBox="0 0 320 160" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="CLAUDE.md merge sequence animation">
  <!-- Background -->
  <rect width="320" height="160" fill="#0f172a"/>
  
  <!-- Labels -->
  <text x="30" y="20" font-size="10" fill="#94a3b8" font-family="monospace">~/.claude/CLAUDE.md</text>
  <text x="30" y="70" font-size="10" fill="#94a3b8" font-family="monospace">.claude/CLAUDE.md</text>
  <text x="30" y="120" font-size="10" fill="#94a3b8" font-family="monospace">packages/api/CLAUDE.md</text>

  <!-- File boxes -->
  <rect x="160" y="8" width="60" height="18" rx="3" fill="#6366f1" opacity="0">
    <animate attributeName="opacity" values="0;1;1;1" keyTimes="0;0.2;0.8;1" dur="4s" repeatCount="indefinite"/>
  </rect>
  <rect x="160" y="58" width="60" height="18" rx="3" fill="#22c55e" opacity="0">
    <animate attributeName="opacity" values="0;0;1;1" keyTimes="0;0.35;0.55;1" dur="4s" repeatCount="indefinite"/>
  </rect>
  <rect x="160" y="108" width="60" height="18" rx="3" fill="#f59e0b" opacity="0">
    <animate attributeName="opacity" values="0;0;0;1" keyTimes="0;0.5;0.7;1" dur="4s" repeatCount="indefinite"/>
  </rect>

  <!-- Arrows to merged -->
  <line x1="220" y1="17" x2="265" y2="75" stroke="#6366f1" stroke-width="1.5" opacity="0">
    <animate attributeName="opacity" values="0;0;1;1" keyTimes="0;0.4;0.6;1" dur="4s" repeatCount="indefinite"/>
  </line>
  <line x1="220" y1="67" x2="265" y2="78" stroke="#22c55e" stroke-width="1.5" opacity="0">
    <animate attributeName="opacity" values="0;0;1;1" keyTimes="0;0.5;0.7;1" dur="4s" repeatCount="indefinite"/>
  </line>
  <line x1="220" y1="117" x2="265" y2="82" stroke="#f59e0b" stroke-width="1.5" opacity="0">
    <animate attributeName="opacity" values="0;0;0;1" keyTimes="0;0.65;0.85;1" dur="4s" repeatCount="indefinite"/>
  </line>

  <!-- Merged result box -->
  <rect x="265" y="60" width="45" height="30" rx="4" fill="#334155" opacity="0">
    <animate attributeName="opacity" values="0;0;0;1" keyTimes="0;0.7;0.85;1" dur="4s" repeatCount="indefinite"/>
  </rect>
  <text x="287" y="79" font-size="8" fill="#e2e8f0" text-anchor="middle" opacity="0">
    <animate attributeName="opacity" values="0;0;0;1" keyTimes="0;0.7;0.85;1" dur="4s" repeatCount="indefinite"/>
    Merged
  </text>

  <!-- File text labels inside boxes -->
  <text x="190" y="21" font-size="8" fill="#e2e8f0" text-anchor="middle" opacity="0">
    <animate attributeName="opacity" values="0;1;1;1" keyTimes="0;0.2;0.8;1" dur="4s" repeatCount="indefinite"/>
    USER
  </text>
  <text x="190" y="71" font-size="8" fill="#e2e8f0" text-anchor="middle" opacity="0">
    <animate attributeName="opacity" values="0;0;1;1" keyTimes="0;0.35;0.55;1" dur="4s" repeatCount="indefinite"/>
    PROJECT
  </text>
  <text x="190" y="121" font-size="8" fill="#e2e8f0" text-anchor="middle" opacity="0">
    <animate attributeName="opacity" values="0;0;0;1" keyTimes="0;0.5;0.7;1" dur="4s" repeatCount="indefinite"/>
    DIR
  </text>
</svg>
```

**Operational checklist for /memory:**
- [ ] All three tiers (user, project, directory) appear in output
- [ ] All @import directives show ✓
- [ ] No unexpected files are loaded (e.g., a stale CLAUDE.md from a deleted branch)
- [ ] The directory-level file matches the package you intend to work in
- [ ] Output matches the documented baseline for your team

sections.heading: 8. Production Tradeoffs, Scenario, and Exam Questions
sections.body_markdown:
## Architectural Tradeoffs Summary

Choosing a CLAUDE.md architecture is a real engineering decision with measurable consequences for team velocity, compliance, and maintainability.

| Architecture | Best for | Risk |
|:-------------|:---------|:-----|
| Single root CLAUDE.md | Small teams, single-package repos | Grows unwieldy; merge conflicts |
| Per-package CLAUDE.md (no imports) | Fully independent packages | Duplication; drift between packages |
| `.claude/rules/` + selective @import | Monorepos, multi-team orgs | Indirection; requires import discipline |
| User-level only | Solo developers | Zero team benefit; onboarding failures |

## Realistic Production Scenario

**Organization:** A 60-engineer fintech company running a Python/TypeScript monorepo with 8 packages (auth, payments, reporting, notifications, data-pipeline, admin-ui, mobile-api, internal-tools).

**Problem:** The platform team notices that Claude generates inconsistent code across packages—some use the approved error-handling pattern, others invent their own. Investigation reveals:
- The error-handling standard was written by the lead architect and lives in her `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`.
- The project-level `.claude/CLAUDE.md` contains only a greeting and a link to the wiki.
- Three packages have no CLAUDE.md at all.
- Two packages have CLAUDE.md files that duplicate 200 lines of shared content.

**Remediation plan:**
1. Create `.claude/rules/` with files: `error-handling.md`, `security.md`, `testing.md`, `typescript-style.md`, `python-style.md`, `api-conventions.md`, `deployment.md`.
2. Migrate the architect's personal instructions: compliance-critical ones go to `security.md`; personal tone preferences stay in her `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`.
3. Update the project-level `.claude/CLAUDE.md` to import `security.md` and `testing.md` universally.
4. Create or update each package's CLAUDE.md to import only domain-relevant rules.
5. Add CODEOWNERS entries: security team owns `security.md`; platform team owns `testing.md` and `deployment.md`; each domain team owns their package's CLAUDE.md.
6. Add onboarding step: new developers run `/memory` from each package directory and compare against a documented baseline.
7. Add a CI lint step that checks for @import targets that do not exist.

**Outcome:** After one sprint, `/memory` output is consistent across all developer machines for each package. The error-handling pattern appears in every session. The architect's personal preferences remain personal.

---

## Exam-Style Questions

### Question 1

A new engineer joins a team and reports that Claude is not following the team's API versioning conventions. The senior engineer's Claude sessions follow them correctly. Which is the MOST LIKELY root cause?

**A.** The new engineer's Claude version is outdated and does not support CLAUDE.md.

**B.** The API versioning conventions are defined in the senior engineer's `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` and are not in the repository.

**C.** The new engineer started their Claude session from the wrong directory.

**D.** The project-level CLAUDE.md has a syntax error that prevents loading.

**Correct answer: B**

*Explanation:* User-level files are not version-controlled and do not propagate to other developers. This is the canonical "silent onboarding failure" pattern. Option C is possible but would affect the senior engineer too if they also started from the wrong directory. Option D would affect both engineers equally. Option A is a distractor; CLAUDE.md support is not version-dependent in this way.*

---

### Question 2

A monorepo has the following structure. Which @import path, written inside `packages/payments/CLAUDE.md`, correctly references `.claude/rules/security.md` at the repo root?

```
repo-root/
├── .claude/
│   └── rules/
│       └── security.md
└── packages/
    └── payments/
        └── CLAUDE.md
```

**A.** `@import .claude/rules/security.md`

**B.** `@import ../../.claude/rules/security.md`

**C.** `@import /repo-root/.claude/rules/security.md`

**D.** `@import ~./claude/rules/security.md`

**Correct answer: B**

*Explanation:* @import paths are resolved relative to the file containing the directive. `packages/payments/CLAUDE.md` is two directories below the repo root, so `../../` navigates to the root, then `.claude/rules/security.md` completes the path. Option A would look for `.claude/rules/security.md` relative to `packages/payments/`, which does not exist. Option C uses an absolute path, which is not supported. Option D is syntactically invalid.*

---

### Question 3

An architect wants to verify that a Claude session in `packages/api/` is loading the correct set of memory files and that all @import directives resolved successfully. What is the correct approach?

**A.** Open the `.claude/CLAUDE.md` file in a text editor and manually trace all @import chains.

**B.** Run `/memory` inside the Claude session started from the `packages/api/` directory and inspect the output for loaded files and import resolution status.

**C.** Delete all CLAUDE.md files and recreate them to ensure a clean state.

**D.** Check the Claude application logs in `~/.claude/logs/` for import errors.

**Correct answer: B**

*Explanation:* `/memory` is the purpose-built diagnostic command that shows the exact set of loaded files, their merge order, and the resolution status of each @import directive. Option A is error-prone for complex import chains and does not reflect runtime behavior. Option C is destructive and unnecessary. Option D may provide some information but is not the canonical diagnostic tool and may not exist in all environments.*

key_takeaways: The CLAUDE.md hierarchy has three tiers—user (~/.claude/CLAUDE.md), project (.claude/CLAUDE.md), and directory (subdirectory CLAUDE.md)—merged additively in that order, with more-specific files taking precedence on conflicts.
User-level files are never version-controlled; placing team-wide instructions there causes silent onboarding failures where new developers receive none of those instructions.
@import resolves paths relative to the containing file and inlines external Markdown at load time, enabling a single authoritative source for shared rules across packages.
The .claude/rules/ directory pattern organizes topic-specific files (testing.md, security.md, api-conventions.md) that are selectively imported by each package's CLAUDE.md based on domain relevance.
Selective @import is both an organizational discipline and a context-window optimization: irrelevant rules waste tokens and can introduce contradictory instructions.

The /memory command is the primary diagnostic tool for hierarchy issues—run it from the intended working directory and verify that all three tiers appear and all @import directives show successful resolution.
further_reading: Explore how Claude's context window budget interacts with large CLAUDE.md configurations and strategies for measuring token consumption per session.
Study CODEOWNERS file patterns and how they can enforce ownership of .claude/rules/ files in GitHub and GitLab workflows.
Investigate CI/CD integration patterns for validating CLAUDE.md structure—linting @import paths, checking for missing files, and enforcing required rules files per package.
Review the broader Claude memory architecture: the distinction between CLAUDE.md persistent memory, in-context memory, and external memory tools (e.g., vector stores) and when each is appropriate.

ts-3-2.title: 3.2 Create and configure custom slash commands and skills

lesson.topic: 3.2 Create and configure custom slash commands and skills
lesson.synopsis: This lesson provides an expert-level treatment of Claude's extensibility model through custom slash commands and skills, covering the full spectrum from project-scoped team commands to personal user variants. You will learn how SKILL.md frontmatter options — `context: fork`, `allowed-tools`, and `argument-hint` — give architects precise control over isolation, safety, and developer experience. The lesson closes with a principled framework for choosing between skills and CLAUDE.md, grounded in production tradeoffs and architectural judgment.
learning_objectives: Distinguish project-scoped from user-scoped commands and skills, and explain the version-control and collaboration implications of each.
Configure SKILL.md frontmatter fields — context, allowed-tools, and argument-hint — to enforce isolation, restrict tool access, and improve invocation ergonomics.
Apply context: fork strategically to prevent verbose or exploratory skill output from polluting the main conversation context.
Design personal skill variants that customise team skills without affecting teammates' workflows.
Justify the architectural choice between skills and CLAUDE.md entries based on invocation frequency, context budget, and team governance requirements.

sections.heading: 1. The Two-Tier Command Model: Project vs User Scope
sections.body_markdown:
Claude's slash-command system is split into two distinct scopes, and choosing the wrong one is one of the most common architectural mistakes teams make.

| Attribute | Project commands | User commands |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Location** | `.claude/commands/` (repo root) | `~/.claude/commands/` (home dir) |
| **Shared via** | Version control (git) | Local machine only |
| **Audience** | Entire team | Individual developer |
| **Lifecycle** | Tied to repo branch | Tied to developer workstation |
| **Override risk** | Affects all consumers | Isolated to one user |
| **Typical use** | Standardised workflows, CI-adjacent tasks | Personal shortcuts, experimental prompts |

**Project commands** live inside `.claude/commands/` at the repository root. Because this directory is committed to version control, every developer who clones the repo immediately has access to the same set of commands. This makes project commands the right vehicle for team-wide standards: a `/review` command that enforces your PR checklist, a `/migrate` command that follows your database migration conventions, or a `/release-notes` command that formats changelogs to your style guide.

**User commands** live in `~/.claude/commands/` on the developer's local machine. They are never committed to any repository and are invisible to teammates. This scope is ideal for personal productivity shortcuts, experimental prompts under development, or commands that reference local paths that would not exist on another machine.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  A[Developer invokes /command]:::concept --> B{Where is it defined?}:::decision
  B -->|.claude/commands/| C[Project scope\nShared via git]:::success
  B -->|~/.claude/commands/| D[User scope\nLocal only]:::process
  C --> E[All teammates see it]:::success
  D --> F[Only this developer sees it]:::warn
```

**Edge case — name collision:** If a user command and a project command share the same name, the resolution order matters. User-scoped commands take precedence over project-scoped commands. This is intentional — it allows individuals to override team defaults — but it can cause subtle divergence if a developer forgets they have a local override. Document this precedence rule in your team's onboarding guide.

**Production tradeoff:** Keeping commands in `.claude/commands/` means they are subject to code review, just like any other file. This is a feature, not a limitation. Treat command files as first-class code artifacts: review them for prompt injection risks, validate that they do not leak secrets, and version them with the same rigour as your application code.

sections.heading: 2. Skills and SKILL.md Frontmatter: The Configuration Contract
sections.body_markdown:
Skills are a more structured form of reusable Claude behaviour than plain slash commands. A skill lives in `.claude/skills/<skill-name>/` and is anchored by a `SKILL.md` file. The `SKILL.md` file serves two roles simultaneously: it is the prompt body that Claude reads, and it is a configuration manifest expressed through YAML frontmatter at the top of the file.

```markdown
---
context: fork
allowed-tools:
  - Write
  - Edit
argument-hint: "Target module path (e.g. src/payments/processor.ts)"
---

# Refactor Module

You are refactoring the module at `{{args}}`. Apply the project's
standard patterns: extract pure functions, add JSDoc, enforce
immutability on data structures. Do not change public API signatures.
```

The three frontmatter keys form a configuration contract between the skill author and the runtime:

| Key | Type | Purpose |
|:---|:---|:---|
| `context` | string (`fork` or omitted) | Controls whether the skill runs in an isolated sub-agent context |
| `allowed-tools` | list of strings | Restricts which Claude tools are available during skill execution |
| `argument-hint` | string | Displayed to the developer when they invoke the skill without arguments |

**Architectural note:** Frontmatter is evaluated before the skill body is executed. This means the runtime enforces `allowed-tools` restrictions before Claude even reads the prompt — it is a hard constraint, not a soft instruction. This distinction matters for security-sensitive workflows.

**Skills directory layout:**

```
.claude/
  commands/
    review.md          ← project slash command
    release-notes.md
  skills/
    refactor-module/
      SKILL.md         ← skill with frontmatter
    analyse-codebase/
      SKILL.md
    generate-tests/
      SKILL.md
```

Skills are invoked differently from slash commands. A slash command is a direct prompt shortcut; a skill is a named, configurable workflow that Claude can be directed to execute. The distinction is subtle but important for exam purposes: **slash commands are invocation shortcuts; skills are configurable, parameterised workflows with runtime constraints.**

sections.heading: 3. context: fork — Isolation as an Architectural Primitive
sections.body_markdown:
`context: fork` is the most architecturally significant frontmatter option. When set, the skill executes in an isolated sub-agent context — a forked copy of the conversation state — rather than in the main session. The sub-agent can read, reason, and act, but its outputs do not flow back into the main conversation's context window.

```svg
<svg viewBox="0 0 320 160" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="context fork isolation diagram showing main session and forked sub-agent">
  <!-- Main session lane -->
  <rect x="10" y="10" width="130" height="140" rx="8" fill="#1e293b" stroke="#6366f1" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="75" y="30" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#a5b4fc" font-family="monospace">Main Session</text>
  <rect x="22" y="40" width="106" height="22" rx="4" fill="#334155"/>
  <text x="75" y="55" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#e2e8f0" font-family="monospace">context window</text>
  <rect x="22" y="70" width="106" height="22" rx="4" fill="#334155"/>
  <text x="75" y="85" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#e2e8f0" font-family="monospace">conversation history</text>
  <rect x="22" y="100" width="106" height="22" rx="4" fill="#334155"/>
  <text x="75" y="115" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#e2e8f0" font-family="monospace">tool results</text>
  <!-- Arrow -->
  <path d="M 140 75 L 175 75" stroke="#f59e0b" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#arr)"/>
  <text x="157" y="68" text-anchor="middle" font-size="8" fill="#f59e0b" font-family="monospace">fork</text>
  <defs>
    <marker id="arr" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="8" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z" fill="#f59e0b"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
  <!-- Forked sub-agent lane -->
  <rect x="178" y="10" width="130" height="140" rx="8" fill="#1e293b" stroke="#22c55e" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="5 3"/>
  <text x="243" y="30" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#86efac" font-family="monospace">Forked Sub-Agent</text>
  <rect x="190" y="40" width="106" height="22" rx="4" fill="#334155"/>
  <text x="243" y="55" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#e2e8f0" font-family="monospace">verbose analysis</text>
  <rect x="190" y="70" width="106" height="22" rx="4" fill="#334155"/>
  <text x="243" y="85" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#e2e8f0" font-family="monospace">exploratory output</text>
  <rect x="190" y="100" width="106" height="22" rx="4" fill="#22c55e" opacity="0.3"/>
  <text x="243" y="115" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#86efac" font-family="monospace">summary returned</text>
  <!-- Return arrow -->
  <path d="M 178 130 L 140 130" stroke="#22c55e" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="3 2" marker-end="url(#arr2)"/>
  <defs>
    <marker id="arr2" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="8" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z" fill="#22c55e"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
  <text x="157" y="145" text-anchor="middle" font-size="8" fill="#22c55e" font-family="monospace">summary only</text>
</svg>
```

**Why isolation matters:** Claude's context window is a finite, shared resource. Every token consumed by a skill's intermediate reasoning, tool call results, and exploratory output is a token unavailable for the rest of the session. For skills that perform deep codebase analysis, generate multiple candidate implementations, or explore branching decision trees, the context cost without isolation can be prohibitive.

**Two canonical use cases for `context: fork`:**

1. **Verbose analysis skills** — A skill that reads 40 source files, generates a dependency graph, and produces a 3,000-token architectural report. Without forking, all intermediate tool results and reasoning stay in the main context. With forking, only the final report summary is returned.

2. **Exploratory/brainstorming skills** — A skill that generates five alternative API designs and evaluates each against a rubric. The exploration is valuable, but the intermediate candidates are noise once a recommendation is made. Forking keeps the main session clean.

**Edge case — fork depth:** Forked sub-agents can themselves invoke skills, but nesting forks more than one level deep creates debugging complexity and can obscure which context is authoritative. As an architectural rule, limit fork depth to one level in production workflows.

**Production tradeoff:** `context: fork` adds latency because the runtime must spin up a sub-agent, execute the skill, and then marshal the result back. For fast, low-output skills (e.g., a skill that writes a single config file), the overhead is not justified. Reserve forking for skills where the context pollution cost exceeds the latency cost — typically skills that consume more than ~500 tokens of intermediate output.

sections.heading: 4. Restricting Tool Access and Prompting for Arguments
sections.body_markdown:
Two frontmatter keys — `allowed-tools` and `argument-hint` — address orthogonal concerns: safety and usability. Together they define the operational envelope of a skill.

## allowed-tools: Least-Privilege Tool Access

By default, a skill inherits the full tool set available in the current session. `allowed-tools` overrides this by specifying an explicit allowlist. Claude will only use the listed tools during skill execution, regardless of what the session permits.

```markdown
---
allowed-tools:
  - Write
  - Edit
---
```

This is a **least-privilege** pattern applied to AI tool use. Consider a skill that scaffolds a new service module: it should write files, but it should never execute shell commands, read environment variables, or make network requests. Restricting to `Write` and `Edit` enforces this boundary at the runtime level, not just at the prompt level.

**Common allowed-tools configurations by skill type:**

| Skill type | Recommended allowed-tools | Rationale |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Code scaffolding | `Write`, `Edit` | File creation only; no execution |
| Documentation generation | `Write`, `Read` | Read source, write docs; no mutation |
| Codebase analysis | `Read`, `Glob`, `Grep` | Read-only; prevents accidental writes |
| Dependency audit | `Read`, `Bash` (scoped) | Needs package manager read commands |
| Test runner | `Bash` | Execution required; no file writes |

**Edge case — tool name casing:** Tool names in `allowed-tools` are case-sensitive. `write` will not match `Write`. Always use the canonical capitalised form. A misconfigured allowlist that silently falls back to the full tool set is a security risk; verify your skill's effective tool set in a staging environment before deploying to production.

**Edge case — tool not in session:** If `allowed-tools` lists a tool that is not available in the current session (e.g., a tool disabled by a project-level `CLAUDE.md` restriction), the skill will simply not have access to it. The allowlist is intersected with the session's available tools, not unioned.

## argument-hint: Guided Invocation

`argument-hint` is a developer-experience feature. When a developer invokes a skill without providing arguments, Claude displays the hint string as a prompt, guiding them to supply the required input.

```markdown
---
argument-hint: "Target module path (e.g. src/payments/processor.ts)"
---
```

Without `argument-hint`, a skill invoked without arguments either fails silently, produces generic output, or asks Claude to guess the intent — all poor outcomes in a team environment. With a well-crafted hint, the skill becomes self-documenting at the point of use.

**Writing effective argument hints:**
- Include the expected format: `"ISO date range: YYYY-MM-DD to YYYY-MM-DD"`
- Include a concrete example: `"e.g. src/payments/processor.ts"`
- Keep it under 80 characters so it renders cleanly in the UI
- Use imperative phrasing: `"Enter the target module path"` not `"The module path"`

**Combining all three frontmatter keys** produces a skill that is isolated, safe, and ergonomic:

```markdown
---
context: fork
allowed-tools:
  - Read
  - Glob
  - Grep
argument-hint: "Module to analyse (e.g. src/auth/)"
---

# Codebase Analysis

Analyse the module at `{{args}}`. Identify coupling hotspots,
cyclical dependencies, and files exceeding 300 lines. Return
a structured summary with severity ratings.
```

This skill forks (keeping verbose analysis out of the main context), restricts to read-only tools (preventing accidental mutations), and prompts the developer for the target module.

sections.heading: 5. Personal Skill Variants: Customisation Without Contamination
sections.body_markdown:
Personal skill variants solve a specific collaboration problem: a developer wants to customise a team skill's behaviour without affecting their teammates.

**The problem scenario:** Your team has a project skill at `.claude/skills/review-pr/SKILL.md` that performs a standard PR review. You want a version that also checks for performance regressions against your personal benchmarking setup — a check that is meaningless on your colleagues' machines because they lack the benchmark tooling.

**The solution:** Create a personal variant in `~/.claude/skills/` with a **different name**.

```
~/.claude/skills/
  review-pr-perf/        ← personal variant, different name
    SKILL.md             ← extends team skill + adds perf checks
```

```markdown
---
allowed-tools:
  - Read
  - Bash
argument-hint: "PR branch name (e.g. feature/payment-refactor)"
---

# PR Review with Performance Check

Perform the standard project PR review for branch `{{args}}`.
Additionally, run `npm run benchmark:compare` and include
regression analysis in the review summary.
```

**Why a different name matters:** If you name your personal variant `review-pr` (same as the project skill), you shadow the project skill for yourself. This is sometimes intentional, but it creates a divergence that is invisible to your teammates and can cause confusion during pair programming or when reviewing session logs. Using a distinct name (`review-pr-perf`) makes the customisation explicit and auditable.

**Naming conventions for personal variants:**

| Pattern | Example | Use case |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Suffix with `-personal` | `review-pr-personal` | Generic personal override |
| Suffix with initials | `review-pr-jk` | Multi-developer team, clear ownership |
| Suffix with capability | `review-pr-perf` | Capability-specific extension |
| Prefix with `my-` | `my-review-pr` | Simple, readable |

**Edge case — skill discovery:** Claude discovers skills by scanning both `~/.claude/skills/` and `.claude/skills/`. If both locations contain a skill with the same name, the user-scoped skill takes precedence — the same resolution order as commands. This means a personal variant with the same name as a project skill will silently override the project skill for that developer. Document this in your team's Claude usage guide.

**Production tradeoff:** Personal variants in `~/.claude/skills/` are not subject to code review. A developer could create a personal skill that bypasses security controls defined in the project skill. Mitigate this by establishing a team norm: personal skills that interact with production systems or sensitive data should be reviewed informally before use, even if they are not committed to the repository.

sections.heading: 6. Skills vs CLAUDE.md: Choosing the Right Extensibility Primitive
sections.body_markdown:
The most consequential architectural decision in Claude extensibility is choosing between skills and CLAUDE.md entries. Getting this wrong leads to either context bloat (putting everything in CLAUDE.md) or fragmented, hard-to-discover workflows (putting everything in skills).

## The Core Distinction

**CLAUDE.md** is always loaded. Every token in CLAUDE.md consumes context on every interaction, regardless of whether that interaction is relevant. It is the right place for universal, always-applicable standards: coding conventions, security policies, team norms, and project-wide constraints.

**Skills** are on-demand. They consume context only when invoked. They are the right place for task-specific workflows, parameterised procedures, and heavyweight processes that are needed occasionally but not constantly.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[New behaviour to encode]:::concept --> B{Always needed\nin every interaction?}:::decision
  B -->|Yes| C[CLAUDE.md\nUniversal standards]:::success
  B -->|No| D{Parameterised\nor task-specific?}:::decision
  D -->|Yes| E[Skill with SKILL.md\nOn-demand invocation]:::success
  D -->|No| F{Shared across\nteam?}:::decision
  F -->|Yes| G[Project slash command\n.claude/commands/]:::success
  F -->|No| H[User slash command\n~/.claude/commands/]:::process
  C --> I[Loaded every session\nContext cost: always]:::warn
  E --> J[Loaded on invocation\nContext cost: on-demand]:::success
```

## Decision Framework

| Criterion | Favour CLAUDE.md | Favour Skills |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Invocation frequency** | Every interaction | Occasional, task-triggered |
| **Context budget sensitivity** | Low (short entries) | High (verbose workflows) |
| **Parameterisation** | Not needed | Required (target file, date range, etc.) |
| **Isolation requirement** | None | Needed (verbose output, exploration) |
| **Tool restriction** | Not applicable | Required (least-privilege) |
| **Team governance** | Enforced universally | Invoked by choice |
| **Discoverability** | Implicit (always active) | Explicit (developer must know to invoke) |

## Concrete Examples

**Put in CLAUDE.md:**
- "All Python functions must have type annotations."
- "Never commit secrets or API keys."
- "Use the `Result<T, E>` pattern for error handling in Rust."
- "The project uses PostgreSQL 15; do not suggest MySQL-specific syntax."

**Put in a skill:**
- A 200-line procedure for scaffolding a new microservice with all boilerplate.
- A codebase analysis workflow that reads 50 files and produces a dependency report.
- A release-notes generator that queries git log and formats output to a template.
- A PR review workflow that checks against a 20-point checklist.

**Edge case — the hybrid pattern:** Some teams put a brief *reference* to a skill in CLAUDE.md: `"For PR reviews, invoke the /review-pr skill."` This costs only a few tokens in CLAUDE.md while ensuring developers know the skill exists. This is a legitimate pattern for discoverability, but keep the CLAUDE.md entry to one line — do not duplicate the skill's logic.

**Production tradeoff:** CLAUDE.md entries are enforced passively — Claude applies them without being asked. Skills require active invocation — a developer must know to run them. For security-critical constraints (e.g., "never write to the production database"), CLAUDE.md is the correct vehicle because it cannot be forgotten. For productivity workflows, skills are correct because they do not burden every interaction with irrelevant context.

sections.heading: 7. Realistic Scenario, Exam Questions, and Architectural Synthesis
sections.body_markdown:
## Realistic Scenario: The Payments Platform Team

A fintech team of eight engineers maintains a payments processing service. They have the following requirements:

1. **All code** must follow PCI-DSS naming conventions and never log card numbers — a universal constraint.
2. **New payment handlers** must be scaffolded with a specific boilerplate (error handling, audit logging, idempotency keys) — a parameterised, occasional task.
3. **Codebase dependency analysis** is run before each sprint planning session — verbose, produces 2,000+ tokens of intermediate output.
4. **One engineer (Priya)** wants a personal variant of the scaffolding skill that also generates her preferred test fixture structure, which is not part of the team standard.
5. **The scaffolding skill** must never execute shell commands — only write files.

**Architectural decisions:**

| Requirement | Solution | Rationale |
|:---|:---|:---|
| PCI-DSS naming + no card logging | `CLAUDE.md` entry | Universal, always-applicable constraint |
| Payment handler scaffolding | `.claude/skills/scaffold-handler/SKILL.md` | Parameterised, occasional, team-shared |
| Dependency analysis | `.claude/skills/dep-analysis/SKILL.md` with `context: fork` | Verbose output; isolate from main context |
| Priya's test fixture variant | `~/.claude/skills/scaffold-handler-priya/SKILL.md` | Personal, different name, no team impact |
| No shell execution in scaffolding | `allowed-tools: [Write, Edit]` in scaffolding skill | Least-privilege enforcement |

**The scaffolding skill:**

```markdown
---
allowed-tools:
  - Write
  - Edit
argument-hint: "Handler name in PascalCase (e.g. RefundPaymentHandler)"
---

# Scaffold Payment Handler

Create a new payment handler named `{{args}}` following the
project's PCI-DSS conventions. Include:
- Error handling with Result<T, PaymentError>
- Audit log calls at entry and exit
- Idempotency key validation
- Unit test stub in __tests__/
```

**The dependency analysis skill:**

```markdown
---
context: fork
allowed-tools:
  - Read
  - Glob
  - Grep
argument-hint: "Service directory to analyse (e.g. src/payments/)"
---

# Dependency Analysis

Analyse all modules under `{{args}}`. Identify:
- Circular dependencies
- Files with > 5 direct imports
- Modules imported by > 10 consumers
Return a structured Markdown report with severity ratings.
```

---

## Exam-Style Questions

### Question 1

A team skill at `.claude/skills/analyse-codebase/SKILL.md` reads 60 source files and produces 3,000 tokens of intermediate reasoning before returning a 200-token summary. A developer reports that after running this skill, subsequent Claude responses in the same session are slower and less coherent. What is the most likely cause, and what is the correct fix?

**A)** The skill's `allowed-tools` list is too broad; restrict it to `Read` only.

**B)** The skill lacks `context: fork`; its verbose intermediate output is filling the main session's context window.

**C)** The skill should be moved to `~/.claude/skills/` to reduce project-level overhead.

**D)** The `argument-hint` is missing, causing Claude to spend extra tokens guessing the target.

**✅ Correct answer: B**

*Explanation:* The symptom — degraded response quality after a verbose skill — is the canonical indicator of context window pollution. `context: fork` runs the skill in an isolated sub-agent, returning only the final summary to the main session. Option A addresses tool safety but not context pollution. Option C changes scope but not isolation. Option D improves ergonomics but has no effect on context consumption.

---

### Question 2

A developer creates a personal skill at `~/.claude/skills/review-pr/SKILL.md` — the same name as the project skill at `.claude/skills/review-pr/SKILL.md`. What is the runtime behaviour, and what is the architectural risk?

**A)** Both skills execute in sequence; the personal skill's output is appended to the project skill's output.

**B)** The project skill takes precedence; the personal skill is ignored.

**C)** The personal skill takes precedence; the project skill is silently shadowed for this developer.

**D)** Claude raises an error and refuses to execute either skill until the name conflict is resolved.

**✅ Correct answer: C**

*Explanation:* User-scoped resources take precedence over project-scoped resources. The developer's personal skill silently overrides the team skill. This is intentional by design (personal customisation), but the risk is invisible divergence — the developer is no longer running the team-standard review, which could cause them to miss project-mandated checks. The correct mitigation is to use a distinct name for personal variants (e.g., `review-pr-personal`).

---

### Question 3

Which of the following is the strongest argument for placing a constraint in `CLAUDE.md` rather than in a skill's `SKILL.md`?

**A)** The constraint involves writing multiple files and benefits from parameterisation.

**B)** The constraint is security-critical and must be enforced on every interaction without requiring developer invocation.

**C)** The constraint produces verbose output that would pollute the main context window.

**D)** The constraint is personal to one developer and should not affect teammates.

**✅ Correct answer: B**

*Explanation:* CLAUDE.md is always loaded and passively enforced — it does not require the developer to remember to invoke anything. For security-critical constraints (e.g., "never log PII", "never write to production"), passive enforcement is essential. Options A and C describe skill use cases. Option D describes a user-scoped personal skill or user command.

---

## Architectural Synthesis

The extensibility model forms a layered governance stack:

$$\text{Governance} = \underbrace{\text{CLAUDE.md}}_{\text{universal}} + \underbrace{\text{Project skills/commands}}_{\text{team workflows}} + \underbrace{\text{User skills/commands}}_{\text{personal}}$$

Each layer has a distinct authority scope and lifecycle. The architect's job is to assign each behaviour to the correct layer — not to the most convenient one. Misassignment creates either context bloat (over-relying on CLAUDE.md), invisible divergence (personal skills shadowing project skills), or security gaps (relying on skill invocation for constraints that must be universal).

key_takeaways: Project commands in `.claude/commands/` are version-controlled and team-shared; user commands in `~/.claude/commands/` are local and personal. User-scoped resources take precedence over project-scoped resources when names collide.

SKILL.md frontmatter (`context`, `allowed-tools`, `argument-hint`) is evaluated before skill execution — these are runtime constraints, not soft instructions, making them reliable for security and isolation enforcement.

`context: fork` is the correct tool for skills that produce verbose intermediate output or exploratory context; it prevents context window pollution at the cost of added latency. Reserve it for skills consuming more than ~500 tokens of intermediate output.
Personal skill variants should use distinct names from their project-skill counterparts to avoid silently shadowing team standards and creating invisible workflow divergence.
`allowed-tools` implements least-privilege tool access at the skill level — always restrict scaffolding and analysis skills to the minimum required tool set to prevent accidental destructive actions.

CLAUDE.md is for universal, always-enforced standards (especially security constraints); skills are for on-demand, parameterised, task-specific workflows. Choosing the wrong primitive leads to either context bloat or unenforced constraints.
further_reading: Explore CLAUDE.md hierarchy and inheritance: how project-level, directory-level, and user-level CLAUDE.md files compose and override each other in nested monorepos.
Study Claude's tool permission model in depth: how session-level tool grants, project-level restrictions, and skill-level allowed-tools interact in a layered permission stack.
Investigate multi-agent orchestration patterns: how forked sub-agents in skills relate to explicit multi-agent workflows, and when to prefer one over the other for complex automation pipelines.

Review prompt injection risk in slash commands and skills: how to audit `.claude/commands/` and `.claude/skills/` for injection vectors, especially in commands that interpolate user-supplied arguments.

ts-3-3.title: 3.3 Apply path-specific rules for conditional convention loading

lesson.topic: 3.3 Apply path-specific rules for conditional convention loading
lesson.synopsis: Path-specific rules in Claude's `.claude/rules/` system let architects scope conventions to exactly the files that need them, using YAML frontmatter glob patterns to trigger conditional loading. This lesson covers how to author, structure, and reason about path-scoped rules — including when to prefer them over directory-level `CLAUDE.md` files — with a focus on production tradeoffs around context window efficiency and maintainability. Mastery of this mechanism is essential for designing Claude configurations that scale across large, heterogeneous codebases without polluting every session with irrelevant instructions.
learning_objectives: Explain how YAML frontmatter `paths` fields in `.claude/rules/` files enable conditional rule activation via glob patterns.
Design path-scoped rule files that load only when editing matching files, minimising irrelevant context and token consumption.
Distinguish when glob-pattern rules are architecturally superior to subdirectory `CLAUDE.md` files for cross-cutting conventions.
Construct glob patterns that target files by type across an entire codebase (e.g., `**/*.test.tsx`) and reason about their precedence and edge cases.
Evaluate production tradeoffs between path-scoped rules, directory-level rules, and project-level rules when conventions span multiple directories.

sections.heading: The Problem: Convention Sprawl in Large Codebases
sections.body_markdown:
Every non-trivial production codebase contains multiple distinct convention domains — infrastructure-as-code, frontend components, API handlers, test suites, database migrations — each with its own idioms, linting expectations, and architectural constraints. Naively placing all conventions in a single root `CLAUDE.md` creates a monolithic context block that loads on every file edit, regardless of relevance.

Consider a monorepo with the following structure:

```
my-platform/
├── .claude/
│   └── CLAUDE.md          ← loaded for every file
├── terraform/             ← IaC conventions
├── src/
│   ├── components/        ← React conventions
│   ├── api/               ← REST/GraphQL conventions
│   └── __tests__/         ← test conventions
└── migrations/            ← SQL migration conventions
```

If `CLAUDE.md` contains all five convention sets, every session — even editing a single Terraform variable — loads React component rules, SQL migration patterns, and test idioms. This wastes tokens, dilutes focus, and increases the risk that Claude applies the wrong convention to the wrong file.

The architectural solution is **conditional convention loading**: rules that activate only when the file being edited matches a declared path pattern. Claude's `.claude/rules/` system provides exactly this mechanism through YAML frontmatter `paths` fields.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  A[File opened for editing]:::concept --> B{Does file path\nmatch any rule's\nglob pattern?}:::decision
  B -->|yes| C[Load matching\nrule files]:::process
  B -->|no| D[Rule skipped —\nzero token cost]:::success
  C --> E[Rule content injected\ninto context]:::process
  E --> F[Claude applies\nscoped conventions]:::success
```

This lesson dissects every layer of that decision branch.

sections.heading: Path Glob Frontmatter Rules: Anatomy of a Scoped Rule File
sections.body_markdown:
A path-scoped rule lives in `.claude/rules/` and uses YAML frontmatter to declare the glob patterns that trigger its activation. The frontmatter block is delimited by `---` and must appear at the very top of the file.

**Minimal example — Terraform conventions:**

```markdown
---
description: Terraform infrastructure conventions
paths:
  - "terraform/**/*"
---

# Terraform Conventions

- Always use `snake_case` for resource names.
- Pin provider versions with `~>` constraints, never `>=`.
- Every `resource` block must include a `tags` map with at least `env` and `owner`.
- Use `locals` for repeated expressions; avoid inline string interpolation in `count` arguments.
```

The `paths` field accepts a **list of glob strings**. Claude evaluates each string against the workspace-relative path of the file currently being edited. If any pattern matches, the rule file is loaded into the context window for that session.

**Supported frontmatter fields:**

| Field | Required | Type | Purpose |
|:------|:--------:|:----:|:--------|
| `description` | No | string | Human-readable label; aids debugging and `--verbose` output |
| `paths` | Yes (for scoping) | list of strings | Glob patterns that trigger loading |

Omitting `paths` entirely causes the rule to load unconditionally — equivalent to placing content in `CLAUDE.md`. This is a common misconfiguration: a rule intended to be scoped silently becomes a global rule when its frontmatter is malformed or the `paths` key is missing.

> A small leak will sink a great ship.
> — Benjamin Franklin

The same principle applies to context windows: a single unscoped rule that should have been path-gated can silently inflate every session's token count.

sections.heading: Path-Scoped Rule Loading: Mechanics and Token Economics
sections.body_markdown:
Understanding *when* and *how* path-scoped rules load is critical for both correctness and cost management.

**Loading trigger:** Claude evaluates the `paths` globs against the file path at the moment a file is opened or becomes the active editing target. Rules are not re-evaluated mid-session unless the active file changes.

**Multiple rules can match simultaneously.** If a file matches three rule files, all three are loaded and concatenated into the context. Order of concatenation follows filesystem sort order within `.claude/rules/` unless the implementation specifies otherwise — treat ordering as undefined and write rules that are independent of each other.

**Token economics.** The practical benefit of path scoping is measurable. Suppose your project has:

| Rule file | Size (tokens) | Scope |
|:----------|-------------:|:------|
| `terraform.md` | 800 | `terraform/**/*` |
| `react-components.md` | 600 | `src/components/**/*.tsx` |
| `test-conventions.md` | 500 | `**/*.test.{ts,tsx}` |
| `migrations.md` | 400 | `migrations/**/*.sql` |
| `api-handlers.md` | 700 | `src/api/**/*.ts` |

Without path scoping, every session loads all 3,000 tokens of convention content. With path scoping, editing a single Terraform file loads only 800 tokens — a **73% reduction** in convention overhead for that session. At scale, across hundreds of daily sessions, this compounds into meaningful latency and cost savings.

The reduction factor $R$ for a session editing file type $i$ is:

$$R_i = 1 - \frac{T_i}{\sum_{j} T_j}$$

where $T_i$ is the token count of rules matching file type $i$ and $\sum_j T_j$ is the total token count of all rules. For the Terraform case: $R = 1 - 800/3000 \approx 0.73$.

**Edge case — files matching no rule:** A file that matches no `paths` pattern loads zero rule files from `.claude/rules/`. It still inherits any unconditional rules (those without `paths`) and the root `CLAUDE.md`. This is intentional: not every file needs specialised conventions.

**Edge case — deeply nested paths:** The glob `terraform/**/*` matches `terraform/modules/vpc/main.tf` but does **not** match `terraform/main.tf` if the implementation requires at least one intermediate directory segment after `**/`. Always test with `**/*` (matches files at any depth including direct children) rather than `**` alone, which may match directories rather than files depending on the glob engine.

sections.heading: Glob Rules vs. Directory CLAUDE.md Files: An Architectural Decision
sections.body_markdown:
Claude supports two mechanisms for scoping conventions to a subset of the codebase: **directory-level `CLAUDE.md` files** and **glob-pattern rules in `.claude/rules/`**. Choosing between them is an architectural judgment call with real production consequences.

**Directory `CLAUDE.md` files** work by physical co-location: place a `CLAUDE.md` inside `src/components/` and it loads whenever Claude edits any file within that directory subtree. This is intuitive for conventions that map cleanly to a single directory.

**Glob-pattern rules** decouple conventions from directory structure. They activate based on *what a file is*, not *where it lives*.

| Dimension | Directory `CLAUDE.md` | Glob-pattern rule in `.claude/rules/` |
|:----------|:---------------------:|:--------------------------------------:|
| Convention spans one directory | ✅ Natural fit | Overkill |
| Convention spans multiple directories | ❌ Requires duplication | ✅ Single source of truth |
| Convention targets a file type (e.g., `*.test.tsx`) | ❌ Cannot express | ✅ Direct expression |
| Visibility / discoverability | Low (buried in tree) | High (centralised in `.claude/rules/`) |
| Maintenance when directories are renamed | ❌ Rule moves with directory | ✅ Unaffected |
| Supports negation / exclusion patterns | ❌ No | ✅ Glob negation (e.g., `!**/vendor/**`) |

**The canonical case for glob rules:** test files. In a typical TypeScript monorepo, test files are co-located with their subjects:

```
src/
├── components/Button/Button.test.tsx
├── api/auth/auth.test.ts
├── utils/format.test.ts
└── hooks/useData.test.ts
```

There is no single directory that contains all test files. A directory `CLAUDE.md` approach would require placing identical (or symlinked) files in every directory that contains tests — a maintenance nightmare. A single glob rule with `paths: ["**/*.test.{ts,tsx}"]` covers the entire codebase from one file.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  subgraph Directory approach
    D1[components/CLAUDE.md]:::warn
    D2[api/CLAUDE.md]:::warn
    D3[utils/CLAUDE.md]:::warn
    D4[hooks/CLAUDE.md]:::warn
  end
  subgraph Glob approach
    G1[".claude/rules/test-conventions.md\npaths: **/*.test.{ts,tsx}"]:::success
  end
  D1 & D2 & D3 & D4 -->|duplicated content| X[Test conventions]:::concept
  G1 -->|single source| X
```

**When directory `CLAUDE.md` is still the right choice:** If a convention is genuinely directory-local — for example, a legacy module with its own idiosyncratic patterns that should never bleed into the rest of the codebase — co-location makes the scope self-evident to human readers browsing the filesystem. The rule of thumb: if you would need more than one `CLAUDE.md` to cover the same convention, switch to a glob rule.

sections.heading: Creating Path-Scoped Rules: A Worked Scenario
sections.body_markdown:
**Scenario:** You are the lead architect at a fintech company. The platform is a TypeScript monorepo with the following convention domains:

1. **Terraform** (`terraform/**/*`) — strict tagging, version pinning, no `count` on resources with dependencies.
2. **React components** (`src/components/**/*.tsx`) — functional components only, no class components, mandatory `data-testid` attributes.
3. **Test files** (`**/*.test.{ts,tsx}`) — `describe`/`it` structure, no `test()` at top level, mock factories in `__mocks__` directories.
4. **Database migrations** (`migrations/**/*.sql`) — every migration must include a rollback section, no `DROP TABLE` without `IF EXISTS`.

You will create four rule files.

**Step 1 — Directory structure:**

```
.claude/
└── rules/
    ├── terraform.md
    ├── react-components.md
    ├── test-conventions.md
    └── db-migrations.md
```

**Step 2 — Author each rule file with frontmatter:**

```markdown
<!-- .claude/rules/terraform.md -->
---
description: Terraform IaC conventions
paths:
  - "terraform/**/*"
---

# Terraform Rules
- Pin all provider versions with `~>` (pessimistic constraint).
- Every resource block requires tags: { env, owner, cost-centre }.
- Never use `count` on resources that have `depends_on`.
```

```markdown
<!-- .claude/rules/react-components.md -->
---
description: React component conventions
paths:
  - "src/components/**/*.tsx"
---

# React Component Rules
- Use functional components with explicit return types.
- Every interactive element must have a `data-testid` attribute.
- Props interfaces are named `<ComponentName>Props`.
```

```markdown
<!-- .claude/rules/test-conventions.md -->
---
description: Test file conventions
paths:
  - "**/*.test.ts"
  - "**/*.test.tsx"
  - "**/*.spec.ts"
  - "**/*.spec.tsx"
---

# Test Conventions
- Structure tests with `describe` / `it` — never bare `test()` at module level.
- Mock factories live in `__mocks__/` adjacent to the module under test.
- Each `it` block asserts exactly one behaviour.
```

```markdown
<!-- .claude/rules/db-migrations.md -->
---
description: Database migration conventions
paths:
  - "migrations/**/*.sql"
---

# Migration Rules
- Every migration file must contain both an `-- UP` and a `-- DOWN` section.
- Use `DROP TABLE IF EXISTS`, never bare `DROP TABLE`.
- Migrations are numbered with zero-padded 4-digit prefixes: `0042_add_user_roles.sql`.
```

**Step 3 — Verify scoping mentally:**

When Claude opens `src/components/Button/Button.tsx`, only `react-components.md` loads. When it opens `src/components/Button/Button.test.tsx`, both `react-components.md` (matches `src/components/**/*.tsx`) and `test-conventions.md` (matches `**/*.test.tsx`) load — correct, because the file is both a component file and a test file.

**Edge case — overlapping patterns:** The file `src/components/Button/Button.test.tsx` matches two rules. This is intentional and correct. Design rules to be additive and non-contradictory; if two rules conflict on the same file, the last-loaded rule's instruction wins (implementation-dependent — prefer explicit, non-overlapping instructions).

**Edge case — the `paths` list vs. brace expansion:** Some glob engines expand `**/*.test.{ts,tsx}` as a single pattern; others require two explicit entries. For maximum portability, list each extension separately as shown in `test-conventions.md` above.

sections.heading: Glob Patterns by File Type: Precision and Pitfalls
sections.body_markdown:
Glob patterns targeting file types — rather than directories — are the most powerful and most misused feature of path-scoped rules. This section catalogues the patterns you will encounter in production and the edge cases each carries.

**Common production patterns:**

| Pattern | Matches | Does NOT match |
|:--------|:--------|:---------------|
| `**/*.test.tsx` | Any `.test.tsx` at any depth | `.test.ts`, `.spec.tsx` |
| `**/*.test.{ts,tsx}` | Both `.test.ts` and `.test.tsx` | `.spec.ts` |
