| `src/**/*.ts` | All `.ts` files under `src/` | `lib/*.ts` outside `src/` |
| `terraform/**/*.tf` | All `.tf` files under `terraform/` | `terraform/modules/vpc/vars.tfvars` |
| `**/__tests__/**` | Everything inside any `__tests__/` dir | Co-located `*.test.ts` files |
| `migrations/**/V*.sql` | Flyway-style versioned migrations | `migrations/seed.sql` |

**The double-star semantics:** `**` matches zero or more path segments. This means `src/**/*.ts` matches both `src/index.ts` (zero intermediate segments) and `src/api/v2/handlers/auth.ts` (three intermediate segments). This is the correct mental model for most glob engines, but verify against Claude's specific implementation if behaviour is unexpected.

**Negation patterns:** Some rule systems support negation with `!`. For example, to load a rule for all TypeScript files *except* generated ones:

```yaml
paths:
  - "src/**/*.ts"
  - "!src/**/*.generated.ts"
  - "!src/**/*.d.ts"
```

If negation is supported, it is evaluated after positive patterns — a file matching a positive pattern is excluded if it also matches a negation pattern. If negation is *not* supported by the implementation, you must rely on positive-only patterns and accept that generated files will also load the rule (usually harmless but wasteful).

**Case sensitivity:** Glob matching is case-sensitive on Linux/macOS filesystems. `**/*.TSX` will not match `Button.tsx`. Standardise on lowercase extensions in your codebase and your glob patterns.

**Animated visualisation — how a glob pattern fans out across a file tree:**

```svg
<svg viewBox="0 0 320 160" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Glob pattern matching files across a directory tree">
  <!-- Tree lines -->
  <line x1="40" y1="20" x2="40" y2="140" stroke="#334155" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="40" y1="50" x2="80" y2="50" stroke="#334155" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="40" y1="90" x2="80" y2="90" stroke="#334155" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="40" y1="130" x2="80" y2="130" stroke="#334155" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="80" y1="50" x2="80" y2="130" stroke="#334155" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="80" y1="70" x2="120" y2="70" stroke="#334155" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="80" y1="110" x2="120" y2="110" stroke="#334155" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <!-- Root -->
  <rect x="10" y="10" width="60" height="18" rx="3" fill="#334155"/>
  <text x="40" y="23" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#f8fafc">src/</text>
  <!-- Dir nodes -->
  <rect x="70" y="42" width="70" height="16" rx="3" fill="#334155"/>
  <text x="105" y="53" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#f8fafc">components/</text>
  <rect x="70" y="82" width="50" height="16" rx="3" fill="#334155"/>
  <text x="95" y="93" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#f8fafc">api/</text>
  <rect x="70" y="122" width="50" height="16" rx="3" fill="#334155"/>
  <text x="95" y="133" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#f8fafc">utils/</text>
  <!-- File nodes -->
  <rect x="150" y="62" width="90" height="16" rx="3" fill="#6366f1"/>
  <text x="195" y="73" text-anchor="middle" font-size="8" fill="#fff">Button.test.tsx ✓</text>
  <rect x="150" y="102" width="90" height="16" rx="3" fill="#6366f1"/>
  <text x="195" y="113" text-anchor="middle" font-size="8" fill="#fff">auth.test.ts ✓</text>
  <rect x="150" y="122" width="90" height="16" rx="3" fill="#22c55e"/>
  <text x="195" y="133" text-anchor="middle" font-size="8" fill="#fff">format.test.ts ✓</text>
  <!-- Animated pulse on matched files -->
  <rect x="150" y="62" width="90" height="16" rx="3" fill="none" stroke="#f59e0b" stroke-width="2">
    <animate attributeName="opacity" values="0;1;0" dur="1.8s" repeatCount="indefinite"/>
  </rect>
  <rect x="150" y="102" width="90" height="16" rx="3" fill="none" stroke="#f59e0b" stroke-width="2">
    <animate attributeName="opacity" values="0;1;0" dur="1.8s" begin="0.6s" repeatCount="indefinite"/>
  </rect>
  <rect x="150" y="122" width="90" height="16" rx="3" fill="none" stroke="#f59e0b" stroke-width="2">
    <animate attributeName="opacity" values="0;1;0" dur="1.8s" begin="1.2s" repeatCount="indefinite"/>
  </rect>
  <!-- Pattern label -->
  <text x="160" y="15" font-size="9" fill="#f59e0b" font-weight="bold">**/*.test.{ts,tsx}</text>
  <text x="160" y="26" font-size="8" fill="#94a3b8">matches across all dirs</text>
</svg>
```

**Production pitfall — overly broad patterns:** The pattern `**/*.ts` will match every TypeScript file in the project, including generated files, declaration files, and configuration files. If your rule contains React-specific conventions, applying it to `vite.config.ts` is noise at best and confusing at worst. Always scope to the narrowest pattern that correctly covers your intent.

sections.heading: Path Rules Over Subdirectories: When to Centralise
sections.body_markdown:
The decision to use path-scoped rules instead of subdirectory `CLAUDE.md` files is not merely aesthetic — it has direct consequences for maintainability, discoverability, and correctness in production systems.

**The subdirectory `CLAUDE.md` trap:** Teams often start by placing `CLAUDE.md` files in subdirectories as conventions emerge organically. This works until the codebase is refactored. When `src/components/` is split into `src/ui/` and `src/features/`, the `CLAUDE.md` in `src/components/` must be manually copied or moved. If it is forgotten, Claude silently loses those conventions for the new directories.

**The centralisation argument:** A `.claude/rules/` file with a glob pattern is immune to directory renames. The pattern `src/**/*.tsx` continues to match files regardless of how the directory tree is reorganised, as long as files remain under `src/` and retain the `.tsx` extension.

**Decision framework:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[New convention to encode]:::concept --> B{Does it apply to\na single, stable directory?}:::decision
  B -->|yes| C{Is that directory\nunlikely to be renamed\nor split?}:::decision
  C -->|yes| D[Use subdirectory\nCLAUDE.md]:::success
  C -->|no| E[Use glob rule\nin .claude/rules/]:::success
  B -->|no — spans multiple dirs\nor targets a file type| E
```

**Hybrid architectures:** In large organisations, both mechanisms coexist. A team might use:

- Root `CLAUDE.md` for universal conventions (commit message format, security policies).
- `.claude/rules/` glob rules for cross-cutting file-type conventions (tests, migrations, IaC).
- Subdirectory `CLAUDE.md` for genuinely local, stable conventions (a legacy payment module with its own quirks).

The key architectural principle: **conventions that cross directory boundaries belong in glob rules; conventions that are genuinely local belong in directory files**.

**Subdirectory `CLAUDE.md` inheritance:** Directory `CLAUDE.md` files are additive — a file in `src/components/Button/` inherits from `src/components/CLAUDE.md`, `src/CLAUDE.md`, and the root `CLAUDE.md`. This inheritance chain can cause unexpected convention accumulation. Glob rules do not inherit; each rule is either loaded or not based on its `paths` match. This makes glob rules more predictable in complex projects.

**Operational tip:** Audit your `.claude/rules/` directory as part of your architecture review process. Each rule file should have a clear owner, a `description` in its frontmatter, and a `paths` pattern that is as narrow as correct. Rules without `paths` are global and should be treated with the same scrutiny as root `CLAUDE.md` content.

sections.heading: Production Tradeoffs, Exam Scenarios, and Practice Questions
sections.body_markdown:
## Realistic Production Scenario

**Context:** You are the Claude Certified Architect for a healthcare SaaS platform. The codebase is a TypeScript/Python monorepo:

```
platform/
├── terraform/          # AWS infrastructure
├── backend/
│   ├── api/            # FastAPI handlers
│   ├── models/         # SQLAlchemy models
│   └── tests/          # Python pytest files
├── frontend/
│   ├── src/
│   │   ├── components/ # React components
│   │   └── pages/      # Next.js pages
│   └── __tests__/      # Jest test files
└── migrations/         # Alembic SQL migrations
```

The security team mandates that all files handling PHI (Protected Health Information) — identifiable by the path segment `phi` or the filename suffix `.phi.ts` — must load a strict data-handling rule that prohibits logging patient identifiers and requires encryption annotations.

**Architectural decision:** You cannot use a directory `CLAUDE.md` because PHI-handling files are scattered across `backend/api/phi/`, `frontend/src/components/phi/`, and `backend/models/phi_models.py`. A single glob rule is the correct solution:

```markdown
---
description: PHI data-handling security rules (HIPAA)
paths:
  - "**/phi/**/*"
  - "**/*.phi.ts"
  - "**/*.phi.py"
---

# PHI Security Rules
- NEVER log values from PHI fields. Use `[REDACTED]` placeholders.
- All PHI fields in API responses must be annotated with `@encrypted`.
- PHI model fields require a `phi=True` marker in the field definition.
```

This single rule file enforces HIPAA-relevant conventions across the entire codebase, regardless of directory structure, and loads *only* when editing PHI-related files.

---

## Exam-Style Questions

### Question 1

A `.claude/rules/` file contains the following frontmatter:

```yaml
---
description: API handler conventions
paths:
  - "src/api/**/*.ts"
---
```

A developer edits the file `src/api/v2/handlers/auth.ts`. Which statement is correct?

**A.** The rule loads because `src/api/v2/handlers/auth.ts` matches `src/api/**/*.ts`.

**B.** The rule does not load because `**` requires at least two intermediate directory segments.

**C.** The rule loads only if there is no `CLAUDE.md` in `src/api/`.

**D.** The rule loads only for files directly inside `src/api/`, not subdirectories.

**✅ Correct answer: A**

*Explanation:* The `**` glob matches zero or more path segments, so `src/api/**/*.ts` matches files at any depth under `src/api/`, including `src/api/v2/handlers/auth.ts`. Option B is incorrect — `**` does not require a minimum number of segments. Option C is incorrect — glob rules and directory `CLAUDE.md` files are additive, not mutually exclusive. Option D describes the behaviour of `src/api/*.ts` (single `*`), not `src/api/**/*.ts`.

---

### Question 2

Your team has test files co-located throughout the codebase (e.g., `src/components/Button/Button.test.tsx`, `src/api/auth.test.ts`, `lib/utils/format.test.ts`). You want Claude to apply test-writing conventions only when editing these files. Which approach is most architecturally sound?

**A.** Place a `CLAUDE.md` in every directory that contains test files.

**B.** Add test conventions to the root `CLAUDE.md` so they are always available.

**C.** Create `.claude/rules/test-conventions.md` with `paths: ["**/*.test.{ts,tsx}"]`.

**D.** Create `.claude/rules/test-conventions.md` with no `paths` field.

**✅ Correct answer: C**

*Explanation:* Option A requires duplicating or symlinking the same content across many directories and breaks when directories are added or renamed. Option B loads test conventions for every file edit, wasting tokens and potentially confusing Claude when editing non-test files. Option D omits `paths`, making the rule unconditional — equivalent to option B. Option C uses a single glob rule that activates precisely when a test file is being edited, regardless of its directory location.

---

### Question 3

A rule file in `.claude/rules/` has the following frontmatter:

```yaml
---
description: React component rules
paths:
  - "src/components/**/*.tsx"
---
```

A developer edits `src/components/Button/Button.test.tsx`. The project also has a rule file with `paths: ["**/*.test.tsx"]`. What happens?

**A.** Only the React component rule loads, because it is more specific.

**B.** Only the test rule loads, because test conventions take precedence.

**C.** Both rules load, because the file matches both glob patterns.

**D.** Neither rule loads, because conflicting rules cancel each other out.

**✅ Correct answer: C**

*Explanation:* Path-scoped rules are evaluated independently. `Button.test.tsx` matches `src/components/**/*.tsx` (it is a `.tsx` file under `src/components/`) AND `**/*.test.tsx` (it is a test file). Both rules are loaded and their content is concatenated into the context. There is no precedence or cancellation mechanism — all matching rules load. This is the intended behaviour for files that belong to multiple convention domains. Architects should ensure that rules loaded simultaneously for the same file are non-contradictory.

key_takeaways: Path-scoped rules in `.claude/rules/` use YAML frontmatter `paths` fields containing glob patterns to load conventions only when editing matching files, directly reducing token consumption and context noise.
Omitting the `paths` field from a rule file's frontmatter silently makes it unconditional — a common misconfiguration that negates all scoping benefits.

Glob-pattern rules are architecturally superior to directory `CLAUDE.md` files whenever a convention applies to files spread across multiple directories or targets a file type (e.g., `**/*.test.tsx`) rather than a location.
Multiple rules can match a single file simultaneously; all matching rules are loaded additively — design rules to be independent and non-contradictory to avoid conflicts.
The `**` glob matches zero or more path segments, so `terraform/**/*` covers files at any depth under `terraform/`; use `**/*` rather than `**` alone to reliably target files rather than directories.

In production, a hybrid architecture is common: root `CLAUDE.md` for universal policies, `.claude/rules/` glob rules for cross-cutting file-type conventions, and subdirectory `CLAUDE.md` files only for genuinely stable, local conventions.
further_reading: 3.4 Rule precedence and conflict resolution — how Claude resolves contradictions when multiple rule files load for the same file
3.5 Dynamic rule composition — combining path-scoped rules with project-level and user-level rules in a layered configuration hierarchy
Glob pattern specification (POSIX.1-2017 §2.13) — the formal semantics of `*`, `**`, `?`, and bracket expressions for precise pattern authoring
Context window budgeting for Claude Architect configurations — measuring and optimising total token load across rule files, CLAUDE.md, and conversation history

ts-3-4.title: 3.4 Determine when to use plan mode vs direct execution

lesson.topic: 3.4 Determine when to use plan mode vs direct execution
lesson.synopsis: Choosing between plan mode and direct execution is one of the highest-leverage architectural decisions when orchestrating Claude-based agents. This lesson establishes the criteria, tradeoffs, and patterns that govern that choice — from single-file bug fixes to multi-service migrations — and shows how the Explore subagent preserves context window budget during verbose discovery phases. Mastery of this decision boundary directly reduces rework, prevents context exhaustion, and produces more reliable agentic pipelines in production.
learning_objectives: Distinguish the structural characteristics of tasks that warrant plan mode versus direct execution, and articulate the production tradeoffs of each choice.
Apply the Explore subagent pattern to isolate verbose discovery output and preserve main-conversation context during multi-phase agentic tasks.
Select plan mode for tasks with architectural implications — such as library migrations, microservice restructuring, or multi-file refactors — and justify that selection against concrete criteria.
Select direct execution for well-scoped, low-risk changes and recognise the signals that indicate a task has outgrown direct execution.
Combine plan mode and direct execution in a single workflow, using planning for investigation and execution for implementation, to maximise both safety and throughput.

sections.heading: The Core Decision: Why Mode Selection Is an Architectural Act
sections.body_markdown:
Every time you invoke Claude in an agentic context, you implicitly choose a posture: *think first, then act* (plan mode) or *act now* (direct execution). That choice is not cosmetic — it determines whether Claude explores the solution space before touching state, or commits immediately to a path. Getting it wrong in either direction has real costs: unnecessary planning overhead slows simple tasks; premature execution on complex tasks produces cascading rework.

> Measure twice, cut once.
> — English proverb

The decision maps onto a two-axis risk model. The first axis is **scope uncertainty** — how well-understood is the change? The second is **blast radius** — how many files, services, or data stores are affected if the first approach turns out to be wrong?

$$\text{Plan Mode Pressure} \propto \frac{\text{Scope Uncertainty} \times \text{Blast Radius}}{\text{Reversibility}}$$

When the numerator is large and reversibility is low, plan mode is the correct default. When the numerator is small and reversibility is high (e.g., a change behind a feature flag, or a single-function edit with a clear stack trace), direct execution is appropriate.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New task arrives]:::concept --> B{Scope well-understood?}:::decision
    B -->|Yes| C{Single file / function?}:::decision
    B -->|No| F[Plan Mode]:::success
    C -->|Yes| D[Direct Execution]:::success
    C -->|No| E{Architectural implications?}:::decision
    E -->|Yes| F
    E -->|No| G{> 5 files affected?}:::decision
    G -->|Yes| F
    G -->|No| D
    F --> H[Explore → Design → Execute]:::process
    D --> I[Execute → Verify]:::process
```

This flowchart is a first-pass heuristic. The sections that follow refine each branch with concrete criteria and production examples.

sections.heading: Plan Mode Use Cases: When Thinking Before Acting Is Non-Negotiable
sections.body_markdown:
Plan mode is designed for tasks where the cost of a wrong first move exceeds the cost of the planning overhead itself. Four structural signals reliably indicate plan mode is required:

| Signal | Example | Why it demands planning |
|:-------|:--------|:------------------------|
| Large-scale change | Migrating 45+ files from `moment.js` to `date-fns` | Import paths, API surface differences, and test coverage gaps are only visible after full discovery |
| Multiple valid approaches | Choosing between REST, gRPC, and GraphQL for a new service boundary | Infrastructure requirements differ; the wrong choice is expensive to reverse |
| Architectural decisions | Splitting a monolith into microservices | Service boundaries, shared state, and deployment topology must be designed before any file is touched |
| Multi-file modifications with dependencies | Refactoring a shared utility used by 30 modules | Downstream consumers must be enumerated before the utility signature changes |

**Plan mode is not just about safety — it is about information.** Before Claude can make a good architectural decision, it needs a map of the territory. Plan mode creates the space for that map to be built.

### What plan mode actually does

In plan mode, Claude:
1. Reads and indexes relevant files without modifying them.
2. Identifies constraints, dependencies, and ambiguities.
3. Generates a structured plan — typically a ranked list of steps with rationale.
4. Surfaces the plan for human review *before* any state changes occur.

This is the agentic equivalent of a design review. The plan is an artifact that can be critiqued, revised, and approved — reducing the probability that execution produces a surprise.

### Edge case: plan mode for tasks that *look* simple

A task described as "update the logging format" can hide architectural complexity. If the logging library is a shared singleton injected across 60 services, changing its format affects every downstream log aggregator, alerting rule, and dashboard. The surface description is simple; the blast radius is not. When in doubt, a brief planning pass costs far less than discovering the blast radius mid-execution.

sections.heading: Direct Execution Use Cases: When Planning Is Overhead
sections.body_markdown:
Direct execution is appropriate when the change is well-understood, the scope is bounded, and the cost of a mistake is low or easily reversed. Forcing plan mode onto these tasks adds latency and consumes context window budget without adding safety.

### Canonical direct-execution scenarios

- **Single-file bug fix with a clear stack trace.** The stack trace names the file, line, and function. The fix is a one-line guard clause. There is no ambiguity about what to change or where.
- **Adding a date validation conditional.** A product requirement says "reject dates more than 90 days in the past." The function is identified; the logic is a single `if` statement.
- **Renaming a local variable within one function.** No external API surface changes; the scope is entirely contained.
- **Updating a hardcoded constant.** A timeout value needs to increase from 30 s to 60 s in a single configuration file.

### The reversibility criterion

Direct execution is safest when the change is reversible. Version control makes most code changes reversible, but database schema migrations, infrastructure provisioning, and external API calls are not. Even a "simple" task that touches an irreversible operation should be treated with plan-mode caution.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Proposed change]:::concept --> B{Reversible?}:::decision
    B -->|Yes| C{Scope bounded to 1-2 files?}:::decision
    B -->|No| D[Treat as plan-mode candidate]:::warn
    C -->|Yes| E{Logic unambiguous?}:::decision
    C -->|No| D
    E -->|Yes| F[Direct Execution]:::success
    E -->|No| D
```

### Production tradeoff: latency vs. safety

In a high-throughput agentic pipeline — say, an automated code-review bot processing hundreds of PRs per day — forcing plan mode on every task would multiply latency and token cost by a factor of 3–5×. Direct execution for well-scoped tasks is not just acceptable; it is the correct engineering choice. The discipline is in correctly classifying tasks, not in defaulting to the more cautious mode for everything.

sections.heading: Safe Exploration Before Changes: Plan Mode as a Risk Firewall
sections.body_markdown:
One of plan mode's most underappreciated properties is that it creates a **read-only phase** before any write operations occur. This is not merely a UX nicety — it is a structural guarantee that prevents a class of costly mistakes.

### The rework cost model

Consider a library migration affecting $N$ files. If Claude begins executing without a plan and discovers at file $k$ that the chosen migration strategy is incompatible with a pattern used in files $k+1$ through $N$, the cost is:

$$C_{\text{rework}} = C_{\text{undo}}(k) + C_{\text{replan}} + C_{\text{re-execute}}(N)
$$

With plan mode, the incompatibility is discovered during the read-only exploration phase, before any file is modified. The cost collapses to:

$$C_{\text{plan}} = C_{\text{explore}}(N) + C_{\text{replan}}
$$

For large $N$, $C_{\text{plan}} \ll C_{\text{rework}}$. This is the mathematical case for plan mode on large-scale changes.

### Concrete example: microservice restructuring

A team asks Claude to split a monolithic `UserService` into `AuthService`, `ProfileService`, and `NotificationService`. Without a planning phase, Claude might begin by extracting `AuthService` — only to discover mid-way that `ProfileService` depends on an internal `AuthService` method that was never exposed as a public API. The extraction is now partially complete, the codebase is in an inconsistent state, and rolling back requires manual intervention.

With plan mode:
1. Claude reads all files in `UserService` and maps internal dependencies.
2. It identifies the hidden `AuthService`→`ProfileService` coupling.
3. The plan includes a step to define a new internal API contract *before* any extraction begins.
4. Human review catches an additional coupling the plan missed.
5. Execution proceeds against a validated plan.

The planning phase turned a potential mid-execution crisis into a pre-execution design decision.

### Safe exploration is not just for code

The same principle applies to infrastructure-as-code, database migrations, and CI/CD pipeline changes. Any task where mid-execution inconsistency is worse than pre-execution delay is a candidate for plan mode's read-only exploration phase.

sections.heading: The Explore Subagent: Isolating Discovery to Preserve Context
sections.body_markdown:
Context window exhaustion is a first-class production concern in multi-phase agentic tasks. A discovery phase that reads 50 files, grep-searches 200 symbol references, and traces 15 call chains can consume tens of thousands of tokens — tokens that are then unavailable for the planning and execution phases that follow.

The **Explore subagent** pattern addresses this directly: spawn a subordinate agent whose sole responsibility is discovery, have it return a *summary* to the main conversation, and discard the verbose intermediate output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Main Agent: receives task]:::concept --> B[Spawn Explore Subagent]:::process
    B --> C[Explore Subagent: reads files, traces deps, searches symbols]:::process
    C --> D[Explore Subagent: synthesises findings into structured summary]:::process
    D --> E[Summary returned to Main Agent]:::success
    C --> F[Verbose intermediate output discarded]:::warn
    E --> G[Main Agent: plans using summary]:::process
    G --> H[Main Agent: executes plan]:::success
```

### What the Explore subagent returns

The summary should be structured and dense — not a prose narrative. A well-designed Explore subagent output for a library migration might look like:

```markdown
## Discovery Summary: moment.js → date-fns migration

**Files affected:** 47 (list attached)
**Import patterns found:** 3 distinct patterns (default import, named import, side-effect import)
**API surface used:** 12 moment methods; 9 have direct date-fns equivalents, 3 require custom wrappers
**Test coverage:** 31/47 files have unit tests; 16 are untested
**Risk flags:**
  - `moment.duration()` used in 4 files; no direct date-fns equivalent
  - Locale configuration set globally in `src/i18n/setup.js`; must be migrated first
```

This summary is ~200 tokens. The raw discovery output that produced it might have been 15,000 tokens. The main agent's context window is preserved for planning and execution.

### Context preservation arithmetic

Let $W$ be the total context window budget, $D$ be the token cost of raw discovery, and $P + E$ be the token cost of planning plus execution. Without the Explore subagent:

$$\text{Available for P+E} = W - D$$

With the Explore subagent (summary cost $S \ll D$):

$$\text{Available for P+E} = W - S$$

For a 200K-token context window with $D = 40{,}000$ and $S = 500$, the difference is 39,500 tokens — enough for several additional planning iterations or a substantially larger execution phase.

### When to use the Explore subagent

| Condition | Use Explore subagent? |
|:----------|:---------------------:|
| Discovery touches > 20 files | Yes |
| Symbol search returns > 100 results | Yes |
| Call-chain tracing spans > 3 service boundaries | Yes |
| Discovery is a single `grep` on one file | No |
| Task is direct execution with no discovery phase | No |

sections.heading: Plan Mode for Architecture: Criteria and Production Patterns
sections.body_markdown:
Architectural tasks are the canonical home of plan mode. Three characteristics define an architectural task: it involves **structural decisions** (not just implementation details), it has **long-lived consequences** (the decision is expensive to reverse), and it has **cross-cutting effects** (it touches multiple components, teams, or deployment units).

### Worked scenario: choosing between integration approaches

A platform team is asked to integrate a new payment provider. Two approaches are viable:

- **Approach A:** Direct REST integration from the existing `OrderService`. Fast to implement; couples `OrderService` to payment provider details.
- **Approach B:** New `PaymentService` microservice with an event-driven interface. Requires new infrastructure (message broker, deployment pipeline); decouples `OrderService` from payment details.

This is not a coding task — it is an architectural decision with different infrastructure requirements, different failure modes, and different long-term maintenance costs. Direct execution is inappropriate because there is no single "correct" implementation to execute toward. Plan mode is required to:

1. Enumerate the constraints (SLA requirements, team ownership, existing infrastructure).
2. Evaluate both approaches against those constraints.
3. Produce a recommendation with explicit tradeoffs.
4. Define the implementation plan for the chosen approach.

Only after step 4 does execution begin.

### Library migration affecting 45+ files

A migration from `moment.js` to `date-fns` across 47 files is a textbook plan-mode task. The planning phase must:

- Map all import patterns (the Explore subagent is ideal here).
- Identify API surface gaps requiring custom wrappers.
- Sequence the migration to keep the codebase in a working state at each step (e.g., migrate test utilities before production code, migrate leaf modules before shared utilities).
- Define a rollback checkpoint after each batch.

Attempting this as direct execution — file by file, without a sequencing plan — risks leaving the codebase in a partially-migrated state where both libraries are present and their outputs are mixed.

### Microservice restructuring

Splitting a monolith requires decisions about:
- Service boundaries (which domain objects belong to which service)
- Shared state (how to handle data that was previously in a single database)
- API contracts (synchronous vs. asynchronous communication)
- Deployment sequencing (which service to extract first to minimise risk)

Each of these is a decision point, not an implementation step. Plan mode surfaces all decision points before any code is written, enabling human review at the highest-leverage moment.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Architectural task]:::concept --> B[Plan Mode: Explore phase]:::process
    B --> C[Plan Mode: Design phase]:::process
    C --> D{Human review}:::decision
    D -->|Approved| E[Execution phase]:::success
    D -->|Revisions needed| C
    E --> F[Incremental delivery with checkpoints]:::success
    C --> G[Decision log produced]:::process
```

sections.heading: Combining Plan and Execution: The Two-Phase Workflow
sections.body_markdown:
The most powerful production pattern is not a binary choice between plan mode and direct execution — it is a **two-phase workflow** that uses each mode for what it does best: plan mode for investigation and design, direct execution for implementation.

### The canonical two-phase pattern

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Complex task received]:::concept --> B[Phase 1: Plan Mode]:::process
    B --> C[Explore subagent: discovery summary]:::process
    C --> D[Main agent: architectural design]:::process
    D --> E[Structured plan with sequenced steps]:::success
    E --> F{Human approval}:::decision
    F -->|Approved| G[Phase 2: Direct Execution per step]:::process
    F -->|Revisions| D
    G --> H[Step 1 executed and verified]:::success
    H --> I[Step 2 executed and verified]:::success
    I --> J[... N steps executed and verified]:::success
    J --> K[Task complete]:::success
    G --> L{Unexpected complexity discovered?}:::decision
    L -->|Yes| B
    L -->|No| H
```

Note the feedback loop: if direct execution uncovers unexpected complexity (a hidden dependency, an undocumented API constraint), the workflow re-enters plan mode rather than continuing to execute against an invalidated plan.

### Worked example: library migration end-to-end

**Phase 1 — Plan mode:**
1. Spawn Explore subagent → returns 200-token summary of 47 affected files, 3 import patterns, 3 API gaps.
2. Main agent designs migration in 4 batches: (a) test utilities, (b) shared date helpers, (c) leaf components, (d) integration tests.
3. Plan identifies that `moment.duration()` requires a custom wrapper; wrapper is designed in the plan.
4. Human reviews and approves plan.

**Phase 2 — Direct execution (per batch):**
- Batch (a): Execute migration of 6 test utility files. Run tests. All pass.
- Batch (b): Execute migration of 3 shared helpers including custom `duration` wrapper. Run tests. All pass.
- Batch (c): Execute migration of 35 leaf components. Run tests. 2 failures — both in files that used an undocumented `moment` locale side-effect.
- **Re-enter plan mode** for the 2 failing files. Update plan. Execute fix. Tests pass.
- Batch (d): Execute migration of 3 integration test files. All pass.

The re-entry into plan mode at batch (c) is not a failure of the process — it is the process working correctly. The two-phase workflow is designed to handle discovered complexity gracefully.

### Production tradeoffs summary

| Dimension | Plan Mode | Direct Execution |
|:----------|:----------|:-----------------|
| Latency | Higher (exploration + design) | Lower (immediate action) |
| Token cost | Higher | Lower |
| Safety on complex tasks | High | Low |
| Safety on simple tasks | Equivalent | Equivalent |
| Human review opportunity | Built-in | Absent |
| Context window pressure | Managed via Explore subagent | Low (short tasks) |
| Rework risk | Low | High for complex tasks |

The two-phase workflow captures the best of both: plan mode's safety and visibility for the design phase, direct execution's efficiency for the implementation phase.

sections.heading: Realistic Scenario, Edge Cases, and Exam Questions
sections.body_markdown:
### Realistic scenario: migrating authentication middleware

A fintech platform runs 12 microservices. The security team mandates migrating from a custom JWT library (`jwt-custom`) to the industry-standard `jose` library. The migration affects:
- 12 services, each with its own token-validation middleware
- 3 shared authentication utilities in a monorepo package
- 2 API gateway configurations
- 1 token-issuance service with a different API surface

**Step 1 — Mode selection decision:**
The task has 18+ affected components, multiple API surface differences, and a security-critical blast radius. Direct execution is ruled out immediately. Plan mode is selected.

**Step 2 — Explore subagent dispatch:**
The Explore subagent reads all 12 middleware files, the 3 shared utilities, and the 2 gateway configs. It returns a structured summary: 4 distinct usage patterns of `jwt-custom`, 2 API surface gaps in `jose` (no built-in key rotation helper, different error type hierarchy), and a list of 6 services that use the shared utilities (meaning shared utility migration must precede service migration).

**Step 3 — Plan mode design:**
The main agent designs a sequenced migration:
1. Migrate shared utilities first (unblocks all 6 dependent services).
2. Implement `jose` wrappers for the 2 API gaps.
3. Migrate the token-issuance service (highest risk; isolated first).
4. Migrate remaining 11 services in 3 batches.
5. Update gateway configs last (after all services are validated).

**Step 4 — Human review:**
The security architect reviews the plan, notes that the token-issuance service should be migrated in a feature-flagged deployment, and adds a rollback checkpoint after step 3. Plan is approved.

**Step 5 — Direct execution per step:**
Each step is executed as a direct-execution task with a clear, bounded scope derived from the plan. Tests run after each step. No re-entry into plan mode is required.

---

### Edge cases

**Edge case 1: A "simple" task with a hidden blast radius.**
A request to "update the error message format" seems trivial. But if the error messages are parsed by a downstream log aggregator using regex patterns, changing the format breaks alerting. The correct response is a brief plan-mode pass to enumerate all consumers of the error format before executing.

**Edge case 2: Plan mode on a task that is genuinely simple.**
Forcing plan mode on a one-line bug fix wastes tokens and adds latency. Worse, it can produce an over-engineered plan that introduces unnecessary complexity. Recognising when *not* to use plan mode is as important as knowing when to use it.

**Edge case 3: Context window exhaustion mid-plan.**
If the Explore subagent is not used and the discovery phase is verbose, the main agent may exhaust its context window before the plan is complete. The result is a truncated plan that misses late-discovered dependencies — potentially worse than no plan at all. The Explore subagent pattern is the mitigation.

---

### Exam-style questions

**Question 1**

A team asks Claude to migrate a monorepo from Webpack 4 to Webpack 5. The monorepo contains 60 packages, each with its own `webpack.config.js`. Several packages use custom loaders that are incompatible with Webpack 5. Which approach is most appropriate?

A) Direct execution — Webpack migrations are well-documented and the steps are deterministic.
B) Plan mode with an Explore subagent to discover all config patterns and incompatible loaders, followed by a sequenced execution plan.
C) Plan mode without an Explore subagent, reading all 60 configs in the main conversation.
D) Direct execution with a rollback script prepared in advance.

**Correct answer: B**

*Explanation:* The task has 60+ affected files, multiple incompatibility patterns, and a non-trivial blast radius (broken builds across the entire monorepo). Plan mode is required. Option C is technically plan mode but risks context window exhaustion by reading 60 configs in the main conversation — the Explore subagent is the correct tool for that discovery phase. Option A underestimates the complexity introduced by custom loaders. Option D addresses rollback but not the planning gap.

---

**Question 2**

An engineer reports a `NullPointerException` in `OrderService.java` at line 142. The stack trace clearly identifies the cause: a missing null check before accessing `order.getCustomer().getEmail()`. What is the correct mode selection?

A) Plan mode — null pointer exceptions can indicate deeper architectural issues.
B) Direct execution — the scope is a single null check in one file with a clear stack trace.
C) Plan mode with an Explore subagent to check all usages of `getCustomer()` across the codebase.
D) Direct execution, but only after a manual code review of the entire `OrderService` class.

**Correct answer: B**

*Explanation:* This is the canonical direct-execution scenario: single file, single function, unambiguous fix, reversible change. Option A over-applies plan mode to a well-understood problem. Option C is appropriate only if the task were "audit all null-safety issues in the codebase" — not a single identified bug. Option D adds unnecessary manual overhead to a bounded task.

---

**Question 3**

During a two-phase migration workflow, direct execution of batch 3 (of 4) reveals that a shared utility function has an undocumented side effect that invalidates the migration plan for the remaining batch. What is the correct response?

A) Continue executing batch 4 using the original plan and fix the side effect afterward.
B) Abort the entire migration and restart from scratch.
C) Re-enter plan mode with the new information, update the plan for batch 4, obtain approval, then resume direct execution.
D) Switch to direct execution for the planning phase to save tokens.

**Correct answer: C**

*Explanation:* The two-phase workflow includes an explicit feedback loop: when direct execution uncovers unexpected complexity that invalidates the current plan, the correct response is to re-enter plan mode with the new information. Option A risks executing against an invalid plan. Option B discards all completed work unnecessarily. Option D is incoherent — direct execution is not a substitute for planning when a plan is needed.

key_takeaways: Plan mode is mandatory when scope uncertainty is high, blast radius is large, or reversibility is low — the planning overhead is always cheaper than mid-execution rework on complex tasks.
Direct execution is the correct choice for well-scoped, reversible, single-file changes; forcing plan mode onto simple tasks wastes tokens and adds latency without adding safety.

The Explore subagent pattern is the primary tool for preventing context window exhaustion during verbose discovery phases — it returns a dense summary to the main agent and discards raw intermediate output.

Architectural tasks (microservice restructuring, library migrations, integration approach selection) are structurally incompatible with direct execution because they require decision-making before implementation, not just implementation.

The two-phase workflow — plan mode for investigation and design, direct execution for implementation — is the production-grade pattern for complex tasks; it includes an explicit feedback loop back to plan mode when execution uncovers unexpected complexity.
Mode selection is itself an architectural judgment: the discipline is in correctly classifying tasks, not in defaulting to the more cautious mode for everything.
further_reading: Agentic orchestration patterns: how to design multi-agent pipelines with explicit phase boundaries and human-in-the-loop checkpoints
Context window management strategies: token budgeting, summarisation agents, and sliding-window techniques for long-running agentic tasks
Reversibility-first design: structuring infrastructure-as-code and database migrations to maximise rollback safety
Tool use and subagent delegation: when to spawn a subagent versus extending the main agent's tool list

ts-3-5.title: 3.5 Apply iterative refinement techniques for progressive improvement

lesson.topic: 3.5 Apply iterative refinement techniques for progressive improvement
lesson.synopsis: Iterative refinement with Claude is not about retrying the same prompt — it is a disciplined engineering practice that uses concrete examples, test-driven feedback loops, and structured elicitation to converge on correct, production-ready outputs. This lesson examines five high-leverage techniques — input/output examples, test-driven iteration, the interview pattern, bundled versus sequential fixes, and edge-case test cases — and shows how to choose among them based on problem structure and production risk. Mastery of these techniques is the difference between a developer who occasionally gets lucky with AI assistance and one who reliably ships quality software.
learning_objectives: Explain why concrete input/output examples outperform prose descriptions for communicating transformation requirements to Claude.
Design a test-driven iteration workflow in which failing test suites drive progressive improvement across multiple Claude turns.
Apply the interview pattern to surface latent design considerations before committing to an implementation in an unfamiliar domain.
Distinguish between problems that require bundled fixes in a single message and those that benefit from sequential, independent iteration.
Construct targeted test cases for edge-case handling and evaluate when to escalate from sequential to bundled remediation.

sections.heading: Why Iterative Refinement Is an Architectural Discipline
sections.body_markdown:
Iterative refinement with Claude is not a fallback for when the first prompt fails — it is the primary engineering workflow for any non-trivial task. The core insight is that **the cost of a wrong assumption compounds with implementation depth**: a misunderstood data-transformation rule caught at the example stage costs one message; the same misunderstanding caught after 400 lines of migration code costs a full rewrite plus regression risk.

Think of each refinement technique as a **feedback mechanism operating at a different layer of the system**:

| Technique | Feedback layer | Best for |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Concrete I/O examples | Specification | Ambiguous transformations |
| Test-driven iteration | Behaviour | Progressive correctness |
| Interview pattern | Design | Unfamiliar domains |
| Bundled fixes | Interaction effects | Coupled bugs |
| Sequential fixes | Independence | Orthogonal features |

The architectural judgment required is knowing **which layer is failing** before choosing a technique. Applying sequential fixes to coupled bugs, or skipping the interview pattern in an unfamiliar domain, are the two most common and costly mistakes practitioners make.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Task arrives]:::concept --> B{Specification clear?}:::decision
    B -->|No| C[Provide I/O examples]:::process
    B -->|Yes| D{Domain familiar?}:::decision
    C --> D
    D -->|No| E[Run interview pattern]:::process
    D -->|Yes| F{Tests exist?}:::decision
    E --> F
    F -->|No| G[Write tests first]:::process
    F -->|Yes| H[Iterate on failures]:::process
    G --> H
    H --> I{Bugs interact?}:::decision
    I -->|Yes| J[Bundle fixes]:::success
    I -->|No| K[Sequential fixes]:::success
```

This flowchart is the decision spine of the entire lesson. Each branch is a section below.

sections.heading: Concrete Input/Output Examples: Eliminating Specification Ambiguity
sections.body_markdown:
Natural language descriptions of data transformations are interpreted inconsistently because they rely on shared context that Claude — and often the developer — does not have. **Providing 2–3 concrete input/output pairs collapses the interpretation space to a single, verifiable contract.**

### Why prose fails

Consider the instruction: *"Normalise phone numbers to a standard format."* Claude might produce E.164 (`+14155552671`), national format (`(415) 555-2671`), or digits-only (`14155552671`). All are defensible readings. The developer's mental model is one of these; Claude's is another; the production system expects a third.

### The 2–3 example rule

Two examples establish the pattern; a third example tests the boundary. The examples should be chosen to cover:
1. The **happy path** (canonical input → expected output)
2. A **variant** that exercises a non-obvious rule (e.g., international prefix handling)
3. An **edge case** that would be silently wrong without explicit guidance (e.g., extension numbers, null input)

**Prompt fragment — phone normalisation:**

```
Normalise phone numbers. Examples:
  Input: "(415) 555-2671"      → Output: "+14155552671"
  Input: "415.555.2671 x42"   → Output: "+14155552671x42"
  Input: null                  → Output: null
Apply this rule to the following dataset...
```

With these three examples, Claude's output space is constrained to a single interpretation. The extension-handling rule and the null-passthrough rule would never have been inferred from prose alone.

### Production tradeoff: verbosity vs. precision

Examples add tokens and therefore latency and cost. The tradeoff is straightforward: **use examples whenever the transformation has more than one plausible interpretation, or when the output feeds a downstream system with a strict schema.** For simple, unambiguous tasks (e.g., "convert this list to uppercase"), prose is sufficient.

### Connection to adjacent topics

Input/output examples are the prompt-engineering equivalent of **property-based testing**: they define the invariants the output must satisfy rather than describing the algorithm. When you later write test suites (Section 3), the examples become the first test cases almost automatically.

sections.heading: Test-Driven Iteration: Letting Failures Drive Convergence
sections.body_markdown:
Test-driven iteration inverts the usual workflow: **write the test suite before asking Claude to implement anything, then share failing test output to guide each subsequent turn.** This technique is the highest-leverage refinement pattern for complex logic because it makes progress measurable and regression-proof.

### The workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Write test suite]:::process --> B[Share tests + task]:::process
    B --> C[Claude produces implementation]:::process
    C --> D[Run tests locally]:::process
    D --> E{All pass?}:::decision
    E -->|Yes| F[Ship]:::success
    E -->|No| G[Share failing output]:::process
    G --> C
```

### What to include in the test suite

A production-grade test suite shared with Claude should cover three layers:

| Layer | What it tests | Example |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Expected behaviour | Core contract | `assert normalise("415-555-2671") == "+14155552671"` |
| Edge cases | Boundary inputs | `assert normalise(None) is None` |
| Performance requirements | Throughput / latency | `assert duration < 0.5s for 10,000 rows` |

### Sharing failures effectively

Do not paste the entire test file on every turn. Share **only the failing tests and their output**. This focuses Claude's attention and avoids re-litigating already-passing behaviour. A well-structured failure message looks like:

```
Three tests are still failing after your last change:

FAIL: test_extension_preserved
  Input:  "415-555-2671 x42"
  Expected: "+14155552671x42"
  Got:      "+14155552671"

FAIL: test_null_passthrough
  Input:  None
  Expected: None
  Got:      AttributeError: 'NoneType' has no attribute 'replace'

FAIL: test_performance
  10,000 rows: 1.8s (limit: 0.5s)
```

This message gives Claude three independent signals: a logic gap, an unhandled null, and a performance regression. Whether to address them in one message or separately is the subject of Section 5.

### Production tradeoff: test quality determines iteration quality

The refinement loop is only as good as the test suite. Weak tests (happy-path only) produce implementations that pass tests but fail in production. **Invest in edge-case and performance tests before the first Claude turn, not after.** The cost of writing thorough tests upfront is almost always less than the cost of debugging a production incident caused by an edge case the tests never covered.

### Animated: the test-driven feedback cycle

```svg
<svg viewBox="0 0 320 160" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Test-driven iteration feedback loop">
  <!-- Track -->
  <ellipse cx="160" cy="80" rx="130" ry="55" fill="none" stroke="#334155" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="4 5"/>
  <!-- Labels at fixed positions -->
  <text x="160" y="18" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#334155" font-family="sans-serif">Write Tests</text>
  <text x="296" y="84" text-anchor="start" font-size="11" fill="#334155" font-family="sans-serif">Implement</text>
  <text x="160" y="148" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#334155" font-family="sans-serif">Run Tests</text>
  <text x="4" y="84" text-anchor="start" font-size="11" fill="#f59e0b" font-family="sans-serif">Share Failures</text>
  <!-- Orbiting dot -->
  <g>
    <circle r="9" fill="#6366f1"/>
    <animateMotion dur="5s" repeatCount="indefinite" path="M 290 80 A 130 55 0 1 1 30 80 A 130 55 0 1 1 290 80"/>
  </g>
  <!-- Centre label -->
  <text x="160" y="76" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" fill="#22c55e" font-family="sans-serif" font-weight="bold">Converge</text>
  <text x="160" y="91" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#22c55e" font-family="sans-serif">on correctness</text>
</svg>
```

sections.heading: The Interview Pattern: Surfacing What You Don't Know You Don't Know
sections.body_markdown:
The interview pattern inverts the usual prompt structure: instead of telling Claude what to build, you **ask Claude to interview you** — to surface the design considerations, failure modes, and constraints you may not have anticipated before a single line of implementation is written.

### When to use it

The interview pattern is highest-value when:
- You are implementing in an **unfamiliar domain** (e.g., a distributed cache, a financial settlement engine, a CRDT-based sync layer)
- The problem has **non-obvious failure modes** (e.g., cache invalidation, distributed consensus, schema migration rollback)
- You are making **architectural decisions that are expensive to reverse** (e.g., choosing an event-sourcing model vs. a CRUD model)

### How to invoke it

```
Before implementing a Redis-backed session cache for our authentication service,
I want to surface design considerations I may have missed.
Please interview me — ask me the questions whose answers would most change
your implementation approach. Ask one question at a time.
```

Claude will typically surface questions such as:
- *What is the expected cache hit rate, and what happens on a miss — block or serve stale?*
- *How should the cache behave during a Redis failover? Silent degradation or hard failure?*
- *What is the invalidation strategy when a user's permissions change mid-session?*
- *Do sessions need to be invalidated across all nodes simultaneously, or is eventual consistency acceptable?*

Each of these questions represents a **design decision that, if left implicit, becomes a latent production bug**.

### Interview pattern for cache invalidation — a worked example

A developer asks Claude to implement a write-through cache for a product catalogue. Without the interview pattern, Claude produces a reasonable default: invalidate on write, TTL of 5 minutes. In production, this fails silently when:
1. A bulk price update touches 50,000 records — the cache is never fully invalidated
2. A CDN layer caches the API response independently, creating a two-layer staleness problem
3. A rollback of the price update leaves the cache in an inconsistent state

With the interview pattern, questions 1–3 surface before implementation. The developer discovers they need a **tag-based invalidation strategy** (invalidate all records with tag `catalogue:prices`) rather than key-by-key invalidation. This is a fundamentally different architecture — one that cannot be retrofitted cheaply.

### Production tradeoff: time spent vs. rework avoided

The interview pattern adds one to three turns before implementation begins. The tradeoff is asymmetric: **a 10-minute interview that surfaces a cache invalidation strategy saves days of production debugging.** The pattern is not appropriate for well-understood, low-risk tasks — use it selectively for high-stakes architectural decisions.

### Connection to adjacent topics

The interview pattern is the prompt-engineering analogue of an **Architecture Decision Record (ADR)** process: it forces explicit consideration of alternatives and failure modes before committing. The outputs of the interview — the answered questions — become the specification that drives the test suite in Section 3.

sections.heading: Single Message vs. Sequential Fixes: Matching Strategy to Problem Structure
sections.body_markdown:
One of the most consequential judgment calls in iterative refinement is whether to address multiple issues in a single message or to fix them one at a time. **The correct answer depends entirely on whether the issues interact.**

### The interaction test

Two issues interact if fixing one changes the correct fix for the other. Formally, let $f_A$ be the fix for issue $A$ and $f_B$ be the fix for issue $B$. They interact if:

$$f_{A \cup B} \neq f_A \oplus f_B$$

where $\oplus$ denotes independent composition. When this inequality holds, sequential fixes will produce a broken intermediate state and may cause Claude to re-introduce a previously fixed bug.

### Decision table

| Problem structure | Strategy | Rationale |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Issues are orthogonal (different modules, no shared state) | Sequential | Easier to review, smaller diffs, clearer causality |
| Issues share state or control flow | Bundled single message | Prevents inconsistent intermediate states |
| One issue is a root cause of others | Fix root cause first, then reassess | Downstream issues may disappear |
| Performance + correctness regression together | Bundled | Performance fix may change the correct algorithm |

### Worked example: bundled fix

A migration script has three bugs:
1. Null values in `user.email` cause an `AttributeError` on `.lower()`
2. The deduplication logic uses `==` on unhashed emails, missing case variants
3. The batch size is 10,000 rows, causing OOM on large tables

Bugs 1 and 2 interact: the correct fix for bug 2 (normalise to lowercase before hashing) **also fixes bug 1** if implemented as `email.lower() if email else None`. Fixing them sequentially risks Claude producing a null-guard in turn 1 that is then overwritten by the normalisation logic in turn 2. The correct approach is a single message:

```
Three interacting issues in the migration script:

1. Null emails crash on .lower() — need null guard
2. Deduplication misses case variants — need lowercase normalisation before hashing
   (Note: the null guard from issue 1 must compose with this fix)
3. Batch size 10,000 causes OOM on tables > 5M rows — reduce to 500 with progress logging

Please address all three in a single revised version.
```

### Worked example: sequential fix

The same script also has two independent issues:
- The logging format uses `print()` instead of the project's `structlog` logger
- The progress bar library (`tqdm`) is not in `requirements.txt`

These are orthogonal: fixing the logger does not affect `requirements.txt`. Sequential fixes are correct here — they produce smaller, reviewable diffs and make the git history legible.

### Production tradeoff: review burden vs. correctness risk

Bundled fixes produce larger diffs that are harder to review. Sequential fixes produce cleaner history but risk inconsistent intermediate states for coupled bugs. **Default to sequential; escalate to bundled only when you can articulate the interaction.**

sections.heading: Test Cases for Edge Handling: Precision Over Description
sections.body_markdown:
Edge cases are where production systems fail, and they are also where natural language descriptions are most likely to be misinterpreted. **Providing specific test cases with example input and expected output is the most reliable way to communicate edge-case requirements.**

### The anatomy of an edge-case test message

An effective edge-case message has four components:
1. **The failing behaviour** — what currently happens
2. **The input that triggers it** — exact value, not a description
3. **The expected output** — exact value, not a description
4. **The invariant** — the rule the fix must preserve

```
The migration script fails on the following edge cases:

Edge case 1 — null email:
  Input:    {"user_id": 42, "email": null}
  Current:  AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'lower'
  Expected: {"user_id": 42, "email": null}  (pass through unchanged)
  Invariant: null emails must not be deduplicated against each other

Edge case 2 — empty string email:
  Input:    {"user_id": 99, "email": ""}
  Current:  Inserted as duplicate of other empty-string emails
  Expected: {"user_id": 99, "email": ""}  (treat as distinct from null; do not deduplicate)
  Invariant: empty string ≠ null in deduplication logic

Edge case 3 — email with leading/trailing whitespace:
  Input:    {"user_id": 7, "email": "  Alice@Example.com  "}
  Current:  Stored as-is, not matched against "alice@example.com"
  Expected: {"user_id": 7, "email": "alice@example.com"}  (strip + lowercase)
  Invariant: normalisation must be idempotent
```

### Why exact values matter

The distinction between `null` and `""` (empty string) in edge case 2 above is invisible in prose. A developer writing *"handle missing emails gracefully"* almost certainly means null; Claude may reasonably include empty strings in that category. The test case makes the distinction unambiguous.

### Idempotency as a test invariant

For transformation functions, idempotency is a critical invariant: applying the function twice should produce the same result as applying it once. Formally:

$$f(f(x)) = f(x) \quad \forall x \in \text{domain}$$

Including this as an explicit invariant in the test message prevents Claude from producing a normalisation function that strips whitespace on the first call but not on subsequent calls (a common bug when stripping is applied conditionally).

### Connection to test-driven iteration

Edge-case test messages are the natural input to the test-driven iteration loop. Each edge case becomes a test assertion; the loop runs until all assertions pass. The two techniques compose: write the edge-case tests upfront (Section 3), then use the exact-value message format above when a specific edge case fails.

sections.heading: Realistic Scenario, Exam Questions, and Architectural Synthesis
sections.body_markdown:
## Realistic Scenario: Migrating a Legacy User Table

A team is migrating a 12-million-row `users` table from a legacy MySQL schema to a new PostgreSQL schema. The migration script must:
- Normalise email addresses (strip, lowercase)
- Deduplicate by normalised email (keep the oldest account)
- Handle null and empty-string emails without crashing
- Complete within a 4-hour maintenance window (requires batching)
- Be idempotent (safe to re-run if interrupted)

**Turn 1 — Interview pattern:**
Before writing a line of code, the architect invokes the interview pattern:

```
We are writing a one-time migration script for a 12M-row users table.
Before I describe the requirements, please interview me to surface
considerations I may have missed. One question at a time.
```

Claude surfaces: *"If the migration is interrupted mid-run, how should a re-run behave — skip already-migrated rows, or re-process them safely?"* This question reveals that the team had not designed for idempotency. The architect adds a `migrated_at` timestamp column to the source table as a checkpoint.

**Turn 2 — I/O examples:**
The architect provides three concrete examples covering the happy path, the deduplication case, and the null case.

**Turn 3 — Test suite:**
The architect shares a pytest suite covering 12 cases: 4 happy-path, 5 edge-case, 3 performance (batch throughput, total runtime estimate, memory ceiling).

**Turn 4 — First implementation + failures:**
Claude produces the script. 3 of 12 tests fail: null crash, empty-string deduplication, and batch throughput (800 rows/s vs. 2,000 rows/s target).

**Turn 5 — Bundled fix:**
The null crash and empty-string deduplication interact (both affect the normalisation function), so they are bundled with the performance fix (which changes the batch query structure) into a single message with exact input/output examples for each failing test.

**Turn 6 — All tests pass.** The script ships.

---

## Exam-Style Questions

### Question 1

A developer is iterating on a data pipeline that has two bugs: (A) a divide-by-zero error when the input list is empty, and (B) a rounding error in the final aggregation step. The developer asks Claude to fix both in separate sequential messages. What is the most likely production risk?

**A.** Claude will refuse to fix the second bug without seeing the first fix applied.
**B.** The fixes are independent, so sequential iteration is correct and there is no additional risk.
**C.** If the rounding fix changes the aggregation algorithm, it may reintroduce the divide-by-zero error if the empty-list guard was implemented inline with the old algorithm.
**D.** Sequential fixes always produce larger diffs than bundled fixes.

**Correct answer: C**

> *Explanation:* The two bugs interact if the rounding fix restructures the aggregation function. A null guard implemented against the old algorithm may not compose correctly with the new one. The architect should assess whether the fixes share code paths before choosing sequential vs. bundled strategy. B is wrong because independence must be verified, not assumed. A and D are false.

---

### Question 2

An architect is implementing a distributed rate limiter using Redis. They have used Claude for Redis integrations before. Which technique provides the highest value before implementation begins?

**A.** Providing 2–3 concrete input/output examples of rate-limit decisions.
**B.** Writing a test suite covering expected behaviour and edge cases.
**C.** Running the interview pattern to surface failure modes and design decisions.
**D.** Providing a sequential series of fixes starting from a minimal implementation.

**Correct answer: C**

> *Explanation:* A distributed rate limiter has non-obvious failure modes: Redis failover behaviour, clock skew between nodes, sliding vs. fixed window semantics, and race conditions in the increment-then-check pattern. These are design decisions that cannot be captured by I/O examples (A) or tests (B) until the design is settled. The interview pattern surfaces them before any code is written. D is a refinement technique, not a pre-implementation technique.

---

### Question 3

A developer shares the following with Claude: *"Fix the null handling in the email normalisation function."* The function subsequently passes null-input tests but fails on empty-string inputs in production. What was the most likely root cause of the production failure?

**A.** Claude misunderstood the word "null" to mean Python `None` rather than SQL `NULL`.
**B.** The prose description did not distinguish between `null` and empty string `""` as separate edge cases, so Claude only handled the case it inferred.
**C.** The developer should have used the interview pattern instead of a prose fix request.
**D.** Sequential fixes cannot handle null and empty-string cases simultaneously.

**Correct answer: B**

> *Explanation:* Natural language descriptions of edge cases collapse distinct values into vague categories. "Null handling" is interpreted as handling Python `None`; empty string is a separate, non-obvious edge case that requires an explicit test case with exact input/output values. The fix is to provide both cases as concrete test assertions (as shown in Section 6). A is plausible but secondary. C is incorrect — the interview pattern is for design, not edge-case specification. D is false.

---

## Architectural Synthesis

The five techniques in this lesson are not alternatives — they are **layers of a single refinement stack**:

1. **Interview pattern** eliminates design-level unknowns before specification
2. **I/O examples** collapse specification ambiguity before implementation
3. **Test suite** makes correctness measurable before the first implementation turn
4. **Edge-case test messages** target the specific failures that survive the happy-path suite
5. **Bundled vs. sequential judgment** prevents interaction effects from corrupting the convergence path

Applied in order, these techniques transform Claude from a code generator that occasionally produces correct output into a **reliable engineering collaborator** whose outputs are verifiable, reproducible, and production-safe.

key_takeaways: Concrete input/output examples are the most reliable way to communicate transformation requirements — 2–3 examples covering the happy path, a variant, and an edge case collapse the interpretation space to a single verifiable contract.

Test-driven iteration — writing the full test suite before the first implementation turn — makes progress measurable and prevents regression; share only failing tests and their output on each subsequent turn.

The interview pattern should be invoked before implementation in any unfamiliar domain or high-stakes architectural decision; the questions it surfaces represent latent production bugs that prose specifications will never catch.
Fixes should be bundled in a single message when they interact (share state, control flow, or a root cause); sequential fixes are correct only when issues are demonstrably orthogonal.

Edge-case test messages must use exact input and expected output values — prose descriptions of edge cases are systematically misinterpreted because they collapse distinct values (null vs. empty string) into vague categories.

The five refinement techniques form a stack: interview pattern → I/O examples → test suite → edge-case tests → bundled/sequential judgment. Applied in order, they transform AI-assisted development from opportunistic to reliable.
further_reading: Prompt chaining and multi-turn conversation architecture — how to structure long refinement sessions without context degradation
Property-based testing (Hypothesis, QuickCheck) — the formal analogue of I/O example-driven specification
Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) — the documentation practice that mirrors the interview pattern at the team level
Chaos engineering and failure mode analysis — the production discipline that the interview pattern approximates at the design stage
Test pyramid strategy — how unit, integration, and contract tests map to the three layers of the test suite described in Section 3

ts-3-6.title: 3.6 Integrate Claude Code into CI/CD pipelines

lesson.topic: 3.6 Integrate Claude Code into CI/CD pipelines
lesson.synopsis: This lesson covers the architectural patterns and operational mechanics for integrating Claude Code into CI/CD pipelines as a first-class automated agent — not a developer convenience. It examines how the `-p` flag, structured JSON output, CLAUDE.md context injection, and session isolation combine to produce reliable, machine-parseable AI review and test-generation steps. Production tradeoffs around deduplication, context window management, and review quality are treated with the depth expected of a Claude Certified Architect.
learning_objectives: Explain how the `-p` (print) flag enables non-interactive Claude Code execution and why omitting it causes CI pipeline hangs.
Configure `--output-format json` and `--json-schema` flags to produce structured, machine-parseable findings suitable for automated PR comment posting.
Design CLAUDE.md files that supply CI-invoked Claude Code with project context including testing standards, fixture conventions, and review criteria.
Justify the use of independent Claude review instances over same-session review, and describe the session-isolation mechanism that makes independent review more effective.
Implement incremental review deduplication strategies that prevent duplicate PR comments across successive commits.

sections.heading: 1. The Non-Interactive Print Flag: Making Claude Code Pipeline-Safe
sections.body_markdown:
The single most important flag for CI integration is `-p` (long form: `--print`). Without it, Claude Code assumes an interactive terminal and will block waiting for user input — causing your pipeline to hang indefinitely.

When `-p` is supplied, Claude Code:
1. Accepts the prompt from the command-line argument (or stdin pipe).
2. Executes the task.
3. Prints the result to stdout.
4. Exits with a non-zero code on failure.

```bash
# Minimal CI invocation — review a diff and exit
claude -p "Review the following diff for security issues:\n$(git diff origin/main)" \
  --allowedTools none
```

The `--allowedTools none` guard is a production best-practice: it prevents Claude from invoking shell tools or file-write operations during a read-only review step, limiting blast radius.

### Why Hangs Are Catastrophic in CI

CI runners are billed by the minute on most platforms. A hung Claude Code process will consume the runner's timeout (often 6–60 minutes) before the job is killed, wasting compute budget and blocking the merge queue. The `-p` flag is the architectural guarantee that this cannot happen.

| Mode | Waits for input? | Exits automatically? | Safe in CI? |
|:-----|:----------------:|:--------------------:|:-----------:|
| Interactive (default) | Yes | No | ❌ |
| `-p` / `--print` | No | Yes | ✅ |

### Edge Case: Piped Prompts vs. Inline Prompts

For large diffs or long context payloads, inline shell quoting becomes fragile. Prefer piping:

```bash
# Pipe a constructed prompt file — avoids shell quoting limits
cat prompt.txt | claude -p --output-format json
```

This also keeps the pipeline script readable and avoids argument-length limits on some Linux kernels ($\approx$ 2 MB for `ARG_MAX`).

### Connection to Adjacent Topics

The `-p` flag is the entry point for all other CI patterns in this lesson. JSON output flags, CLAUDE.md context, and deduplication strategies are all layered on top of a `-p`-gated invocation. Think of `-p` as the foundation of the CI integration contract.

sections.heading: 2. Structured Output: `--output-format json` and `--json-schema`
sections.body_markdown:
Raw prose output from Claude Code is useful for human readers but hostile to automation. The `--output-format json` flag switches Claude's response envelope to a JSON object, and `--json-schema` lets you enforce a specific schema for the `result` field inside that envelope.

### The Output Envelope

With `--output-format json`, every response is wrapped:

```json
{
  "type": "result",
  "subtype": "success",
  "cost_usd": 0.0042,
  "duration_ms": 1840,
  "result": "<your structured payload here>"
}
```

The `result` field is a string by default. When you add `--json-schema`, Claude is instructed to populate `result` with a JSON object conforming to your schema.

### Defining a Review Schema

```json
{
  "$schema": "http://json-schema.org/draft-07/schema#",
  "type": "object",
  "properties": {
    "findings": {
      "type": "array",
      "items": {
        "type": "object",
        "required": ["severity", "file", "line", "message"],
        "properties": {
          "severity": { "type": "string", "enum": ["critical", "high", "medium", "low", "info"] },
          "file": { "type": "string" },
          "line": { "type": "integer" },
          "message": { "type": "string" }
        }
      }
    },
    "summary": { "type": "string" }
  },
  "required": ["findings", "summary"]
}
```

Save this as `review-schema.json` and invoke:

```bash
claude -p "$(cat prompt.txt)" \
  --output-format json \
  --json-schema review-schema.json \
  | jq '.result | fromjson'
```

### Consuming Findings in a GitHub Actions Step

```yaml
- name: Claude Code Review
  id: review
  run: |
    OUTPUT=$(cat prompt.txt | claude -p \
      --output-format json \
      --json-schema review-schema.json)
    echo "findings=$(echo $OUTPUT | jq -c '.result | fromjson | .findings')" \
      >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

- name: Post inline PR comments
  uses: actions/github-script@v7
  with:
    script: |
      const findings = JSON.parse('${{ steps.review.outputs.findings }}');
      for (const f of findings) {
        await github.rest.pulls.createReviewComment({
          owner: context.repo.owner,
          repo: context.repo.repo,
          pull_number: context.payload.pull_request.number,
          path: f.file,
          line: f.line,
          body: `**[${f.severity.toUpperCase()}]** ${f.message}`
        });
      }
```

### Production Tradeoff: Schema Strictness vs. Flexibility

A strict schema with `additionalProperties: false` reduces hallucinated fields but can cause Claude to omit nuanced findings that don't fit neatly into your taxonomy. A pragmatic pattern is to allow an optional `detail` free-text field alongside the structured fields — you get machine-parseability for automation and human-readable depth for engineers.

| Schema Strictness | Automation Reliability | Finding Richness | Recommended For |
|:------------------|:----------------------:|:----------------:|:----------------|
| Strict (no extras) | High | Lower | Security gates, SLA checks |
| Permissive (extras allowed) | Medium | Higher | Developer feedback, code quality |
| No schema | Low | Highest | Exploratory / local use only |

sections.heading: 3. CLAUDE.md as CI Context: Injecting Project Intelligence
sections.body_markdown:
CLAUDE.md is the primary mechanism for giving CI-invoked Claude Code the project knowledge it needs to produce relevant, non-generic output. When Claude Code runs, it reads CLAUDE.md from the repository root (and optionally from subdirectories) before processing any prompt. In a CI context, this replaces the conversational back-and-forth that a developer would use to orient Claude interactively.

### What Belongs in CLAUDE.md for CI

A CLAUDE.md optimised for CI invocations should contain:

1. **Testing standards** — which frameworks are used, naming conventions, required coverage thresholds.
2. **Fixture conventions** — where fixtures live, how to reference them, factory patterns.
3. **Review criteria** — what constitutes a blocking finding vs. a suggestion, security policies, style rules.
4. **Available tooling** — linters, formatters, and their configuration files.
5. **Scope boundaries** — which directories are auto-generated and should be excluded from review.

```markdown
# CLAUDE.md

## Testing Standards
- Framework: pytest with pytest-asyncio for async tests
- All tests live in `tests/` mirroring the `src/` structure
- Use `tests/fixtures/` for shared fixtures; import via `conftest.py`
- Minimum branch coverage: 80% (enforced by `pytest --cov`)
- Test naming: `test_<unit>_<scenario>_<expected_outcome>`

## Review Criteria
- BLOCKING: Any SQL query constructed via string concatenation
- BLOCKING: Secrets or credentials in source files
- SUGGESTION: Missing type annotations on public functions
- IGNORE: Auto-generated files in `src/generated/`

## Available Fixtures
- `user_factory` — creates a User ORM instance with sensible defaults
- `db_session` — provides a transactional test DB session (rolls back after each test)
- `mock_http_client` — pre-configured httpx mock for external API calls
```

### CLAUDE.md Inheritance in Monorepos

Claude Code supports hierarchical CLAUDE.md files. A root-level CLAUDE.md sets global standards; package-level CLAUDE.md files override or extend them for that subtree. In a CI pipeline reviewing a specific package, Claude will merge both contexts:

```
repo/
├── CLAUDE.md              ← global: org-wide standards
├── packages/
│   ├── auth-service/
│   │   └── CLAUDE.md      ← local: auth-specific review rules
│   └── billing-service/
│       └── CLAUDE.md      ← local: PCI-DSS compliance checks
```

### Architectural Judgment: CLAUDE.md as a Living Contract

Treat CLAUDE.md as a first-class engineering artifact, not documentation afterthought. When a new testing pattern is adopted, update CLAUDE.md in the same PR. When a security policy changes, update CLAUDE.md before the policy takes effect. The quality of CI-invoked Claude Code output is directly proportional to the quality of CLAUDE.md — a stale or vague CLAUDE.md produces generic, low-signal findings.

> The quality of the answer depends entirely on the quality of the question.
> — Voltaire

### Edge Case: CLAUDE.md Size and Context Window Budget

Claude Code reads CLAUDE.md into the context window before the prompt. A CLAUDE.md that exceeds ~4,000 tokens starts competing with the diff or code being reviewed. Keep CLAUDE.md focused: use headings and bullet points, not prose paragraphs. If your standards document is long, maintain a concise CLAUDE.md that references the canonical document by path, and include only the actionable rules inline.

sections.heading: 4. Independent Review Instances: Session Isolation and Cognitive Bias
sections.body_markdown:
One of the most architecturally significant decisions in a CI review pipeline is whether to use the same Claude session that generated the code to also review it. The answer, for production systems, is: **never**.

### Why Same-Session Review Fails

When Claude generates code in a session, the session context contains:
- The original intent and constraints that shaped the generation.
- Implicit assumptions made during generation.
- The "reasoning path" that led to the current implementation.
