$$n \approx \frac{(z_{\alpha} \sqrt{p_0(1-p_0)} + z_{\beta} \sqrt{p(1-p)})^2}{(p - p_0)^2}$$

For $p_0 = 0.995$, $p = 0.998$, this requires $n \approx 3{,}800$ — a reminder that validating critical fields is expensive and must be planned for.

**Edge case: distribution shift between validation and production.** A segment that passes validation may degrade in production if the document distribution shifts (new vendors, new form versions, seasonal patterns). Validation is a point-in-time gate; ongoing sampling (Section 5) is the continuous guard.

**Connection to adjacent topics.** This validation protocol is structurally identical to A/B testing a new model version: you are testing whether the model's accuracy on each segment exceeds a minimum bar. The same statistical machinery — power analysis, confidence intervals, multiple-comparison correction when testing many segments simultaneously — applies directly.

sections.heading: 5. Sampling for Ongoing Measurement and Novel Pattern Detection
sections.body_markdown:
Passing the initial validation gate does not mean a segment stays accurate forever. Ongoing stratified sampling of *high-confidence* extractions is the production mechanism for detecting drift, novel layouts, and silent model degradation.

**Why sample high-confidence extractions specifically?** Low-confidence extractions are already routed to human review (Section 7), so their error rate is observed directly. The blind spot is the high-confidence bucket: these extractions are automated and never seen by a human unless you deliberately sample them. If the model becomes overconfident on a new document variant, errors accumulate invisibly.

**Sampling cadence and volume.** A practical design:

| Stratum | Sample Rate | Review Cadence | Purpose |
|:--------|------------:|:--------------|:--------|
| High-confidence, critical fields | 2–5% | Daily | Catch silent errors fast |
| High-confidence, medium fields | 0.5–1% | Weekly | Trend monitoring |
| High-confidence, low fields | 0.1–0.2% | Monthly | Baseline drift detection |
| All confidence, new doc types | 20–100% | Per batch | Validate before trusting |

The sample rate for critical fields should be set so that, at current volume, you review enough examples per day to detect a 1% error rate increase with 80% power within one review cycle.

**Reservoir sampling for streaming pipelines.** In a high-throughput pipeline, you cannot buffer all extractions and then sample. Use reservoir sampling (Algorithm R) to maintain a fixed-size sample of $k$ items from a stream of unknown length $N$:

```python
import random

def reservoir_sample(stream, k: int) -> list:
    """Vitter's Algorithm R — O(N) time, O(k) space."""
    reservoir = []
    for i, item in enumerate(stream):
        if i < k:
            reservoir.append(item)
        else:
            j = random.randint(0, i)
            if j < k:
                reservoir[j] = item
    return reservoir
```

For stratified reservoir sampling, maintain a separate reservoir per stratum and route each incoming extraction to its stratum's reservoir.

**Novel pattern detection.** Beyond measuring known error rates, ongoing sampling surfaces *unknown unknowns*: document layouts the model has never seen, new field formats, or adversarial inputs. Reviewers should be instructed to flag not just errors but also *unusual* extractions — cases where the model was correct but for the wrong reason, or where the source document was ambiguous. These flags feed back into the training pipeline.

**Animated view: the sampling feedback loop.**

```svg
<svg viewBox="0 0 320 160" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Animated feedback loop: production extractions flow into sampling, then to review, then back to model improvement">
  <!-- Pipeline arrow -->
  <rect x="8" y="60" width="80" height="40" rx="8" fill="#6366f1"/>
  <text x="48" y="84" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#fff" font-family="sans-serif">Production</text>
  <text x="48" y="96" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#fff" font-family="sans-serif">Extractions</text>

  <rect x="120" y="60" width="80" height="40" rx="8" fill="#f59e0b"/>
  <text x="160" y="84" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#fff" font-family="sans-serif">Stratified</text>
  <text x="160" y="96" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#fff" font-family="sans-serif">Sample</text>

  <rect x="232" y="60" width="80" height="40" rx="8" fill="#22c55e"/>
  <text x="272" y="84" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#fff" font-family="sans-serif">Human</text>
  <text x="272" y="96" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#fff" font-family="sans-serif">Review</text>

  <!-- Forward arrows -->
  <line x1="88" y1="80" x2="118" y2="80" stroke="#334155" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#arr)"/>
  <line x1="200" y1="80" x2="230" y2="80" stroke="#334155" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#arr)"/>

  <!-- Feedback arrow (curved, bottom) -->
  <path d="M 272 100 Q 272 140 160 140 Q 48 140 48 100" fill="none" stroke="#6366f1" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="5 3" marker-end="url(#arr2)"/>
  <text x="160" y="155" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#6366f1" font-family="sans-serif">Error labels → retraining</text>

  <!-- Animated dot on feedback path -->
  <circle r="5" fill="#f59e0b">
    <animateMotion dur="4s" repeatCount="indefinite"
      path="M 272 100 Q 272 140 160 140 Q 48 140 48 100"/>
  </circle>

  <!-- Animated dot on forward path -->
  <circle r="5" fill="#22c55e">
    <animateMotion dur="4s" repeatCount="indefinite" begin="2s"
      path="M 88 80 L 118 80 L 200 80 L 230 80"/>
  </circle>

  <defs>
    <marker id="arr" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="8" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z" fill="#334155"/>
    </marker>
    <marker id="arr2" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="8" refX="2" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M8,0 L8,6 L0,3 z" fill="#6366f1"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
</svg>
```

The amber dot represents a sampled extraction flowing to review; the indigo dot represents error labels flowing back to improve the model.

sections.heading: 6. Analyzing Accuracy by Segment Before Reducing Human Review
sections.body_markdown:
Reducing human review volume is a business goal, but it must be earned segment by segment — not granted globally when the aggregate metric looks good. This section describes the analytical process and the governance structure around it.

**The segment accuracy matrix.** Before any reduction in review coverage, produce a full accuracy matrix:

| | Invoice Total | Vendor Name | Line-Item Price | Contract Date | Party Name |
|:---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Standard Invoice | 99.6% | 99.1% | 97.8% | — | — |
| Multi-page Contract | — | 98.2% | — | 95.1% | 94.3% |
| Handwritten Form | 91.0% | 88.4% | 82.1% | 87.0% | 85.2% |

Cells marked — indicate the field does not appear in that document type. Each cell must independently meet its risk-tier threshold before automation is approved for that cell.

**Decision rule.** The automation decision is a cell-wise AND, not a document-wise OR:

$$\text{Automate}(d, f) = \mathbf{1}[\text{Accuracy}(d, f) \geq \theta(f)] \quad \forall (d, f)$$

where $d$ is document type, $f$ is field, and $\theta(f)$ is the risk-tier threshold for field $f$.

In the matrix above, handwritten forms fail on every field — they should remain fully reviewed. Multi-page contracts fail on contract date and party name — those fields should remain reviewed even if other contract fields are automated.

**Governance: who approves the reduction?** Architectural judgment here is not purely technical. The decision to reduce review on a critical field (e.g., invoice total) should require sign-off from:
- The ML team (statistical validation)
- The business owner (risk acceptance)
- The compliance/audit function (regulatory exposure)

Document this decision with the validation data, the threshold rationale, and the ongoing sampling plan that will detect future degradation.

**Pitfall: Simpson's paradox in segment analysis.** When comparing accuracy before and after a model update, be alert to Simpson's paradox: the new model may appear better overall but be worse on a specific segment if the document mix changed between evaluation periods. Always compare segment-by-segment, controlling for document type distribution.

**Connection to SLA design.** Segment-level accuracy analysis directly informs SLA commitments. If you commit to 99% accuracy on invoice totals, you need per-segment evidence that the model meets 99% on every document type that contains invoice totals — not just on the dominant type.

sections.heading: 7. Calibrating Review Thresholds and Routing Low-Confidence Extractions
sections.body_markdown:
The routing decision — automate vs. send to human review — is a threshold applied to the calibrated field-level confidence score. Setting that threshold correctly is one of the most consequential architectural decisions in a document-processing pipeline.

**The precision-recall tradeoff for routing.** Think of routing as a binary classifier: "send to review" is the positive class. The threshold $\tau$ controls the tradeoff:

- **Lower $\tau$** → more extractions sent to review → higher recall of errors caught, but higher reviewer load.
- **Higher $\tau$** → fewer extractions sent to review → lower reviewer load, but more errors pass through undetected.

The optimal $\tau$ minimises a cost function:

$$C(\tau) = C_{\text{miss}} \cdot P(\text{error, not reviewed} \mid \tau) + C_{\text{review}} \cdot P(\text{reviewed} \mid \tau)$$

where $C_{\text{miss}}$ is the cost of an undetected error and $C_{\text{review}}$ is the cost of a human review. For critical fields, $C_{\text{miss}} \gg C_{\text{review}}$, so the threshold should be set high (more review). For low-risk fields, the balance shifts.

**Calibration procedure for threshold setting.**

1. On the labeled validation set, compute the calibrated confidence score for each extraction.
2. For each candidate threshold $\tau \in \{0.70, 0.75, \ldots, 0.99\}$, compute:
   - Review rate: fraction of extractions with calibrated score $< \tau$
   - Error escape rate: fraction of errors with calibrated score $\geq \tau$ (i.e., not sent to review)
3. Plot the review rate vs. error escape rate curve (analogous to a ROC curve).
4. Select $\tau$ at the operating point that meets the business's maximum acceptable error escape rate.

```python
import numpy as np

def threshold_analysis(calibrated_scores, is_correct, thresholds):
    results = []
    for tau in thresholds:
        reviewed = calibrated_scores < tau
        errors = ~is_correct
        review_rate = reviewed.mean()
        escaped_errors = (errors & ~reviewed).sum() / max(errors.sum(), 1)
        results.append({
            "threshold": tau,
            "review_rate": review_rate,
            "error_escape_rate": escaped_errors
        })
    return results
```

**Routing architecture.** The routing logic must handle more than just the confidence score. A well-designed router also escalates on:

- **Contradictory fields**: e.g., the extracted total does not equal the sum of line items.
- **Missing required fields**: a field that must be present is absent or blank.
- **Out-of-distribution signals**: the document's layout similarity score to the training distribution falls below a threshold.
- **Business rule violations**: extracted date is in the future, amount exceeds a plausible range.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Extracted document\nwith field-level scores]:::concept
    B{Calibrated confidence\n≥ threshold τ for all fields?}:::decision
    C{Business rules\npass?}:::decision
    D{Contradictions\nor missing fields?}:::decision
    E[Automate — write\nto downstream system]:::success
    F[Route to human review\nqueue — priority HIGH]:::warn
    G[Route to human review\nqueue — priority MEDIUM]:::warn
    A --> B
    B -->|No — low confidence| F
    B -->|Yes| C
    C -->|No — rule violation| F
    C -->|Yes| D
    D -->|Yes — contradiction| G
    D -->|No| E
```

**Prioritising limited reviewer capacity.** When review volume exceeds reviewer capacity, you need a priority queue. Rank items by:

$$\text{Priority}(i) = w_1 \cdot \text{RiskTier}(f_i) + w_2 \cdot (1 - \hat{p}_i) + w_3 \cdot \text{BusinessUrgency}(d_i)$$

where $\hat{p}_i$ is the calibrated confidence, $\text{RiskTier}$ encodes the field's criticality, and $\text{BusinessUrgency}$ encodes time sensitivity (e.g., invoices due today rank higher). This ensures that when reviewers are overloaded, the highest-risk errors are caught first.

**SLA for review queues.** Define maximum queue age by priority tier:

| Priority | Max Queue Age | Example Trigger |
|:---------|:-------------|:----------------|
| Critical | 2 hours | Low-confidence payment amount |
| High | 8 hours | Low-confidence contract date |
| Medium | 24 hours | Contradictory line items |
| Low | 72 hours | Missing optional field |

sections.heading: 8. Realistic Scenario and Exam-Style Questions
sections.body_markdown:
## Realistic Scenario: Accounts Payable Automation at a Mid-Size Manufacturer

A manufacturer processes approximately 12,000 invoices per month through an AI extraction pipeline. The pipeline extracts: vendor name, invoice date, invoice total, line-item descriptions, and line-item unit prices. Documents arrive as scanned PDFs (70%), email-attached PDFs (20%), and handwritten fax images (10%).

The ML team reports 96.8% overall extraction accuracy and recommends removing human review for all high-confidence extractions (calibrated score ≥ 0.90). The finance director asks the architect to evaluate this recommendation.

**Architect's analysis:**

1. **Decompose by segment.** The 96.8% aggregate masks the following:
   - Scanned PDFs: 99.1% accuracy on invoice total, 98.8% on unit price.
   - Email PDFs: 98.2% accuracy on invoice total, 97.1% on unit price.
   - Handwritten faxes: 89.3% accuracy on invoice total, **76.4%** on unit price.

2. **Apply risk-tier thresholds.** Invoice total and unit price are Critical fields (threshold: 99.5%). No segment meets this threshold. Even scanned PDFs at 99.1% fall short.

3. **Recommendation.** Do not remove human review globally. Instead:
   - For scanned and email PDFs: lower the review threshold to 0.97 (calibrated), which routes ~8% of extractions to review and achieves an estimated 99.6% accuracy on the automated portion.
   - For handwritten faxes: route 100% to human review until the model is fine-tuned on a labeled fax dataset.
   - Implement stratified sampling at 3% of high-confidence scanned/email extractions for ongoing monitoring.

4. **Capacity planning.** At 12,000 invoices/month, 8% review rate = 960 invoices/month for scanned/email + all ~1,200 handwritten faxes = ~2,160 reviews/month, or ~108 reviews per business day. This is feasible with 2 FTE reviewers at 60 reviews/day each.

---

## Exam-Style Questions

### Question 1

An AI invoice extraction pipeline reports 97.5% overall accuracy across 50,000 invoices per month. The team proposes automating all extractions with calibrated confidence ≥ 0.92. Which response best reflects sound architectural judgment?

**A.** Accept the proposal; 97.5% accuracy is above the industry standard of 95% for automated pipelines.

**B.** Accept the proposal but add a monthly audit of 100 randomly selected invoices to check for drift.

**C.** Reject the proposal until accuracy is validated separately for each document type and each critical field, with sample sizes sufficient for statistical significance.

**D.** Reject the proposal and require 99.9% overall accuracy before any automation is permitted.

**Correct answer: C.**

*Explanation:* The 97.5% aggregate may mask poor performance on specific document types or fields (e.g., handwritten forms, unit price fields). Option A ignores the masking problem. Option B uses simple random sampling, which under-samples rare strata. Option D sets an unrealistic blanket threshold that ignores risk tiering. Option C correctly requires per-segment validation with adequate statistical power before automation is approved.

---

### Question 2

A model outputs a raw confidence score of 0.94 for a "contract expiry date" extraction. The team has not performed calibration. What is the most accurate statement about using this score to set a review threshold?

**A.** The score is reliable; neural models are generally well-calibrated on structured fields.

**B.** The score may be overconfident; the empirical accuracy at raw score 0.94 could be significantly lower, and calibration against a labeled validation set is required before setting thresholds.

**C.** The score should be rounded to 1.0 for high-confidence fields to reduce reviewer load.

**D.** The score is only useful for ranking extractions, not for setting absolute thresholds, so calibration is unnecessary.

**Correct answer: B.**

*Explanation:* Neural extractors are typically overconfident, especially on out-of-distribution inputs. A raw score of 0.94 may correspond to an empirical accuracy of 80% or lower on certain document types. Calibration using isotonic regression or Platt scaling on a labeled validation set is required to make the score meaningful as a routing threshold. Option D is partially true (ranking is valid uncalibrated) but incorrect in concluding calibration is unnecessary for threshold setting.

---

### Question 3

A document-processing pipeline has been running for six months with stratified sampling of high-confidence extractions at 2% per stratum. The team observes that the error rate for the "purchase order number" field on scanned invoices has risen from 0.8% to 3.1% over the past three weeks, while the aggregate accuracy remains stable at 97.2%. What is the most appropriate immediate action?

**A.** Take no action; the aggregate metric is stable and the field is not critical.

**B.** Increase the overall review threshold from 0.92 to 0.95 across all document types and fields.

**C.** Investigate the "purchase order number" field on scanned invoices specifically — review recent extractions, check for new document layouts or format changes, and temporarily lower the automation threshold for that stratum until the root cause is identified.

**D.** Retrain the entire model on the full historical dataset to restore accuracy.

**Correct answer: C.**

*Explanation:* The stratum-specific spike (0.8% → 3.1%) is a classic signal of a novel error pattern — likely a new invoice layout or format change — that the aggregate metric masks entirely. The correct response is targeted: investigate and temporarily increase review for the affected stratum, not a blanket threshold change (B) which wastes reviewer capacity on unaffected strata, and not a full retrain (D) which is slow and may not address a layout-specific issue. Option A ignores a statistically significant signal (the increase exceeds the p-chart UCL) on a field that may affect downstream procurement matching.

key_takeaways: Aggregate accuracy metrics routinely mask critical failures on rare document types and specific fields; always decompose accuracy by document type × field before making automation decisions.

Stratified random sampling — not simple random sampling — is required to measure error rates with statistical power across heterogeneous document populations, and per-stratum p-charts provide early warning of novel error patterns.

Model confidence scores must be calibrated per field against labeled validation sets before they can be used as routing thresholds; uncalibrated scores are typically overconfident and will under-route errors to review.

Automation of a segment should be gated on a cell-wise accuracy check (every document type × field combination independently meets its risk-tier threshold), not on a document-level or aggregate check.

Ongoing stratified sampling of high-confidence extractions is the only mechanism for detecting silent model degradation and novel layouts after deployment — the initial validation gate is not sufficient.
When reviewer capacity is limited, a priority queue ranked by field risk tier, calibrated confidence deficit, and business urgency ensures the highest-risk errors are caught first.
further_reading: Active learning and uncertainty sampling — how to select the most informative examples for labeling from the human review queue to improve model calibration efficiently.
Statistical process control for ML systems — applying Shewhart control charts (p-charts, CUSUM) to per-stratum error rates for production monitoring.
Conformal prediction — a distribution-free framework for constructing prediction sets with guaranteed coverage, as an alternative to calibrated point confidence scores.
Human-in-the-loop system design — queue management, reviewer interface design, and inter-annotator agreement measurement for production review workflows.
Cost-sensitive learning — training models with asymmetric loss functions that reflect the business cost of errors on critical fields, as a complement to post-hoc routing thresholds.

ts-5-6.title: 5.6 Preserve information provenance and handle uncertainty in multi-source synthesis

lesson.topic: 5.6 Preserve information provenance and handle uncertainty in multi-source synthesis
lesson.synopsis: Multi-source synthesis pipelines routinely destroy the provenance metadata that makes claims trustworthy: summarization compresses findings without preserving who said what, conflicting statistics get silently resolved, and temporal differences masquerade as contradictions. This lesson equips architects with the structural patterns—claim-source mappings, conflict annotations, temporal tagging, and content-type-aware rendering—needed to build synthesis agents that remain epistemically honest from retrieval through final output. The focus is on production tradeoffs and the architectural judgment required to design pipelines that surface uncertainty rather than hide it.
learning_objectives: Explain how summarization steps destroy source attribution and design pipeline contracts that prevent provenance loss.
Design structured claim-source mapping schemas that subagents emit and synthesis agents preserve through merging.
Apply conflict-annotation patterns to handle contradictory statistics from credible sources without arbitrary resolution.
Architect temporal tagging requirements that prevent stale or mismatched data from being misinterpreted as factual contradictions.
Distinguish architectural strategies for rendering heterogeneous content types (financial tables, news prose, technical lists) appropriately in synthesis outputs.

sections.heading: 1. How Summarization Destroys Attribution
sections.body_markdown:
The single most common provenance failure in multi-agent pipelines is **attribution loss during summarization**: a subagent retrieves a document, extracts a finding, and returns a plain-text summary that strips the claim from its source. By the time the synthesis agent combines findings from five subagents, the output reads as a confident, unified narrative with no indication of which claim came from which source, how credible each source is, or whether two claims that appear complementary actually contradict each other.

### The Compression Trap

Consider a research pipeline with three document-analysis subagents:

| Subagent | Raw finding | Naïve summary returned |
|:---------|:------------|:-----------------------|
| Agent A | WHO report (2023): global obesity prevalence 39% in adults | "Obesity affects roughly 39% of adults globally." |
| Agent B | CDC dataset (2021): US adult obesity 41.9% | "In the US, obesity prevalence is about 42%." |
| Agent C | Lancet meta-analysis (2022): age-standardised global rate 27% | "A meta-analysis puts global obesity at 27%." |

A synthesis agent receiving these three plain-text strings faces an impossible task: the 39% and 27% figures appear contradictory, but the contradiction is methodological (age-standardised vs. crude prevalence), not factual. Without source metadata, the synthesis agent cannot know this. It will either pick one number, average them, or flag a vague "sources disagree" note—all of which are epistemically dishonest.

### Why This Happens Architecturally

The root cause is a **pipeline contract failure**: the interface between document-analysis subagents and the synthesis agent is defined as `string → string`, so structured metadata has nowhere to go. Architects who treat subagent outputs as opaque text blobs guarantee provenance loss. The fix is a typed output contract (see Section 3).

### Edge Case: Summarization of Summaries

In deep pipelines—where a mid-tier aggregator summarizes subagent outputs before passing them to a top-level synthesizer—attribution can be lost *twice*. Each compression step is a lossy transformation. Architects must enforce provenance preservation at every layer boundary, not just at the final synthesis step.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  D1[Document A]:::concept --> SA[Subagent 1\nsummarizes]:::process
  D2[Document B]:::concept --> SB[Subagent 2\nsummarizes]:::process
  SA -->|plain text| AGG[Mid-tier aggregator\nre-summarizes]:::process
  SB -->|plain text| AGG
  AGG -->|plain text| SYN[Synthesis agent]:::process
  SYN --> OUT[Final output\nno attribution]:::warn
```

Each arrow labeled "plain text" is a provenance destruction point. The architectural remedy is to replace every plain-text interface with a structured claim-source mapping.

sections.heading: 2. Structured Claim-Source Mappings
sections.body_markdown:
The foundational fix for attribution loss is requiring every subagent to emit a **structured claim-source mapping** rather than a free-text summary. This is a typed data contract that binds each extracted claim to its origin metadata, making provenance a first-class citizen of the pipeline.

### Minimum Viable Schema

A claim-source mapping must carry at minimum:

| Field | Type | Purpose |
|:------|:-----|:--------|
| `claim_id` | UUID | Stable reference for downstream agents |
| `claim_text` | string | The extracted assertion, verbatim or minimally paraphrased |
| `source_url` | string \| null | Canonical URL or DOI of the source document |
| `document_name` | string | Human-readable title or filename |
| `relevant_excerpt` | string | The exact passage supporting the claim |
| `publication_date` | ISO 8601 date | When the source was published or data collected |
| `confidence` | enum: high/medium/low | Subagent's assessed reliability of the claim |
| `methodology_note` | string \| null | Key methodological context (e.g., "age-standardised") |

### Example Output Contract

```python
# Subagent output — typed dataclass (Python pseudocode)
@dataclass
class ClaimSourceMapping:
    claim_id: str          # UUID4
    claim_text: str
    source_url: str | None
    document_name: str
    relevant_excerpt: str
    publication_date: str  # ISO 8601, e.g. "2023-04-15"
    confidence: Literal["high", "medium", "low"]
    methodology_note: str | None = None
    conflict_flag: bool = False
    conflict_note: str | None = None
```

The synthesis agent receives a **list** of these objects, not a string. It merges them by topic, preserving all fields, and emits a synthesis object that carries the full provenance graph.

### Merging Without Losing Provenance

When the synthesis agent combines claims from multiple subagents, it must **never flatten** a list of `ClaimSourceMapping` objects into a single string. Instead, it groups claims by topic and produces a `SynthesizedTopic` that retains the source list:

```python
@dataclass
class SynthesizedTopic:
    topic_label: str
    narrative_summary: str          # prose for human readers
    supporting_claims: list[ClaimSourceMapping]  # full provenance preserved
    conflicting_claims: list[ClaimSourceMapping] # flagged separately
    established_consensus: bool
```

This design means the final rendering layer can always trace any sentence in `narrative_summary` back to a specific `ClaimSourceMapping` with its `source_url` and `relevant_excerpt`.

### Production Tradeoff: Schema Rigidity vs. Flexibility

A strict schema catches provenance failures at the type level but increases subagent implementation cost and can break when documents have unusual structures (e.g., oral testimony transcripts with no publication date). The architectural judgment call is to make `source_url` and `publication_date` nullable with explicit `null` semantics ("source has no URL" vs. "URL was not retrieved") rather than omitting them. Null is honest; absence is ambiguous.

sections.heading: 3. Handling Conflicting Statistics from Credible Sources
sections.body_markdown:
When two credible sources report different values for the same quantity, the worst architectural response is silent selection—picking one number and discarding the other. The correct response is **conflict annotation**: preserving both values, attributing each to its source, and surfacing the conflict explicitly so that downstream agents or human reviewers can make an informed reconciliation decision.

### Anatomy of a Statistical Conflict

Statistical conflicts between credible sources typically arise from one of four causes:

| Conflict type | Example | Resolution strategy |
|:--------------|:--------|:--------------------|
| Methodological difference | Age-standardised vs. crude rate | Annotate methodology; present both |
| Temporal difference | 2019 data vs. 2023 data | Tag dates; prefer recency if comparable |
| Population scope | Global vs. national | Annotate scope; do not average |
| Measurement instrument | Self-reported vs. clinical measurement | Flag instrument; note direction of bias |

### The Annotation Pattern

Rather than resolving the conflict, the subagent (or synthesis agent) annotates it:

```python
conflict_group = ConflictGroup(
    topic="Global adult obesity prevalence",
    claims=[
        ClaimSourceMapping(
            claim_text="Global adult obesity prevalence is 39%",
            document_name="WHO Global Health Observatory 2023",
            publication_date="2023-01-01",
            methodology_note="Crude prevalence, self-reported BMI",
            conflict_flag=True,
            conflict_note="Differs from Lancet 2022 due to age-standardisation"
        ),
        ClaimSourceMapping(
            claim_text="Age-standardised global obesity rate is 27%",
            document_name="Lancet meta-analysis 2022",
            publication_date="2022-09-01",
            methodology_note="Age-standardised, clinical measurement",
            conflict_flag=True,
            conflict_note="Lower than WHO figure due to age-standardisation method"
        )
    ],
    reconciliation_status="unresolved",
    coordinator_note="Recommend presenting both figures with methodology context."
)
```

### Letting the Coordinator Decide

A critical architectural principle: **document-analysis subagents should complete their work with conflicting values included and explicitly annotated, then pass the annotated conflict to the coordinator agent for reconciliation decisions before synthesis.** Subagents should not resolve conflicts unilaterally—they lack the cross-source context to do so correctly.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  SA[Subagent A\nfinds 39%]:::process --> CA[Conflict Annotator]:::process
  SB[Subagent B\nfinds 27%]:::process --> CA
  CA -->|ConflictGroup| CO[Coordinator agent]:::decision
  CO -->|reconciliation policy| SY[Synthesis agent]:::process
  SY --> OUT[Output with\nboth values cited]:::success
  CO -->|escalate| HR[Human reviewer]:::warn
```

### Edge Case: Three-Way Conflicts

When three or more credible sources disagree, the conflict annotation must capture the full distribution, not just a pairwise comparison. The coordinator can then apply a reconciliation policy such as:
- **Majority rule** (only appropriate when methodologies are comparable)
- **Most recent** (only appropriate when the quantity is time-varying)
- **Methodologically superior** (requires explicit quality ranking)
- **Present all** (always safe; preferred when stakes are high)

Architects should encode the reconciliation policy as a configurable parameter of the coordinator agent, not as hardcoded logic inside subagents.

### What Not to Do

> The temptation to average conflicting statistics is almost always wrong. Averaging a crude rate and an age-standardised rate produces a number that corresponds to no real-world quantity.

Silent averaging is the statistical equivalent of fabrication. Flag it as an anti-pattern in code review checklists.

sections.heading: 4. Temporal Data: Tagging, Interpretation, and Preventing False Contradictions
sections.body_markdown:
Temporal differences between sources are the most common source of **false contradictions** in synthesis pipelines. A 2019 unemployment figure and a 2023 unemployment figure for the same country are not contradictory—they describe different moments in time. But a synthesis agent that receives both as plain text, without dates, will flag them as conflicting and either suppress one or produce a confused narrative.

### The Mandatory Date Fields

Every subagent output must include two temporal fields:

| Field | Semantics | Example |
|:------|:----------|:--------|
| `publication_date` | When the source document was published | `"2023-06-15"` |
| `data_collection_date` | When the underlying data was collected | `"2022-01-01"` to `"2022-12-31"` |

These are distinct. A 2024 report may analyse 2019 survey data. Using `publication_date` as a proxy for data currency is a common and consequential error.

### Temporal Interpretation Logic

The synthesis agent must apply temporal reasoning before flagging a conflict:

```python
def is_temporal_conflict(claim_a: ClaimSourceMapping,
                         claim_b: ClaimSourceMapping) -> bool:
    """
    Returns False if the claims describe different time periods
    (i.e., the difference is temporal, not factual).
    """
    date_a = parse_date(claim_a.data_collection_date)
    date_b = parse_date(claim_b.data_collection_date)
    # If data collection periods don't overlap, it's a time series, not a conflict
    if abs((date_a - date_b).days) > TEMPORAL_CONFLICT_THRESHOLD_DAYS:
        return False  # temporal difference, not contradiction
    return True  # same period, genuine conflict
```

The threshold `TEMPORAL_CONFLICT_THRESHOLD_DAYS` is a configurable architectural parameter. For slowly-changing quantities (e.g., population), 365 days may be appropriate. For rapidly-changing quantities (e.g., daily active users), even 30 days may be too large.

### Animated: Temporal Data Flow Through a Pipeline

```svg
<svg viewBox="0 0 320 160" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Temporal data flowing through a synthesis pipeline with date tags preserved">
  <!-- Background -->
  <rect width="320" height="160" fill="#0f172a" rx="8"/>
  <!-- Source boxes -->
  <rect x="8" y="20" width="72" height="36" rx="5" fill="#1e293b" stroke="#6366f1" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="44" y="34" text-anchor="middle" fill="#a5b4fc" font-size="8" font-family="monospace">Source A</text>
  <text x="44" y="48" text-anchor="middle" fill="#f59e0b" font-size="7" font-family="monospace">data: 2019</text>
  <rect x="8" y="70" width="72" height="36" rx="5" fill="#1e293b" stroke="#6366f1" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="44" y="84" text-anchor="middle" fill="#a5b4fc" font-size="8" font-family="monospace">Source B</text>
  <text x="44" y="98" text-anchor="middle" fill="#f59e0b" font-size="7" font-family="monospace">data: 2023</text>
  <!-- Arrows -->
  <line x1="80" y1="38" x2="120" y2="60" stroke="#334155" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="80" y1="88" x2="120" y2="80" stroke="#334155" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <!-- Synthesis box -->
  <rect x="120" y="50" width="80" height="40" rx="5" fill="#1e293b" stroke="#22c55e" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="160" y="67" text-anchor="middle" fill="#86efac" font-size="8" font-family="monospace">Synthesis</text>
  <text x="160" y="81" text-anchor="middle" fill="#86efac" font-size="7" font-family="monospace">dates preserved</text>
  <!-- Output box -->
  <rect x="228" y="50" width="80" height="40" rx="5" fill="#1e293b" stroke="#22c55e" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="268" y="64" text-anchor="middle" fill="#86efac" font-size="8" font-family="monospace">Output</text>
  <text x="268" y="76" text-anchor="middle" fill="#f59e0b" font-size="7" font-family="monospace">2019 vs 2023</text>
  <text x="268" y="86" text-anchor="middle" fill="#86efac" font-size="7" font-family="monospace">= time series</text>
  <!-- Animated packet A -->
  <circle r="5" fill="#6366f1">
    <animateMotion dur="3s" repeatCount="indefinite" begin="0s"
      path="M 80 38 L 120 60 L 200 70 L 228 70"/>
  </circle>
  <!-- Animated packet B -->
  <circle r="5" fill="#f59e0b">
    <animateMotion dur="3s" repeatCount="indefinite" begin="1.5s"
      path="M 80 88 L 120 80 L 200 70 L 228 70"/>
  </circle>
  <!-- Label -->
  <text x="160" y="140" text-anchor="middle" fill="#64748b" font-size="7" font-family="sans-serif">Date tags travel with claims — no false contradiction</text>
</svg>
```

### Production Tradeoff: Strict vs. Lenient Date Requirements

Making `data_collection_date` a required field will cause subagents to fail on sources that don't report collection dates (e.g., undated web pages, legacy PDFs). The architectural options are:

1. **Strict**: Reject sources without dates. High provenance quality; reduces corpus coverage.
2. **Lenient with explicit null**: Accept null dates but flag the claim as `temporal_confidence: low`. Broader coverage; requires downstream agents to handle uncertainty.
3. **Inferred date**: Use heuristics (document metadata, copyright year, URL date patterns) to estimate a date, flagged as `date_inferred: true`. Pragmatic but introduces estimation error.

For production systems, option 2 or 3 is usually correct. Option 1 is appropriate only when temporal precision is a hard requirement (e.g., financial regulatory reporting).

sections.heading: 5. Distinguishing Established from Contested: Report Structure
sections.body_markdown:
A synthesis report that presents well-established consensus findings and actively contested claims in the same undifferentiated prose is epistemically misleading. Architects must design synthesis agents to produce **structurally differentiated outputs** that make the epistemic status of each claim explicit.

### The Two-Section Pattern

The minimum viable structure for any synthesis report on a topic with mixed epistemic status:

```
Report: [Topic]

## Established Findings
[Claims with high source agreement, strong methodology, replicated results]
Each claim: text + source list + methodology note

## Contested or Uncertain Findings
[Claims where credible sources disagree, methodology is disputed,
or evidence base is thin]
Each claim: text + all conflicting sources + nature of disagreement

## Data Gaps
[Questions the retrieved sources do not answer]
```

### Preserving Original Source Characterizations

A critical subtlety: synthesis agents must preserve how the **original source** characterizes the certainty of its own claims. If a source says "preliminary evidence suggests" or "the authors note significant limitations," that hedging must survive into the synthesis output. Stripping hedges to produce cleaner prose is a form of misrepresentation.

| Source says | Synthesis must NOT say | Synthesis must say |
|:------------|:-----------------------|:-------------------|
| "Preliminary evidence suggests X" | "X is the case" | "Preliminary evidence suggests X [Source]" |
| "Results were not statistically significant" | "The study found X" | "The study found X, though results were not statistically significant [Source]" |
| "This is a contested area" | "Experts agree that X" | "This remains contested; [Source A] argues X while [Source B] argues Y" |

### Preserving Methodological Context

Methodological context is not a footnote—it is often the most important information in a synthesis. The `methodology_note` field in the claim-source mapping schema (Section 2) exists precisely to carry this context through the pipeline. The synthesis agent must surface it, not suppress it.

**Example**: A synthesis on the effectiveness of a drug intervention must distinguish:
- RCT evidence (high internal validity, potentially limited generalizability)
- Observational evidence (broader population, confounding risk)
- Meta-analytic evidence (depends on included study quality)

Collapsing these into "studies show the drug is effective" destroys the information that a clinician or policymaker needs to act.

### Edge Case: Contested by Whom?

Not all contestation is equal. A claim contested by 95% of domain experts and 5% of outliers should be characterized differently from a claim where the expert community is genuinely split 50/50. The synthesis agent should, where possible, annotate the **distribution of expert opinion**, not just the existence of disagreement. This requires subagents to retrieve and report on the broader literature, not just individual documents.

sections.heading: 6. Annotating Conflicts for the Coordinator Agent
sections.body_markdown:
In a multi-agent architecture, the **coordinator agent** is the appropriate locus for reconciliation decisions—not the document-analysis subagents, and not the synthesis agent. This separation of concerns is a key architectural principle: subagents have deep knowledge of individual documents; the coordinator has cross-source context; the synthesis agent has narrative construction capability. Reconciliation requires cross-source context, so it belongs to the coordinator.

### The Subagent's Responsibility

A document-analysis subagent's job is to **complete its analysis fully and honestly**, including values that conflict with what other subagents may have found. It must not:
- Suppress a finding because it seems inconsistent with the task framing
- Silently select one of two conflicting values from within its own document
- Defer its analysis pending resolution of a conflict it cannot see

It must:
- Extract all relevant claims from its assigned document
- Flag any internal inconsistencies within that document
- Emit a complete `ClaimSourceMapping` list with `conflict_flag: true` on any claim it knows (from its context) may conflict with other sources

### The Coordinator's Reconciliation Interface

```python
@dataclass
class ReconciliationRequest:
    conflict_group: ConflictGroup
    reconciliation_options: list[str]  # e.g., ["present_all", "prefer_recent", "prefer_rct"]
    coordinator_policy: str            # configured policy name
    escalate_to_human: bool

@dataclass
class ReconciliationDecision:
    conflict_group_id: str
    decision: str                      # e.g., "present_all"
    rationale: str
    resolved_claim: ClaimSourceMapping | None  # if a single value was chosen
    retained_conflicts: list[ClaimSourceMapping]  # conflicts to surface in output
```

### Coordinator Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  IN[Receive ConflictGroup\nfrom subagents]:::concept --> POL{Apply\nreconciliation policy}:::decision
  POL -->|temporal difference| TD[Tag as time series\nretain both]:::success
  POL -->|methodological difference| MD[Annotate methodology\nretain both]:::success
  POL -->|scope difference| SD[Annotate scope\nretain both]:::success
  POL -->|genuine factual conflict| GF{Escalate?}:::decision
  GF -->|yes| HR[Human reviewer]:::warn
  GF -->|no| PA[Present all\nwith attribution]:::success
  TD --> SY[Pass to synthesis agent]:::process
  MD --> SY
  SD --> SY
  PA --> SY
  HR -->|decision returned| SY
```

### Production Tradeoff: Latency vs. Epistemic Honesty

Routing every conflict through a coordinator adds latency. For high-throughput pipelines, architects face a tradeoff:

| Strategy | Latency | Epistemic quality | Risk |
|:---------|--------:|:-----------------:|:----|
| Always escalate to coordinator | High | Highest | Bottleneck |
| Escalate only high-confidence conflicts | Medium | High | May miss subtle conflicts |
| Subagent resolves low-stakes conflicts | Low | Medium | Silent resolution errors |
| Synthesis agent resolves all | Lowest | Lowest | Systematic provenance loss |

The recommended default for production systems handling consequential decisions (medical, legal, financial) is **always escalate to coordinator**. For lower-stakes summarization tasks, escalating only conflicts where `confidence: high` on both sides is a reasonable compromise.

sections.heading: 7. Rendering Content Types Appropriately in Synthesis Outputs
sections.body_markdown:
A synthesis pipeline that converts all content to a uniform format—typically undifferentiated prose—destroys the structural information that makes different content types useful. Financial data is best understood as a table; breaking news is best understood as prose; technical findings are best understood as structured lists. Architects must design synthesis agents to **render content type-appropriately**, not uniformly.

### Content Type Taxonomy

| Content type | Optimal rendering | Why |
|:-------------|:-----------------|:----|
| Financial data (prices, rates, ratios) | GFM table with right-aligned numeric columns | Enables comparison; preserves precision |
| News and narrative events | Prose paragraphs with inline citations | Preserves temporal flow and context |
| Technical findings (benchmarks, specs) | Structured lists or tables | Enables scanning; preserves hierarchy |
| Statistical comparisons | Table + methodology annotation | Prevents apples-to-oranges comparison |
| Legal or regulatory text | Block-quoted excerpt + prose interpretation | Preserves exact wording; separates interpretation |
| Conflicting claims | Side-by-side table or annotated list | Makes disagreement visible |

### Example: Financial Data Rendered as Table

A synthesis agent combining interest rate data from three central bank sources should produce:

| Central Bank | Policy Rate | Effective Date | Source | Publication Date |
|:-------------|------------:|:--------------:|:-------|:----------------|
| US Federal Reserve | 5.25% | 2023-07-26 | Fed Press Release | 2023-07-26 |
| European Central Bank | 4.00% | 2023-09-14 | ECB Monetary Policy Statement | 2023-09-14 |
| Bank of England | 5.25% | 2023-08-03 | MPC Minutes | 2023-08-03 |

Not: "Interest rates are around 4–5% across major central banks." That prose rendering loses precision, comparability, and temporal attribution.

### Example: Technical Findings as Structured List

A synthesis of GPU benchmark results should produce:

**GPU Performance Comparison (FP32 TFLOPS, as reported)**
- **Model A** — 82.6 TFLOPS *(Source: Manufacturer datasheet, 2023-10-01; peak theoretical)*
- **Model B** — 79.4 TFLOPS *(Source: Independent benchmark, 2023-11-15; sustained throughput)*
- ⚠️ **Methodology conflict**: Manufacturer peak vs. independent sustained — not directly comparable. See conflict annotation C-007.

Not: "Model A and Model B have similar performance around 80 TFLOPS."

### Implementing Content-Type-Aware Rendering

The synthesis agent must know the content type of each `ClaimSourceMapping` to render it correctly. Add a `content_type` field to the schema:

```python
content_type: Literal[
    "financial_data",
    "news_narrative",
    "technical_finding",
    "statistical_comparison",
    "legal_text",
    "conflicting_claim"
]
```

The rendering layer then dispatches on `content_type`:

```python
def render_claim(claim: ClaimSourceMapping) -> str:
    match claim.content_type:
        case "financial_data":
            return render_as_table_row(claim)
        case "news_narrative":
            return render_as_prose_with_citation(claim)
        case "technical_finding":
            return render_as_list_item(claim)
        case "conflicting_claim":
            return render_as_annotated_conflict(claim)
        case _:
            return render_as_prose_with_citation(claim)  # safe default
```

### Edge Case: Mixed Content in a Single Topic

A synthesis on "central bank policy and its economic effects" may combine financial tables (rate decisions), news prose (market reactions), and technical findings (econometric model outputs). The synthesis agent must not force all three into a single format. The correct output is a **composite document** with clearly labeled sections, each rendered in its appropriate format, with cross-references between sections preserving the claim-source graph.

### Production Tradeoff: Rendering Complexity vs. Output Consistency

Content-type-aware rendering increases synthesis agent complexity and makes output format less predictable for downstream consumers. Architects must weigh this against the epistemic cost of uniform rendering. The recommended approach is to define a **rendering contract** as part of the pipeline specification: downstream consumers receive a structured document object (not a string), and the rendering layer is a separate, configurable component that can be swapped without changing the synthesis logic.

sections.heading: 8. Realistic Scenario and Exam-Style Questions
sections.body_markdown:
## Realistic Scenario: Multi-Source Healthcare Policy Brief

Your team is building a multi-agent pipeline to produce a policy brief on childhood vaccination coverage. The pipeline has:
- **4 document-analysis subagents** (WHO reports, CDC data, peer-reviewed journals, news articles)
- **1 coordinator agent** (reconciles conflicts, applies policy)
- **1 synthesis agent** (produces the final brief)
- **1 rendering agent** (formats output for policymakers)

**What goes wrong in a naïve implementation:**

1. Subagent 1 returns: *"Global childhood vaccination coverage is 83%."* (WHO 2022, crude coverage)
2. Subagent 2 returns: *"Vaccination coverage has declined to 67% in low-income countries."* (UNICEF 2023, LIC-specific)
3. Subagent 3 returns: *"A 2019 Lancet study found 89% coverage for DTP3."* (pre-COVID, specific antigen)
4. Subagent 4 returns: *"News reports indicate vaccine hesitancy is rising."* (no date, no source)

The synthesis agent, receiving plain text, produces: *"Vaccination coverage ranges from 67% to 89%, with rising hesitancy."* This is misleading: the figures are not comparable (global vs. LIC, different antigens, different years), and the hesitancy claim is unsourced.

**What a well-architected implementation does:**

- Each subagent emits a `ClaimSourceMapping` with `publication_date`, `data_collection_date`, `methodology_note`, and `content_type`.
- The coordinator identifies that the 83%, 67%, and 89% figures are not contradictory—they describe different populations, antigens, and time periods. It annotates each with scope and temporal context.
- The synthesis agent produces a structured brief with an **Established Findings** section (global coverage trend), a **Contested/Uncertain** section (hesitancy magnitude), and a **Data Gaps** section (post-2023 LIC data).
- The rendering agent formats coverage statistics as a table, narrative context as prose, and the hesitancy claim as an annotated uncertain finding with its source flagged as `temporal_confidence: low`.

---

## Exam-Style Questions

### Question 1

A document-analysis subagent retrieves two statistics from the same credible source: a 2020 figure of 4.2% unemployment and a 2023 figure of 3.7% unemployment. The subagent's task is to report on current unemployment. Which action is most architecturally correct?

**A.** Return only the 2023 figure, as it is more recent and the task asks for current data.

**B.** Return both figures as a `ConflictGroup` with `conflict_flag: true`, annotated with their respective `data_collection_date` values, and let the coordinator decide.

**C.** Average the two figures and return 3.95% as a representative estimate.

**D.** Return both figures as plain text with a note that they disagree.

**Correct answer: A**

*Explanation:* This is a **temporal difference, not a conflict**. The two figures describe different points in time for a time-varying quantity. The subagent has sufficient context to determine that the 2023 figure is the appropriate one for a "current unemployment" task, and returning it with its `data_collection_date` is correct. Option B would be correct if the figures were from the same time period but different sources. Option C (averaging) is always wrong for time-series data. Option D loses structured metadata. Note: if the subagent is uncertain whether the task requires historical context, it should return both with temporal annotations—but for a clearly scoped "current" task, the recent figure with its date is the right answer.

---

### Question 2

A synthesis agent is combining findings from five subagents on the efficacy of a new antiviral drug. Three subagents report RCT evidence showing 68% efficacy; two subagents report observational studies showing 81% efficacy. The coordinator has not yet issued a reconciliation decision. What should the synthesis agent do?

**A.** Report 68% efficacy, preferring RCT evidence as methodologically superior.

**B.** Report 81% efficacy, as it represents the majority of retrieved values.

**C.** Wait for the coordinator's reconciliation decision before producing output.

**D.** Report both figures with methodology annotations, flagging the conflict for the coordinator.

**Correct answer: D**

*Explanation:* The synthesis agent should not resolve methodological conflicts unilaterally (ruling out A and B). Option C (waiting) is incorrect because the synthesis agent's role is to produce a draft output that the coordinator can review—not to block on coordinator input. The correct pattern is for the synthesis agent to produce output that **includes the conflict annotation**, preserving both values with their methodological context, so the coordinator can make an informed reconciliation decision. This is the "complete your work with conflicts annotated" principle from Section 6.

---

### Question 3

Your pipeline's rendering agent receives a `SynthesizedTopic` containing: (a) three years of quarterly revenue figures from SEC filings, (b) a narrative description of a product launch, and (c) a technical benchmark comparison of two competing products. Which rendering approach is most appropriate?

**A.** Render all three as prose paragraphs to produce a readable, uniform document.

**B.** Render all three as bullet lists for scannability.

**C.** Render revenue figures as a right-aligned numeric table, the product launch as prose with inline citations, and the benchmark comparison as a structured list with methodology annotations.

**D.** Render revenue figures as prose, since tables are harder to read in some output formats.

**Correct answer: C**

*Explanation:* Content-type-aware rendering (Section 7) requires matching the rendering format to the information structure. Revenue figures are tabular by nature—rendering them as prose loses comparability and precision. The product launch narrative has temporal flow best preserved in prose. The benchmark comparison requires structure and methodology annotation to prevent misinterpretation. Option A (uniform prose) is the most common anti-pattern. Option B (uniform lists) is better than A but still wrong for financial data. Option D sacrifices precision for format compatibility—the correct architectural response is to design the output format to support tables, not to degrade the data.

key_takeaways: Summarization steps are provenance destruction points: every plain-text interface between pipeline agents is an opportunity to lose claim-source attribution. Replace string interfaces with typed claim-source mapping schemas.

Structured claim-source mappings must carry source URL, document name, relevant excerpt, publication date, data collection date, methodology note, and content type — and synthesis agents must preserve these fields through merging, never flatten them.

Conflicting statistics from credible sources should never be silently resolved. Annotate conflicts with full source attribution and pass them to the coordinator agent for reconciliation; subagents and synthesis agents should not resolve cross-source conflicts unilaterally.

Temporal differences are not contradictions. Requiring `data_collection_date` (distinct from `publication_date`) in all subagent outputs enables synthesis agents to distinguish time-series data from genuine factual conflicts.

Synthesis reports must structurally distinguish established findings from contested ones, and must preserve original source hedging language — stripping uncertainty qualifiers to produce cleaner prose is a form of misrepresentation.

Content-type-aware rendering — financial data as tables, news as prose, technical findings as structured lists — is an architectural requirement, not a cosmetic choice. Uniform rendering destroys the structural information that makes different content types useful.
further_reading: Coordinator-agent design patterns: how to architect reconciliation policies as configurable, auditable components separate from synthesis logic.
Uncertainty quantification in LLM pipelines: probabilistic confidence scoring, calibration, and how to propagate uncertainty estimates through multi-step reasoning chains.
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) provenance: citation grounding techniques, chunk-level attribution, and faithfulness evaluation metrics for RAG outputs.
Knowledge graph integration for claim-source mapping: using structured knowledge representations to maintain provenance across complex multi-hop synthesis tasks.
Epistemic status communication in AI outputs: design patterns for surfacing confidence, contestation, and data quality to end users without overwhelming them with caveats.

ts-4-2.title: 4.2 Apply few-shot prompting to improve output consistency and quality

lesson.topic: 4.2 Apply few-shot prompting to improve output consistency and quality
lesson.synopsis: Few-shot prompting is the single most reliable lever for achieving consistent, production-grade output from large language models when detailed instructions alone produce drift or hallucination. This lesson examines the mechanics of few-shot design for extraction, classification, and code-review tasks — covering ambiguous-case handling, format enforcement, false-positive reduction, and varied document structures. Architectural tradeoffs (token cost, example selection, maintenance burden) are treated as first-class concerns throughout.
learning_objectives: Explain why few-shot examples outperform instruction-only prompts for output consistency and how to select examples that maximise generalisation.
Design 2–4 targeted few-shot examples for ambiguous scenarios that make the model's reasoning for choosing one action over plausible alternatives explicit.
Apply few-shot techniques to reduce extraction hallucination across varied document structures and output formats.
Distinguish between few-shot examples that teach acceptable patterns versus genuine issues, and articulate the production tradeoff between false-positive rate and recall.
Evaluate architectural tradeoffs — token budget, example maintenance, and retrieval-augmented selection — when deploying few-shot prompts at scale.

sections.heading: Why Few-Shot Beats Instructions Alone
sections.body_markdown:
Few-shot examples are the most effective technique for achieving consistently formatted, actionable output when detailed instructions alone produce inconsistent results. Instructions describe *what* to do; examples demonstrate *how* it looks when done correctly. The model's in-context learning mechanism treats each example as a local constraint that narrows the distribution of plausible completions far more tightly than prose rules.

## The Instruction-Only Failure Mode

Consider a code-review pipeline that must emit structured JSON:

```
Instruction: For each issue found, output a JSON object with keys:
  location, issue, severity (critical|high|medium|low), suggested_fix.
```

With only this instruction, production runs reveal:
- Severity values like `"CRITICAL"`, `"Critical"`, `"sev:critical"` — all semantically identical but schema-breaking.
- `suggested_fix` sometimes a one-liner, sometimes a multi-paragraph essay.
- `location` expressed as a line number, a function name, or a file path — inconsistently.

None of these failures are bugs in the instruction; they are *ambiguity in the instruction's implied format*. The model has seen thousands of valid JSON schemas and picks one plausible interpretation per call.

## Why Examples Resolve This

Each few-shot example acts as a **format anchor**. The model observes the exact casing, field ordering, verbosity level, and value vocabulary you intend, then continues that pattern. Empirically, two well-chosen examples reduce format-error rates more than doubling the length of the instruction block.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  A[Instruction only]:::concept --> B{Model samples\nfrom broad prior}:::decision
  B -->|High variance| C[Inconsistent output]:::warn
  A2[Instruction + few-shot]:::concept --> D{Model samples\nnear example distribution}:::decision
  D -->|Low variance| E[Consistent output]:::success
```

## Production Tradeoff: Token Cost vs. Consistency Gain

| Strategy | Avg. tokens/call | Format error rate | Maintenance cost |
|:---------|----------------:|------------------:|:----------------|
| Instruction only | ~200 | ~18 % | Low |
| 1 example | ~450 | ~9 % | Low |
| 2–3 examples | ~800 | ~3 % | Medium |
| 4–6 examples | ~1,400 | ~1 % | High |
| RAG-selected examples | ~900 | ~2 % | High |

The sweet spot for most production pipelines is **2–3 carefully chosen examples** — enough to anchor format and demonstrate ambiguous-case handling without ballooning context windows or retrieval complexity. Beyond 4 examples, marginal gains diminish rapidly while token cost grows linearly.

> The purpose of abstraction is not to be vague, but to create a new semantic level in which one can be absolutely precise.
> — Edsger W. Dijkstra

Few-shot examples are the mechanism by which you make your *intended* semantic level precise to the model.

sections.heading: Demonstrating Ambiguous-Case Handling
sections.body_markdown:
The highest-value use of few-shot examples is not the easy cases — it is the **ambiguous cases** where multiple reasonable actions exist and the model must learn your organisation's preferred judgment.

## What Makes a Case Ambiguous?

Ambiguity arises when:
1. The input matches more than one category or action with roughly equal surface plausibility.
2. The correct action depends on implicit domain knowledge (e.g., "this measurement is informal but valid").
3. The boundary between a pattern and a problem is context-dependent.

## Scenario: Tool Selection for Ambiguous Requests

Imagine a developer assistant that routes user requests to one of three tools: `code_search`, `documentation_lookup`, or `run_tests`. The request *"Why is the login failing?"* could plausibly trigger any of the three.

A few-shot example that shows the reasoning:

```
User request: "Why is the login failing?"
Reasoning: The request asks for a root cause, not a specific symbol or doc page.
  'run_tests' would surface failures but not explain them.
  'documentation_lookup' is appropriate only if the user references a specific API.
  'code_search' for recent changes to auth modules is the most targeted first step.
Chosen tool: code_search
Query: "auth login recent changes"
```

Without this example, the model might default to `run_tests` (the most literal interpretation of "failing") or `documentation_lookup` (the safest hedge). The example teaches the *decision hierarchy*: root-cause requests → code_search first.

## Scenario: Branch-Level Test Coverage Gaps

A coverage analysis tool must decide whether a missing branch is a **gap** (must be flagged) or an **acceptable omission** (e.g., defensive null-check on an always-non-null parameter). The ambiguous case:

```
Function: parse_config(path: str) -> dict
Uncovered branch: path is None → raises ValueError
Context: path is always supplied by the CLI argument parser, never None at runtime.

Reasoning: The branch is technically reachable but unreachable in practice given
  the call graph. Flagging it as a gap would create noise. However, the function
  is public API — external callers could pass None. Classify as LOW-priority gap,
  not omission, to preserve the signal for library consumers.
Classification: gap | severity: low
```

This example teaches the model to distinguish *runtime reachability* from *API contract reachability* — a nuance no instruction sentence captures cleanly.

## Design Principle: Show the Road Not Taken

The most powerful few-shot examples for ambiguous cases explicitly name the **plausible alternatives that were rejected** and explain why. This prevents the model from treating the chosen action as arbitrary and instead internalises the decision rule.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[Ambiguous input]:::concept --> B{Multiple plausible\nactions}:::decision
  B --> C[Action A — chosen]:::success
  B --> D[Action B — rejected:\nreason stated]:::warn
  B --> E[Action C — rejected:\nreason stated]:::warn
  C --> F[Model learns\ndecision hierarchy]:::process
```

sections.heading: Generalising to Novel Patterns
sections.body_markdown:
A common misconception is that few-shot examples only teach the model to handle the *exact* cases shown. In practice, well-designed examples enable the model to **generalise judgment to novel patterns** — provided the examples are chosen to expose the underlying decision rule rather than surface features.

## The Generalisation Mechanism

When the model processes few-shot examples, it is not performing lookup. It is inferring a latent rule from the examples and applying that rule to the new input. The quality of generalisation depends on:

1. **Diversity of examples** — examples that vary surface form but share the same underlying rule teach the rule, not the surface.
2. **Explicit reasoning traces** — showing *why* the answer is correct helps the model abstract the principle.
3. **Coverage of the decision boundary** — at least one example near the boundary between two categories is worth more than three examples deep in the easy region.

## Worked Case: Code Pattern Generalisation

Suppose you are building a static-analysis assistant that must flag unsafe string interpolation in SQL queries. You provide two examples:

**Example 1 (Python, f-string):**
```python
# Input code
query = f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = {user_id}"
cursor.execute(query)

# Output
{"location": "line 2", "issue": "Unsanitised variable interpolated into SQL string",
 "severity": "critical", "suggested_fix": "Use parameterised query: cursor.execute('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = %s', (user_id,))"}
```

**Example 2 (Python, % formatting):**
```python
# Input code
query = "DELETE FROM sessions WHERE token = '%s'" % token
cursor.execute(query)

# Output
{"location": "line 2", "issue": "Unsanitised variable interpolated into SQL string",
 "severity": "critical", "suggested_fix": "Use parameterised query: cursor.execute('DELETE FROM sessions WHERE token = %s', (token,))"}
```

With only these two examples, the model correctly flags a *novel* pattern it has never seen in the examples:

```python
# Novel input — string concatenation
query = "UPDATE accounts SET balance = " + str(amount) + " WHERE id = " + str(acct_id)
```

The model generalises because both examples share the rule *"runtime variable inserted into SQL string without parameterisation"*, not the surface feature *"f-string"*.

## Boundary Examples Are Disproportionately Valuable

Consider adding a **negative boundary example** — a case that *looks* like the issue but is not:

```python
# Input code — parameterised, safe
query = "SELECT * FROM products WHERE category = %s"
cursor.execute(query, (category,))

# Output
{"location": "line 1", "issue": null, "severity": null, "suggested_fix": null}
```

This boundary example prevents the model from flagging *all* SQL strings — it learns that the `%s` placeholder is safe when passed as a parameter tuple, not when substituted via Python's `%` string operator.

## Architectural Implication: Example Selection Strategy

For production pipelines with large example libraries, **retrieval-augmented few-shot selection** (embedding the current input and retrieving the $k$ nearest examples) outperforms static example sets when the input distribution is wide. The tradeoff:

| Selection method | Generalisation quality | Token cost | Latency overhead |
|:----------------|:----------------------:|----------:|:----------------|
| Static (hand-picked) | Good for narrow domains | Fixed | None |
| Random from pool | Poor | Fixed | None |
| Nearest-neighbour retrieval | Best for wide domains | Variable | +50–150 ms |
| Diversity-maximising retrieval | Best for boundary cases | Variable | +100–200 ms |

sections.heading: Reducing Extraction Hallucination
sections.body_markdown:
Extraction tasks — pulling structured fields from unstructured text — are among the highest-hallucination scenarios in production LLM pipelines. Few-shot examples are the most effective single intervention for reducing hallucination in extraction, particularly for **informal measurements**, **absent fields**, and **varied document structures**.

## Why Extraction Hallucinations Occur

The model's prior strongly favours *plausible* values over *absent* values. When a required field is missing from the source document, the model often fabricates a plausible value rather than returning `null`. This is not a reasoning failure — it is the model doing exactly what its training optimised for: producing fluent, coherent completions.

Few-shot examples that explicitly demonstrate `null` returns for absent fields recalibrate this prior within the context window.

## Scenario: Clinical Trial Document Extraction

You must extract `{trial_id, sample_size, primary_endpoint, dosage_mg}` from clinical trial summaries. Documents vary widely:

- Some state dosage formally: *"administered at 50 mg twice daily"*
- Some use informal language: *"about half a gram each morning"*
- Some omit dosage entirely

**Without few-shot examples**, the model:
- Converts *"about half a gram"* to `500` (correct but inconsistent with unit handling)
- Invents a dosage when none is stated (hallucination)
- Sometimes returns `"not specified"` as a string instead of `null` (schema violation)

**Few-shot example 1 — informal measurement:**
```
Document excerpt: "Participants received approximately half a gram of compound X each morning."
Extraction:
{
  "dosage_mg": 500,
  "dosage_note": "Converted from informal 'half a gram'; verify against protocol."
}
```

**Few-shot example 2 — absent field:**
```
Document excerpt: "The trial enrolled 240 participants across three sites. Primary endpoint was 12-week HbA1c reduction."
Extraction:
{
  "sample_size": 240,
  "primary_endpoint": "12-week HbA1c reduction",
  "dosage_mg": null
}
```

**Few-shot example 3 — ambiguous informal measurement:**
```
Document excerpt: "Subjects took 'a standard dose' of the investigational drug."
Extraction:
{
  "dosage_mg": null,
  "dosage_note": "'Standard dose' is not quantified in this document; cannot extract."
}
```

Example 3 is critical: it teaches the model that *vague qualitative language* does not justify a numeric guess, even when a number would be plausible.

## Hallucination Reduction: Mechanism

The few-shot examples establish three behavioural norms simultaneously:
1. **Conversion norm** — informal units are converted with a note.
2. **Null norm** — absent fields return `null`, not a fabricated value.
3. **Uncertainty norm** — ambiguous language returns `null` with an explanatory note.

Without all three norms demonstrated, the model will apply whichever norm its prior favours for each individual call — producing inconsistent behaviour across a document corpus.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  A[Source document]:::concept --> B{Field present?}:::decision
  B -->|Yes, formal| C[Extract directly]:::process
  B -->|Yes, informal| D[Convert + add note]:::process
  B -->|Vague/ambiguous| E[null + note]:::warn
  B -->|Absent| F[null]:::warn
  C --> G[Structured output]:::success
  D --> G
  E --> G
  F --> G
```

sections.heading: Crafting Examples That Show Chosen Reasoning
sections.body_markdown:
The most architecturally significant design decision in few-shot prompting is whether to include **explicit reasoning traces** in your examples. For ambiguous scenarios, showing the reasoning for why one action was chosen over plausible alternatives is not optional — it is the mechanism by which the model learns the decision rule rather than the decision outcome.

## Structure of a Reasoning-Bearing Example

A well-structured few-shot example for an ambiguous scenario has four parts:

1. **Input** — the ambiguous case, presented exactly as the model will receive real inputs.
2. **Reasoning** — a brief chain-of-thought that names the alternatives considered and explains the rejection criteria.
3. **Decision** — the chosen action, clearly labelled.
4. **Output** — the structured result in the exact format required.

## Four Targeted Examples for a Code-Review Pipeline

### Example A — Acceptable Pattern vs. Genuine Issue

```
Input code:
def get_user(user_id):
    return db.query(f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = {user_id}")

Reasoning:
  This is an f-string interpolating a variable directly into a SQL query.
  Alternative interpretation: user_id might be validated upstream.
  Rejection: Even with upstream validation, parameterised queries are the
    correct pattern; validation does not prevent second-order injection.
  Decision: Flag as genuine issue.

Output:
{
  "location": "get_user, line 2",
  "issue": "SQL injection risk via f-string interpolation",
  "severity": "critical",
  "suggested_fix": "Use db.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = %s', (user_id,))"
}
```

### Example B — Acceptable Pattern (No Issue)

```
Input code:
def get_user(user_id):
    return db.query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = %s", (user_id,))

Reasoning:
  The query uses a parameterised placeholder with a separate argument tuple.
  Alternative interpretation: the %s syntax could be confused with Python's
    string formatting operator.
  Rejection: The second argument is a tuple passed to the DB driver, not
    Python string formatting. This is the correct pattern.
  Decision: No issue.

Output:
{
  "location": "get_user, line 2",
  "issue": null,
  "severity": null,
  "suggested_fix": null
}
```

### Example C — Severity Ambiguity (High vs. Critical)

```
Input code:
def export_report(filename):
    path = "/reports/" + filename
    with open(path, 'w') as f:
        f.write(generate_report())

Reasoning:
  Path traversal risk if filename contains '../'. 
  Alternative severity: 'high' if this endpoint is internal-only.
  Rejection: The function signature gives no indication of caller scope;
    defaulting to 'critical' for path traversal is the conservative choice
    consistent with OWASP guidance.
  Decision: critical.

Output:
{
  "location": "export_report, line 2",
  "issue": "Path traversal via unsanitised filename concatenation",
  "severity": "critical",
  "suggested_fix": "Use os.path.basename(filename) and validate against an allowlist."
}
```

### Example D — Low-Signal Warning (Medium, Not High)

```
Input code:
def load_config():
    with open('config.json') as f:
        return json.load(f)

Reasoning:
  No error handling around file open or JSON parse.
  Alternative severity: 'high' if this is called at startup and failure
    would crash the service.
  Rejection: Missing error handling is a robustness issue, not a security
    vulnerability. Without evidence this is a startup-critical path,
    'medium' is appropriate.
  Decision: medium.

Output:
{
  "location": "load_config, line 2",
  "issue": "No exception handling for file-not-found or malformed JSON",
  "severity": "medium",
  "suggested_fix": "Wrap in try/except (FileNotFoundError, json.JSONDecodeError) and log or raise with context."
}
```

## Why Reasoning Traces Improve Generalisation

Reasoning traces do two things simultaneously:
- They make the **decision boundary** explicit, so the model can apply it to novel inputs that fall near the boundary.
- They prevent **spurious pattern matching** — without reasoning, the model might learn "f-strings → critical" as a surface rule, which would misfire on f-strings used in logging or non-SQL contexts.

The cost is approximately 30–60 additional tokens per example. For ambiguous-case pipelines, this is almost always worth it.

sections.heading: Format Enforcement and Varied Document Structures
sections.body_markdown:
Two of the most common production failure modes are **format drift** (the model gradually deviates from the required output schema) and **empty-field extraction** (required fields return null or are omitted when the document structure differs from the training distribution). Few-shot examples address both directly.

## Output Format Examples: The Four-Field Schema

For a code-review or audit pipeline, the canonical output schema is:

```json
{
  "location": "<file or function name, line number>",
  "issue": "<concise description>",
  "severity": "critical | high | medium | low",
  "suggested_fix": "<actionable one-liner or short snippet>"
}
```

A few-shot example that demonstrates this format must be **exact** — not approximately correct. Every field must appear in every example, including null-valued fields, so the model learns that omitting a field is never acceptable.

**Anti-pattern (omits null fields):**
```json
{"location": "auth.py:42", "issue": "Hardcoded secret", "severity": "critical"}
```

**Correct pattern (all fields present):**
```json
{
  "location": "auth.py:42",
  "issue": "Hardcoded API secret in source code",
  "severity": "critical",
  "suggested_fix": "Move to environment variable: os.environ['API_SECRET']"
}
```

## Handling Varied Document Structures

Document structure variation is the primary cause of empty-field extraction failures. Consider a literature review pipeline that must extract `{authors, year, methodology, sample_size}` from academic papers. Papers vary in structure:

| Structure type | Where methodology appears | Where sample size appears |
|:--------------|:--------------------------|:--------------------------|
| Standard IMRaD | Dedicated Methods section | Methods or Results |
| Review article | Embedded in Introduction | May be absent |
| Conference short paper | Inline with results | Often omitted |
| Preprint | Variable; sometimes abstract | Variable |

A single extraction prompt without few-shot examples will perform well on IMRaD papers (the dominant training distribution) and poorly on the others.

**Few-shot example — inline citation style (no bibliography section):**
```
Document excerpt:
"Building on Smith's 2019 cohort of 312 patients, we replicated the
 intervention using a modified protocol (see Appendix B for details)."

Extraction:
{
  "authors": null,
  "year": null,
  "methodology": "Replication with modified protocol; details in Appendix B",
  "sample_size": null,
  "extraction_note": "This excerpt references an external study; authors/year/sample_size
    belong to Smith 2019, not the current document."
}
```

**Few-shot example — methodology embedded in abstract:**
```
Document excerpt:
"Abstract: We surveyed 1,204 software engineers across 14 countries using
 a validated 42-item instrument. Results show..."

Extraction:
{
```
