```
  "methodology": "Survey, 42-item validated instrument",
  "sample_size": 1204,
  "sample_size_note": "Extracted from abstract; verify against Methods section."
}
```

**Few-shot example — methodology absent (short paper):**
```
Document excerpt:
"We present a new algorithm for graph partitioning and evaluate it on
 three benchmark datasets."

Extraction:
{
  "methodology": null,
  "sample_size": null,
  "extraction_note": "No methodology or sample size described in this excerpt."
}
```

These three examples teach the model to:
1. Distinguish *the current document's* data from *cited documents'* data.
2. Extract from non-standard locations (abstract, inline) with a provenance note.
3. Return `null` confidently when the field is genuinely absent.

## Animated: The Few-Shot Anchoring Effect

```svg
<svg viewBox="0 0 320 160" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Few-shot examples anchoring model output distribution">
  <!-- Wide prior distribution (no few-shot) -->
  <ellipse cx="80" cy="80" rx="60" ry="40" fill="#6366f1" fill-opacity="0.18" stroke="#6366f1" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="80" y="84" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#6366f1">Prior output</text>
  <text x="80" y="96" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#6366f1">distribution</text>
  <!-- Arrow -->
  <line x1="155" y1="80" x2="195" y2="80" stroke="#334155" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#arr)"/>
  <defs>
    <marker id="arr" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="8" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z" fill="#334155"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
  <text x="175" y="74" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#334155">+ few-shot</text>
  <!-- Narrow posterior distribution (with few-shot) -->
  <ellipse cx="250" cy="80" rx="28" ry="18" fill="#22c55e" fill-opacity="0.22" stroke="#22c55e" stroke-width="1.5">
    <animate attributeName="rx" values="28;22;28" dur="2.4s" repeatCount="indefinite"/>
    <animate attributeName="ry" values="18;14;18" dur="2.4s" repeatCount="indefinite"/>
  </ellipse>
  <text x="250" y="84" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#22c55e">Anchored</text>
  <text x="250" y="96" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#22c55e">distribution</text>
  <!-- Labels -->
  <text x="80" y="138" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#334155">High variance</text>
  <text x="250" y="138" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#334155">Low variance</text>
  <text x="160" y="20" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#334155">Few-shot narrows the output distribution</text>
</svg>
```

sections.heading: Distinguishing Patterns from Issues: Reducing False Positives
sections.body_markdown:
A production code-review or security-scanning pipeline has two failure modes: **false negatives** (missing real issues) and **false positives** (flagging acceptable patterns). Few-shot examples are the primary tool for calibrating this tradeoff — but only if you deliberately include examples of *both* genuine issues and acceptable patterns that superficially resemble them.

## The False-Positive Problem

Without negative examples (acceptable patterns), the model learns only the positive class. It will generalise aggressively — flagging any code that *looks like* the issue pattern, even when the context makes it safe. This erodes developer trust and causes alert fatigue, which is the primary reason security tooling gets disabled.

## Pattern vs. Issue: A Taxonomy

| Surface feature | Genuine issue | Acceptable pattern |
|:----------------|:-------------|:-------------------|
| `eval()` call | `eval(user_input)` | `eval("2 + 2")` (constant) |
| `pickle.loads()` | `pickle.loads(request.body)` | `pickle.loads(open('model.pkl','rb').read())` (trusted local file) |
| `subprocess.call()` | `subprocess.call(user_cmd, shell=True)` | `subprocess.call(['ls', '-la'])` (list form, no shell) |
| SQL string | `f"SELECT * WHERE id={x}"` | `"SELECT * WHERE id = %s"` (parameterised) |
| `assert` statement | `assert user.is_admin()` (security check) | `assert len(data) > 0` (test assertion) |

Each row in this table is a candidate pair of few-shot examples: one genuine issue, one acceptable pattern with the same surface feature.

## Worked Pair: `subprocess` Call

**Example — Genuine issue:**
```
Input code:
import subprocess
subprocess.call(user_input, shell=True)

Reasoning:
  shell=True with a user-controlled string enables arbitrary command execution.
  Alternative: maybe user_input is validated. Rejection: no validation visible;
    shell=True is categorically unsafe with external input.
Output:
{
  "location": "line 2",
  "issue": "Arbitrary command execution via shell=True with user-controlled input",
  "severity": "critical",
  "suggested_fix": "Use list form: subprocess.call(['cmd', arg]) with shell=False."
}
```

**Example — Acceptable pattern:**
```
Input code:
import subprocess
subprocess.call(['git', 'status', '--short'])

Reasoning:
  List form with hardcoded arguments; shell=False (default). No user input.
  This is the recommended safe pattern for subprocess calls.
Output:
{
  "location": "line 2",
  "issue": null,
  "severity": null,
  "suggested_fix": null
}
```

## Architectural Judgment: How Many Negative Examples?

The optimal ratio of positive (issue) to negative (acceptable pattern) examples depends on the base rate of issues in your document corpus:

$$\text{Optimal negative ratio} \approx 1 - p_{\text{issue}}$$

where $p_{\text{issue}}$ is the empirical proportion of flagged items in your corpus. If 20% of code snippets contain genuine issues, a 1:4 positive-to-negative ratio in your few-shot set approximates the true distribution and minimises expected calibration error.

In practice, **at least one negative example per positive example** is the minimum for production pipelines. Skipping negative examples is the single most common cause of high false-positive rates in LLM-based code review.

sections.heading: Production Architecture, Scenario, and Exam Questions
sections.body_markdown:
## Realistic Production Scenario

**Organisation:** A fintech company runs an automated compliance-review pipeline that scans pull requests for regulatory violations (PCI-DSS, SOX). The pipeline uses Claude to classify each code change as `compliant`, `review_required`, or `violation`.

**Initial state:** The prompt uses a 400-word instruction block describing each classification and its criteria. In production, the pipeline achieves:
- 91% recall on clear violations
- 34% false-positive rate on `review_required` (developers ignore most alerts)
- Inconsistent output format (sometimes JSON, sometimes prose)

**Intervention:** The team adds four few-shot examples:
1. A clear violation (hardcoded PAN in source) with reasoning.
2. An acceptable pattern (PAN masked before logging) with reasoning showing why it is not a violation.
3. An ambiguous case (PAN in a test fixture with synthetic data) classified as `review_required` with reasoning.
4. A format-only example showing the exact JSON schema with all fields populated.

**Results after intervention:**
- Recall on clear violations: 94% (+3 pp)
- False-positive rate on `review_required`: 11% (−23 pp)
- Format compliance: 99.6% (up from ~72%)
- Token cost per call: +38% (acceptable given alert-fatigue reduction)

**Architectural lesson:** The false-positive reduction (23 pp) came almost entirely from the single negative example (acceptable pattern). The format compliance improvement came from the format-only example. Recall improvement came from the reasoning traces. Each example type solved a distinct failure mode — confirming that few-shot design should be **failure-mode-driven**, not volume-driven.

---

## Exam-Style Questions

### Question 1

A team deploys a few-shot prompt with six examples for a document extraction task. They observe that extraction quality on standard documents is excellent, but the model still halluccinates values for fields that are absent in non-standard document structures. Which change is most likely to resolve this?

**A.** Add two more examples of standard documents with all fields present.

**B.** Add an example of a non-standard document structure where a required field is absent, showing `null` as the correct output.

**C.** Increase the instruction block length to explicitly state "return null if the field is absent."

**D.** Switch to a zero-shot chain-of-thought prompt to improve reasoning.

**Correct answer: B**

*Explanation:* The model's hallucination on non-standard structures is a distribution mismatch — the existing examples only cover standard structures, so the model applies its prior (fabricate a plausible value) when the structure is unfamiliar. Adding an example of the non-standard structure with an explicit `null` output recalibrates the model's behaviour for that structure type. Option C (instruction only) is less effective than a concrete example for this failure mode, as established in Section 1. Option A adds redundant examples that do not address the failure mode. Option D removes the few-shot signal entirely, which would worsen the problem.

---

### Question 2

A code-review pipeline has a 28% false-positive rate. The prompt contains three few-shot examples, all of which are genuine issues. What is the most architecturally sound first intervention?

**A.** Add three more genuine-issue examples to improve the model's pattern recognition.

**B.** Add a system prompt instruction: "Do not flag code that follows best practices."

**C.** Add at least one few-shot example of an acceptable pattern that superficially resembles a genuine issue, with reasoning explaining why it is not flagged.

**D.** Switch to a fine-tuned model trained on the organisation's codebase.

**Correct answer: C**

*Explanation:* A 28% false-positive rate with all-positive few-shot examples is the canonical symptom of a model that has learned only the positive class and is over-generalising. The minimum effective intervention is one negative example (acceptable pattern) with reasoning. This is lower cost, lower latency, and lower maintenance burden than fine-tuning (D), and more effective than instruction-only changes (B) for this specific failure mode. Adding more positive examples (A) would likely worsen the false-positive rate.

---

### Question 3

An architect is designing a few-shot prompt for a tool-routing agent that handles ambiguous user requests. The agent must choose between `search_code`, `search_docs`, and `run_diagnostics`. Which few-shot example design principle is most important for this use case?

**A.** Include examples that cover every possible user request phrasing.

**B.** Include examples that show the chosen tool and explicitly name the rejected alternatives with reasons.

**C.** Include examples only for the most common request type to minimise token cost.

**D.** Include examples that show the output format but omit reasoning to keep examples concise.

**Correct answer: B**

*Explanation:* For ambiguous routing decisions, the model must learn the *decision hierarchy* — which tool takes priority when multiple tools are plausible. Naming rejected alternatives with reasons is the mechanism by which the model internalises this hierarchy and applies it to novel phrasings it has never seen. Option A is infeasible (infinite phrasings exist) and unnecessary if the examples expose the underlying rule. Option C sacrifices coverage of the ambiguous cases, which are precisely where the agent will fail. Option D omits the reasoning that enables generalisation — the model learns the outcome but not the rule.

key_takeaways: Few-shot examples outperform instruction-only prompts for format consistency because they anchor the model's output distribution to a narrow, concrete target — 2–3 well-chosen examples typically reduce format error rates from ~18% to ~3%.
Ambiguous-case examples that explicitly name rejected alternatives and explain the rejection criteria are the highest-value few-shot investment: they teach decision hierarchies, not just outcomes.

Extraction hallucination is best reduced by including examples that demonstrate null returns for absent fields and uncertainty notes for vague language — without these, the model's prior favours fabricating plausible values.

False-positive rates in classification and review pipelines are primarily driven by the absence of negative examples (acceptable patterns); at least one negative example per positive example is the production minimum.

Few-shot examples enable genuine generalisation to novel patterns when they expose the underlying decision rule through diverse surface forms and boundary cases — not when they enumerate every possible input.

Architectural tradeoffs — token cost, example maintenance, and retrieval-augmented selection — must be evaluated per failure mode: format examples are cheap and high-ROI; reasoning-trace examples cost more but are essential for ambiguous routing and classification tasks.
further_reading: Chain-of-thought prompting and its interaction with few-shot examples: how reasoning traces in examples affect multi-step task performance
Retrieval-augmented few-shot selection: embedding-based nearest-neighbour retrieval for dynamic example pools in wide-distribution pipelines
Prompt calibration and expected calibration error: measuring and minimising the gap between model confidence and empirical accuracy in classification tasks
Constitutional AI and RLHF as complements to few-shot prompting: when in-context learning is insufficient and fine-tuning becomes the right architectural choice
